topics.title: Introduction to Electric Motors Used for Robotic Applications: DC Motor - Construction - Principle of Operation - Back EMF - Torque - Characteristics of Shunt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Motors Used for Robotic Applications: DC Motor - Construction - Principle of Operation - Back EMF - Torque - Characteristics of Shunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Motors in Robotic Applications — Formal Definition

In the context of **Robotics and Mechatronic Drives**, an **Electric Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy (supplied from a DC source, AC source, or electronic drive) into controlled mechanical energy (rotational motion) at the output shaft. The choice of motor dictates the dynamic performance, accuracy, payload capacity, and bandwidth of any robotic manipulator or automated system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A robotic electric motor is a precision actuator that provides **controllable torque, speed, and position** as commanded by a controller, enabling accurate motion of robot joints, wheels, and end-effectors.

### Why Electric Motors for Robots?

| Property | Relevance to Robotics |
| :--- | :--- |
| **High Torque-to-Inertia Ratio** | Enables rapid acceleration/deceleration of robot arms |
| **Precise Speed Control** | Required for trajectory tracking in CNC & pick-place robots |
| **Self-Cooling (in many designs)** | Compact packaging inside joint housings |
| **Easy Interface with Microcontrollers/PLCs** | Direct PWM/DAC compatibility for closed-loop control |
| **Bidirectional Operation** | Forward and reverse motion for multi-axis manipulators |

### Classification of Motors for Robotics

Electric motors used in robotic applications are broadly classified into:

1. **DC Motors** (Shunt, Series, Compound, Permanent Magnet, Brushless DC)
2. **AC Motors** (Induction, Synchronous, Servo)
3. **Special Motors** (Stepper, Reluctance, Servo)

For this module, the focus is on the **DC Motor**, the most fundamental building block of electrical drives.

> [!NOTE]
> Although modern industrial robots use Brushless DC (BLDC) or Servo motors, studying the **brushed DC motor** is essential because the *back-EMF* and *armature reaction* concepts form the theoretical foundation for **all** DC drive analysis.

---

## 1.2 DC Motor — Intuitive Overview (Conceptual Analogy)

### Real-World Analogy: The Bicycle Dynamo

Imagine you are riding a bicycle. The **DC Motor** works exactly like a **reversible dynamo** (the small generator that lights your bike's headlamp).

- When you **pedal faster**, the dynamo produces more voltage (this is analogous to **back-EMF** rising with speed).
- When the **headlamp is ON**, current flows and it becomes harder to pedal (this is analogous to **load torque** resisting rotation).
- The dynamo "fights" your pedaling by generating a counter-voltage — that is the **back-EMF**.

In a DC motor, the roles are reversed: **electrical input** causes rotation, and that rotation itself generates a counter-voltage (**back-EMF**) that opposes the applied voltage. This continuous tug-of-war is precisely what **governs the speed and torque** characteristics of the motor.

### Core Operational Insight

$$V_{applied} = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Where $E_b$ is the **back-EMF**, and the difference between applied voltage and $E_b$ drives current $I_a$ through the armature resistance $R_a$. As the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises, current drops, and the motor settles at an equilibrium speed — this is the **self-regulating nature** of a DC motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in DC Motor Analysis:**
> - $P$ = Number of Poles of the motor
> - $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
> - $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding
> - $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Weber, Wb)
> - $N$ = Speed of the armature in RPM
> - $\omega$ = Angular speed in rad/s
> - $T$ = Torque developed in N·m

---

## 1.3 The Shunt DC Motor — Syllabus Highlight

A **Shunt DC Motor** is a DC motor where the **field winding is connected in parallel (shunt)** with the armature winding. Both windings receive the full supply voltage independently.

> [!NOTE]
> The Shunt DC motor is **the most commonly analyzed motor** in KTU university examinations because its **near-constant-speed characteristic** is ideal for driving robotic joints that require stable operation under varying load.

### Block Diagram — Motor in a Robotic Drive System

```
[Controller (PID/MCU)]
        |
        v
[Power Converter (Chopper / PWM Amplifier)]
        |
        v
[DC Motor] --[Encoder/Tachogenerator]--> [Robot Joint / Wheel]
        |____________________________________ feedback
```

This is the standard **closed-loop electrical drive** architecture. The DC motor's predictable torque-speed curve makes it the **plant** in the control loop, while the encoder provides the **feedback signal**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a DC Motor

A DC motor consists of two major sub-assemblies:

### (A) Stator (Stationary Part)

The stator provides the magnetic field and houses the field winding.

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Yoke (Frame)** | Outer cast-iron/steel body; provides mechanical support and completes the magnetic flux path |
| **Pole Core (Pole Shoe)** | Laminated silicon steel; supports the field winding and spreads flux uniformly across the air gap |
| **Field Winding (Shunt)** | Enameled copper wire wound on pole cores; produces the main working flux $\Phi$ |
| **Interpoles (Commutating Poles)** | Small auxiliary poles placed between main poles; reduce sparking at the brushes |

### (B) Rotor (Rotating Part) — The Armature

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Armature Core** | Laminated silicon-steel cylindrical drum; carries the armature winding and reduces eddy-current losses |
| **Armature Winding** | Closed loop of insulated copper conductors housed in armature slots; the "engine" of the motor |
| **Commutator** | Cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments separated by mica; converts external DC to internal AC in the armature |
| **Brushes & Brush Holders** | Carbon/graphite blocks that slide on the commutator; provide the electrical interface between the stationary external circuit and the rotating armature |
| **Shaft & Bearings** | Mechanical shaft that transmits torque; supported by ball/roller bearings seated in the end-shields |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the construction of a DC motor", students often **forget to mention the commutator and brushes** — these are *essential* components unique to DC machines and are frequent 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

### (C) Winding Configuration of a Shunt Motor

```
   +V (Supply Terminal)
     |
     |---[Shunt Field Winding (Rf)]---+--- Neutral (N-)
     |                                |
     +-----------[Armature (Ra)]------+
```

- The **shunt field winding** has a **large number of turns of thin wire** → high resistance → low current.
- The **armature winding** has **low resistance** → carries the bulk of the supply current.

---

## 2.2 Principle of Operation

The operation of a DC motor is based on **two fundamental electromagnetic laws**:

### Law 1: Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor (Lorentz Force)

When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force.

$$\vec{F} = I (\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

Where:
- $F$ = Force in Newtons (N)
- $I$ = Current in Amperes (A)
- $L$ = Length of conductor in the field (m)
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density (Tesla)

### Law 2: Fleming's Left-Hand Rule

To determine the direction of force (and hence rotation), use the left-hand rule:
- **Forefinger** → Direction of magnetic field ($B$)
- **Middle finger** → Direction of current ($I$)
- **Thumb** → Direction of force/motion ($F$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** When DC supply is given to the armature, current flows through the conductors placed in the magnetic field of the stator poles. Each conductor experiences a tangential force $F$. The collective force on all conductors produces a **unidirectional torque** on the armature, causing it to rotate.

The role of the **commutator and brushes** is to **reverse the current direction in each armature conductor** at the precise moment it passes from one pole's influence to the next — this ensures that the torque is **always unidirectional** (continuous rotation).

---

## 2.3 Back EMF (Counter EMF) — The Self-Regulating Mechanism

When the armature rotates in the magnetic field, the armature conductors **cut the magnetic flux**. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF **opposes the cause that produced it** — i.e., it opposes the applied voltage that drove the current which caused the rotation. Hence it is called **Back-EMF** ($E_b$).

### Mathematical Expression for Back-EMF

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of the armature (RPM)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths
- $A = P$ for a **wave-wound** armature; $A = 2$ for a **lap-wound** armature

### Significance of Back-EMF

1. It is **directly proportional to speed** ($E_b \propto N$) at constant flux.
2. It **limits the armature current** to a safe value: $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$.
3. At starting, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$, and $I_a$ is maximum — this is why a **starter** is mandatory for DC motors.

---

## 2.4 Torque Developed in a DC Motor

Torque is the **rotational equivalent of force**, representing the motor's ability to do work.

### Torque Equation

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

Where $T_a$ is the **armature torque** in N·m.

### Shaft Torque (Useful Torque)

The **shaft torque** $T_{sh}$ is what is delivered to the load after mechanical and iron losses are subtracted:

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{losses}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ratio between shaft torque and armature torque is the **mechanical efficiency** of the motor. For a robot joint, $T_{sh}$ is the **payload-handling capacity**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Back-EMF | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ | Volts (V) | $A = P$ (wave) or $A = 2$ (lap) |
| 2 | Armature Torque | $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | N·m | $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ |
| 3 | Voltage Equation | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | Fundamental KVL of armature |
| 4 | Power Developed | $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical power conversion |
| 5 | Angular Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | Conversion from RPM |
| 6 | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | A | Since field is across supply |
| 7 | Line Current | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | A | KCL at terminal node |
| 8 | No-Load Speed | $N_0 = \dfrac{V \cdot 60 A}{P \Phi Z}$ (approx) | RPM | When $I_a \to 0$, $E_b \to V$ |
| 9 | Starting Current (no starter) | $I_{st} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | A | $E_b = 0$ at $N = 0$ |
| 10 | Speed (general) | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{P \Phi Z / 60 A}$ | RPM | Master speed equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students use $A = 2$ for **all** DC machines. Remember: **Lap winding** → $A = P$, **Wave winding** → $A = 2$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark trap in numerical problems.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Back-EMF** is the principle behind **regenerative braking** in electric vehicles and robotic wheels — when supply is cut, the motor acts as a generator and feeds energy back.
- **Torque** determines the **payload specification** of a robot arm (e.g., a 6-DOF manipulator rated at 5 N·m per joint can lift a 2 kg payload at 0.25 m reach).
- The **shunt motor's flat speed-torque curve** is exploited in **lathes, drills, and conveyor robots** where constant speed under variable load is critical.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF Equation ($E_b = P\Phi ZN / 60A$)

**Starting Assumptions:**
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths
- $N$ = armature speed (RPM)

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution

The flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** is:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per rev} = P \cdot \Phi$$

Reason: In one complete revolution, the conductor passes under $P$ pairs of N-S poles. The flux linked per pole is $\Phi$, so total flux cut equals $P \cdot \Phi$ Webers.

### Step 2 — Flux Cut Per Second (Average Rate of Change of Flux)

The armature completes $N$ revolutions in 60 seconds, so the **number of revolutions per second** is:

$$\text{Revs/sec} = \frac{N}{60}$$

The flux cut by **one conductor per second** is:

$$\text{Flux per sec per conductor} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

### Step 3 — EMF Induced in One Conductor

By **Faraday's Law**, the average EMF induced in one conductor is:

$$e_{\text{one}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \;\; \text{Volts}$$

### Step 4 — EMF in the Full Armature Winding

The $Z$ conductors are divided equally among $A$ parallel paths. Each path has $Z/A$ conductors **in series**. Since all parallel paths generate the same EMF, the net armature EMF is the EMF of one path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot e_{\text{one}} \\
&= \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{P \Phi N}{60}\right) \\
&= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard back-EMF equation** used in every KTU numerical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in Part B. You *must* state the underlying assumption: "average flux linkage is used" and "Faraday's Law applied to the average rate of change of flux."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Equation ($T_a = P\Phi Z I_a / 2\pi A$)

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Force on a conductor of length $L$ in flux density $B$ carrying current $I$: $F = BIL$
- Total conductors = $Z$, distributed among $A$ parallel paths.
- Torque = Force $\times$ Radius ($T = F \cdot r$)

### Step 1 — Force on One Conductor

The flux density under one pole (assuming uniform air-gap distribution) is:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_g} = \frac{\Phi}{\pi D L / P}$$

where $A_g$ is the air-gap area under one pole, $D$ is the armature diameter, and $L$ is the core length.

The force on a single conductor is:

$$F_{\text{one}} = B \cdot I_{\text{one}} \cdot L$$

where $I_{\text{one}} = I_c$ is the current per conductor.

### Step 2 — Torque per Conductor

$$T_{\text{one}} = F_{\text{one}} \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Total Conductor Count per Path

In $A$ parallel paths, each path has $Z/A$ conductors. The current per conductor is:

$$I_{\text{one}} = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

### Step 4 — Torque on All Conductors

Summing over all $Z$ conductors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= Z \cdot T_{\text{one}} = Z \cdot F_{\text{one}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} \\
&= Z \cdot B \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2} \\
&= \frac{B \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot Z \cdot D}{2A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substituting $B$ in Terms of $\Phi$

Using $B = \dfrac{P \Phi}{\pi D L}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \frac{1}{2A} \cdot \left(\frac{P \Phi}{\pi D L}\right) \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot Z \cdot D \\
&= \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **armature torque equation** required by the KTU syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the elegant relationship:** Multiplying $T_a$ by $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ yields mechanical power developed: $P_{m} = T_a \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$. This confirms **power balance** in the motor.

---

## 3.3 Speed Equation and Its Use

Starting from the voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Substituting $E_b = P\Phi Z N / 60A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} + I_a R_a \\
\frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} &= V - I_a R_a \\
N &= \frac{(V - I_a R_a) \cdot 60 \, A}{P \Phi Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master speed equation** of a DC motor. It reveals that:
- Speed $\uparrow$ if supply voltage $V \uparrow$ → **Ward-Leonard / Chopper control**
- Speed $\uparrow$ if field flux $\Phi \downarrow$ → **Field-weakening control**

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V shunt motor has 800 armature conductors wave-wound. The armature resistance is $0.2\ \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $250\ \Omega$. The flux per pole is $0.03\ \text{Wb}$. If the motor takes a total line current of 51 A, find:
1. Back-EMF $E_b$
2. Armature current $I_a$
3. Speed $N$ (in RPM)
4. Gross torque developed $T_a$

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, $Z = 800$, wave → $A = 2$, $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb, $I_L = 51$ A.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Field current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Armature current**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 51 - 1 = 50\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Back-EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50 \times 0.2) = 250 - 10 = 240\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Speed**

$$
N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \, A}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.03 \times 800}
$$

$$
N = \frac{28800}{96} = 300\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Torque developed**

$$
T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 50}{2 \pi \times 2}
$$

$$
T_a = \frac{4800}{4 \pi} = \frac{1200}{\pi} \approx 381.97\ \text{N·m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Check:** $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 50 = 12000\ \text{W} = 12\ \text{kW}$. Verify: $P_{dev} = T_a \cdot \omega = 381.97 \times (2\pi \times 300/60) = 381.97 \times 31.416 \approx 12000\ \text{W}$ ✓ (Sanity check passed.)

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructional Diagram — Cross-Sectional View of a 4-Pole DC Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Yoke / Frame] --> B1[Pole Core]
    A1 --> B2[Pole Core]
    B1 --> C1[Shunt Field Winding]
    B2 --> C2[Shunt Field Winding]
    A1 --> D1[End Shield Left]
    A1 --> D2[End Shield Right]
    D1 --> E1[Bearing]
    D2 --> E2[Bearing]
    E1 --> F1[Armature Shaft]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Armature Core with Slots]
    G1 --> H1[Armature Winding]
    H1 --> I1[Commutator Segments]
    I1 --> J1[Brushes and Brush Holder]
    J1 --> K1[DC Supply Terminals]
    G1 --> L1[Air Gap between Pole and Armature]
    B1 --> L1
    B2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **functional block architecture** representation of a 4-pole DC motor. In a physical cross-section, you would see the yoke as the outer ring, four pole cores spaced at 90°, the cylindrical armature in the center, and the commutator on the shaft with brushes pressing on it from outside.

---

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic — Shunt DC Motor with Supply

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DC_Supply [DC Voltage Source V]
        V1[Positive Terminal]
        V2[Negative Terminal]
    end
    V1 --> W1
    V2 --> W4
    W1[Wire from V+] --> S1
    W4[Wire from V-] --> S2
    subgraph Shunt_Path [Shunt Field Branch]
        S1 --> Rsh1((Rsh Field Winding))
        Rsh1 --> S2
    end
    subgraph Armature_Path [Armature Branch]
        W1 --> Ra1((Ra Armature Resistance))
        Ra1 --> Eb1((Eb Back-EMF Source))
        Eb1 --> S2
    end
    Eb1 -.-> Sensor[Speed / Position Encoder]
    Sensor -.-> Ctrl[Robot Controller]
    Ctrl -.-> W1
```

**Key Labels in the Schematic:**
- `Rsh` → Shunt field winding (high resistance, in parallel with armature)
- `Ra` → Armature winding (low resistance)
- `Eb` → Back-EMF source (opposes V)
- `Sensor` → Tachogenerator / Encoder for closed-loop control

---

## 4.3 Power Flow Diagram of a DC Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Electrical Input Power Pin = V * IL] --> B[Armature Copper Loss: Ia^2 * Ra]
    A --> C[Field Copper Loss: Ish^2 * Rsh]
    B --> D[Mechanical Power Developed: Pm = Eb * Ia]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Iron / Core Loss: Pi]
    D --> F[Friction and Windage Loss: Pfw]
    E --> G[Shaft Output Power: Psh = Tsh * omega]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Robotic Joint / Load]
```

**Reading the Power Flow:**
- **Input** → subtract $I_a^2 R_a$ and $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ → **Mechanical Power Developed** $P_m = E_b I_a$
- Subtract iron + friction losses → **Shaft Power** $P_{sh} = T_{sh} \omega$
- This shaft power drives the **robotic joint or load**.

---

## 4.4 Torque-Speed Characteristic — Shunt Motor (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operating Point Analysis] --> B[No Load: N0 approx V*60A / P Phi Z]
    A --> C[Full Load: Nfl slightly less than N0]
    A --> D[Stall: N = 0, I = Ist, T = Tmax]
    B --> E[Slope due to Ra drop: Drop = Ia*Ra]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Characteristic: Nearly Constant Speed, Slight Drop with Load]
    F --> G[Application: Lathes, Fans, Line Shafts, Conveyor Robots]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The shunt motor's defining feature** is its **near-constant speed** characteristic — speed drops only slightly (typically 5-10%) from no-load to full-load. This makes it the **preferred choice for robotic manipulators requiring stable joint velocity**.

---

## 4.5 Control Block — DC Motor in a Robotic Joint

```mermaid
graph TD
    Cmd[Desired Joint Position theta_d] --> Diff
    Actual[Encoder: theta_actual] --> Diff
    Diff[Error e = theta_d - theta_actual] --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> PWM[PWM / Chopper Power Converter]
    PWM --> Motor[DC Shunt Motor]
    Motor --> Joint[Robot Joint Output]
    Joint --> Actual
    Motor -.-> T[Load Torque Disturbance]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the classical **position-control loop** for a robot joint driven by a shunt DC motor. The PID controller adjusts the PWM duty cycle, which changes the effective voltage to the motor, which in turn adjusts torque and speed to track the commanded joint position.
---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]** — 3 Marks
**Define Back-EMF in a DC motor. Explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Back-EMF* ($E_b$) is the electromotive force induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor due to their rotation in the magnetic field. By Lenz's law, it opposes the applied voltage.

**Significance (must write 2 of the following 3 points):**
1. It **regulates armature current**: $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$. As speed increases, $E_b$ rises, reducing $I_a$ and hence torque — establishing **steady-state operation**.
2. It is the **key mechanism that makes a DC motor self-regulating**; without back-EMF, the motor would draw infinite current at start.
3. It forms the basis for **regenerative braking** in electric vehicles and robotic wheels.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set B]** — 3 Marks
**Why does a DC motor require a starter? Justify with the back-EMF equation.**

**Model Answer:**

At the instant of starting, $N = 0$ RPM. From the back-EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = 0$$

Applying the armature voltage equation:

$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V - 0}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

Since $R_a$ is very small (typically 0.1 - 1 $\Omega$), the starting current becomes **10 to 20 times** the rated current. This would **burn the armature winding**. A **starter** (variable external resistance) limits $I_a$ to a safe value (1.5 - 2 $\times$ rated) during start-up and is gradually cut out as $E_b$ builds up with speed.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction of a DC motor**. Label all major parts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **back-EMF equation** for a DC motor starting from Faraday's Law. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction consists of three main parts:**

**(i) Yoke (Frame):** Outer cylindrical cast-iron or steel body. Functions:
- Provides mechanical support for pole cores and end-shields.
- Completes the magnetic flux path between poles.

**(ii) Pole Core and Pole Shoe:** Laminated silicon-steel pieces bolted to the inner surface of the yoke.
- **Pole core** carries the **field winding**.
- **Pole shoe** spreads flux uniformly across the air gap and supports the coil.

**(iii) Field Winding:** Enameled copper wire wound around each pole core, excited by DC supply. For a **shunt motor**, it has many turns of thin wire (high resistance, ~50-500 $\Omega$).

**(iv) Armature Core:** Laminated cylindrical drum mounted on the shaft. Slots are cut on its outer periphery to house the **armature winding**. Lamination reduces **eddy current loss**.

**(v) Armature Winding:** Insulated copper conductors placed in armature slots and connected in a closed loop. The EMF is induced in these conductors.

**(vi) Commutator:** Cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated from each other by mica strips. It acts as a **mechanical rectifier**, converting external DC to internal AC.

**(vii) Brushes and Brush Holders:** Carbon or graphite blocks that ride on the commutator surface. They provide the sliding electrical contact between the rotating armature and the stationary external circuit.

**(viii) Shaft, Bearings, End-Shields:** The shaft transmits mechanical power; ball bearings seated in the end-shields support the rotating assembly.

> **[Sketch to be drawn in exam]:** Cross-section showing yoke, 4 poles with field windings, cylindrical armature with slots, commutator on shaft, and 4 brushes pressing on commutator.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**

**Step 1:** Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $P \cdot \Phi$ Webers.

**Step 2:** Revolutions per second = $N/60$.

**Step 3:** Flux cut per second per conductor = $P \cdot \Phi \cdot (N/60)$ Wb/s.

**Step 4:** EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law):
$$e_{\text{one}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}\ \text{Volts}$$

**Step 5:** With $Z$ conductors and $A$ parallel paths, conductors per path = $Z/A$, and EMF per path (which is the net armature EMF) is:

$$
E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot e_{\text{one}} = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}
$$

**Step 6 (Conclusion):** $E_b$ is **directly proportional to speed $N$** and **flux per pole $\Phi$**. This relationship is central to DC motor speed control.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 1 Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the **torque equation** of a DC motor. State the relationship between mechanical power, torque, and back-EMF. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 250 V, wave-wound shunt motor has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are $0.5\ \Omega$ and $250\ \Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole is $0.04\ \text{Wb}$ and the motor takes an armature current of 30 A. Find:
- (i) Back-EMF
- (ii) Speed in RPM
- (iii) Torque developed
- (iv) Shaft torque, given rotational losses = 400 W. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Starting from the Lorentz force law:**

**Step 1:** Force on a single conductor: $F = B \cdot I_c \cdot L$ where $I_c = I_a/A$ is conductor current.

**Step 2:** Torque per conductor: $T_{\text{one}} = F \cdot (D/2) = B I_c L (D/2)$.

**Step 3:** Summing over all $Z$ conductors:

$$T_a = Z \cdot B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Using $B = P\Phi / (\pi D L)$:

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

**Step 5 (Power relationship):**

$$P_{\text{mech}} = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a$$

This confirms that **mechanical power developed = back-EMF × armature current** — the energy conversion in the motor.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V = 250\ \text{V}$, $Z = 600$, wave-wound → $A = 2$, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$, $\Phi = 0.04\ \text{Wb}$, $I_a = 30\ \text{A}$.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (30 \times 0.5) = 250 - 15 = 235\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Speed:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \, A}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{235 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.04 \times 600} = \frac{28200}{96} \approx 293.75\ \text{RPM}$$

**(iii) Torque developed:**

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.04 \times 600 \times 30}{2 \pi \times 2} = \frac{2880}{4 \pi} = \frac{720}{\pi} \approx 229.18\ \text{N·m}$$

**(iv) Shaft torque:**

Angular speed: $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi (293.75)/60 \approx 30.76\ \text{rad/s}$

Rotational loss torque:

$$T_{\text{loss}} = \frac{P_{\text{loss}}}{\omega} = \frac{400}{30.76} \approx 13.0\ \text{N·m}$$

Shaft torque:

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{\text{loss}} = 229.18 - 13.0 \approx 216.18\ \text{N·m}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong winding constant:** Forgetting whether $A = 2$ (wave) or $A = P$ (lap). Marks deducted: **1-2 marks**.
> 2. **Conflating shaft torque with armature torque:** Numerical problems often ask for both — ignoring rotational losses loses **1-2 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the line current relation:** $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$. In shunt motor problems, students compute $I_a$ wrongly by equating it to $I_L$.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** Examiner strictly deducts **0.5 marks** if RPM, N·m, or V are not specified.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in construction questions — even a labeled **block diagram** is mandatory. Marks deducted: **2-3 marks**.
> 6. **Not stating the underlying laws** in derivations: "By Faraday's Law" or "By Lorentz Force Law" must preface every derivation step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **DC Motor** converts electrical energy to mechanical energy using the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors (Lorentz force).
- [x] **Construction** has two parts: **Stator** (yoke, poles, field winding) and **Rotor** (armature core, winding, commutator, shaft). **Brushes** are the static interface.
- [x] **Principle of Operation** is based on **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** — force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
- [x] **Back-EMF** $E_b = P\Phi ZN / 60A$, opposes applied voltage, and is the **self-regulating mechanism** of the motor.
- [x] **Armature Torque** $T_a = P\Phi Z I_a / 2\pi A$, proportional to flux and armature current.
- [x] **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — the fundamental KVL of the DC motor armature.
- [x] **Shunt Motor:** Field winding in **parallel** with armature; $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$; near-constant speed characteristic.
- [x] **Winding Constants:** Wave → $A = 2$, Lap → $A = P$. Remember this in every numerical.
- [x] **Starting:** $E_b = 0$ at start → $I_{st} = V/R_a$ is dangerously high → **starter** is mandatory.
- [x] **Speed Control Methods:** (1) Armature voltage control (Ward-Leonard/Chopper), (2) Field flux control (field weakening).
- [x] **Mechanical Power:** $P_{dev} = E_b I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$ — verify this in every numerical as a sanity check.
- [x] **Robotic Application:** Shunt DC motors are suited for **constant-speed joint drives**, conveyor systems, and pump actuators; modern robots prefer **BLDC** or **servo motors** but the analysis principles remain the same.
- [x] **Closed-loop control:** Encoder/feedback + PID controller + PWM converter = standard robot motor drive architecture.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 DC Series Motor — Formal Definition

A **DC Series Motor** is a direct-current commutator machine in which the **field winding (excitation winding) is connected in series with the armature winding**. The entire armature current flows through the field winding, meaning field flux is directly proportional to the armature current (before magnetic saturation).

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A DC series motor is a self-excited DC machine where the field winding carries the same current as the armature, producing a flux that is a function of load current. The series motor is characterized by very high starting torque, variable speed characteristics, and is used in traction, cranes, and hoists.

**Key Identity (must be remembered):**
$$I_a = I_f = I_L$$

Where $I_a$ = armature current, $I_f$ = field current, $I_L$ = line current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The defining property of a series motor is that the field winding is in **series with the armature**, NOT across the supply. Many students mistakenly draw the field in parallel.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a heavy cart. When the cart is heavy (high load), you push harder, and your effort (flux) automatically increases. When the cart is empty (low load), you barely push, so your effort reduces. A **series motor behaves exactly like this**: more load → more current → more flux → more torque. It is a **"natural torque booster"** motor.

This is why series motors are used in **trains, cranes, and electric vehicles** where the motor must "fight" the load from a standstill.

---

## 1.2 DC Compound Motor — Formal Definition

A **DC Compound Motor** is a self-excited DC machine that carries **two field windings**: a **shunt field winding** (connected in parallel with the armature) and a **series field winding** (connected in series with the armature). The two fields may either **aid** or **oppose** each other.

**Two Sub-Types:**

| Type | Behavior | Field Polarity |
|------|----------|----------------|
| **Cumulative Compound** | Series field **aids** the shunt field | Same direction |
| **Differential Compound** | Series field **opposes** the shunt field | Opposite direction |

**Total Flux Equation:**
$$\phi_{total} = \phi_{shunt} \pm \phi_{series}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cumulative compound motors** are widely used in industry (presses, elevators, conveyors) because they combine the **high starting torque of a series motor** with the **good speed regulation of a shunt motor**. Differential compound motors have very limited application due to poor stability.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of compound motor as a **"hybrid athlete"**:
- The **shunt field** is like a steady, calm jogger who maintains a constant pace (good speed regulation).
- The **series field** is like a sprinter who bursts energy when needed (high starting torque).
- Together, they make the motor both **stable and powerful**.

---

## 1.3 Necessity of Starters for DC Motors

A **DC motor starter** is an electrical device used to **limit the starting current** drawn by the armature during the initial moments of starting when the motor is at rest.

**Why Starters Are Necessary — The Core Physics:**

When a DC motor is at standstill, the back EMF ($E_b$) is **zero** because:
$$E_b = \frac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$$

At start, $N = 0$, hence $E_b = 0$. Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

At starting instant:
$$I_{a(start)} = \frac{V - 0}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

Since $R_a$ is very small (fraction of an ohm), $I_{a(start)}$ becomes **10 to 20 times the rated current**, which can:
1. **Damage the armature winding** due to excessive heat.
2. **Severely spark the commutator** leading to commutation failure.
3. **Cause a huge voltage dip** in the supply, affecting other connected loads.
4. **Produce a destructive mechanical shock** to the driven load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard Answer (Memorize this):** Starters are required for DC motors to limit the high starting armature current by inserting external resistance in series with the armature at start, which is gradually cut out as the motor accelerates and develops back EMF.

---

## 1.4 DC Servo Motor — Formal Definition

A **DC Servo Motor** is a specially designed DC motor (usually with a separate field winding) used in **closed-loop position or speed control systems**. It is built with **low rotor inertia, high starting torque, and linear torque-speed characteristics** to ensure fast dynamic response.

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A DC servo motor is a high-performance DC motor optimized for servo applications where rapid acceleration, deceleration, and precise position control are required. It operates in the **linear region** of its magnetization curve and is driven by either armature-control or field-control configuration.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

A normal DC motor is like a **bulldozer** — it has high torque but is slow and clumsy to control.

A **DC servo motor** is like a **surgical robot arm** — it has fast, precise, and predictable movements. It is designed not just to rotate, but to **rotate exactly to the position you command it to** and **hold that position** even against disturbances.

> [!NOTE]
> Servo motors are the **"muscles of automation"** — found in CNC machines, robotic arms, printers, cameras (autofocus), and aerospace control surfaces.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Rated starting current without starter:** $\approx \mathbf{10 \text{ to } 20 \times I_{full\ load}}$
- **Safe starting current with starter:** $\approx \mathbf{1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times I_{full\ load}}$
- **Typical armature resistance:** $\approx \mathbf{0.1 \text{ to } 1\ \Omega}$
- **DC Servo motor field supply:** usually **constant** (in armature control)
- **Armature control voltage range:** $\pm 10\text{V}$ to $\pm 200\text{V}$ (application dependent)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Analysis of DC Series Motor

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:

- **Step 1:** Supply voltage $V$ is applied to the series combination of armature and field winding.
- **Step 2:** Current $I_a$ flows through both — the field produces a flux $\phi$ proportional to $I_a$ (linear region).
- **Step 3:** The interaction between armature current and flux produces a torque:
$$T \propto \phi \cdot I_a \propto I_a \cdot I_a \propto I_a^2$$
- **Step 4:** The rotating armature develops a back EMF $E_b \propto \phi N$.
- **Step 5:** As speed builds, $E_b$ rises, current drops, and motor settles at an operating point.

### Why and How the Series Motor Develops High Starting Torque:

Since $T \propto I_a^2$, even a moderate starting current (e.g., $1.5 \times I_{rated}$) produces a torque that is $1.5^2 = 2.25$ times the rated torque. This is the **fundamental reason** series motors dominate traction and heavy-load applications.

---

## 2.2 Operational Analysis of DC Compound Motor

### Cumulative Compound — Logic Flow:

- **Step 1:** Shunt field produces a **constant base flux** $\phi_{sh}$ independent of load.
- **Step 2:** Series field produces a flux $\phi_{se} \propto I_a$, **adding** to the shunt flux.
- **Step 3:** Total flux = $\phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$ — this **increases with load**, so speed drops gracefully with load.
- **Step 4:** Starting torque is enhanced by the series component, giving intermediate performance between series and shunt.

### Differential Compound — Logic Flow:

- **Step 1:** Series flux **opposes** shunt flux.
- **Step 2:** As load increases, net flux **decreases**, so speed tends to rise.
- **Step 3:** This creates an **unstable feedback loop** — if speed rises, $E_b$ rises, current drops, flux drops further, speed rises more → **motor races away**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Differential compound motors are rarely used** as motors (sometimes as generators) because they are inherently unstable. KTU examiners often ask: "Why is the differential compound motor not used?" — answer: due to rising speed characteristic leading to instability.

---

## 2.3 Necessity of Starters — Analytical Justification

### Quantifying the Starting Problem:

For a typical 5 HP, 220 V DC shunt motor with $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$:

$$I_{a(start)\ without\ starter} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.5} = 440\ A$$

If rated current = 22 A, this is **20 times the rated current** — clearly unacceptable.

**With a starter adding $R_{ext} = 10\ \Omega$:**

$$I_{a(start)} = \frac{220}{0.5 + 10} = \frac{220}{10.5} \approx 20.95\ A \approx I_{rated}$$

This is exactly the role of a starter: **insert resistance to keep starting current within safe limits, then cut it out as back EMF builds**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems asked in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$ | Back EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation (motor) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $I_{a(start)} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Starting current without starter | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I_{a(start)} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$ | Starting current with external resistance | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque proportional to flux × armature current | N·m |
| 6 | $T_a \propto I_a^2$ | Series motor torque equation (linear region) | N·m |
| 7 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$ | Speed equation (general DC motor) | RPM |
| 8 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ | Series motor speed (linear region) | RPM |
| 9 | $N \propto \dfrac{V}{\phi}$ | Shunt motor speed (approximately) | RPM |
| 10 | $R_{ext} = \dfrac{V}{I_{safe}} - R_a$ | External starter resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 11 | $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} \pm \phi_{se}$ | Compound motor total flux | Weber (Wb) |
| 12 | $T_{starting} = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_{a(start)}$ | Servo motor starting torque | N·m |
| 13 | $\dfrac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$ | DC Servo transfer function (armature control) | rad/s per V |
| 14 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{K_t K_b}$ | Mechanical time constant of servo | seconds (s) |
| 15 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time constant of servo | seconds (s) |

> **Notation Key:** $P$ = poles, $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (RPM), $Z$ = total conductors, $A$ = parallel paths, $R_a$ = armature resistance ($\Omega$), $K_t$ = torque constant, $K_b$ = back EMF constant, $J$ = moment of inertia, $B$ = friction coefficient.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Series Motors:** Used in **electric trains (locomotives), trams, trolleybuses, cranes, winches, and forklift trucks** where high starting torque and overload tolerance are critical.
- **Compound Motors (Cumulative):** Used in **rolling mills, elevators, printing presses, conveyors, and punch presses** where moderate starting torque and good speed regulation under varying load are required.
- **Differential Compound:** Almost obsolete as a motor; occasionally used as a **DC generator** in older welding sets.
- **DC Servo Motors:** Found in **robotic joints, CNC axis drives, camera autofocus systems, antenna positioning systems, and aerospace flight control surfaces**.
- **DC Starters (3-point/4-point):** Mandatory in every industrial DC motor drive panel; modern variants use **thyristor-based soft starters** which are the electronic successor of the traditional resistance starter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Starting Current is High (DC Motor Armature)

**Given:** A DC shunt motor rated $V = 220\ V$, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $I_{a,rated} = 22\ A$.

**Find:** Starting armature current without any starter.

**Step 1 — Write the armature circuit KVL equation:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the starting condition (motor at rest, $N = 0$):**

The back EMF is given by:
$$E_b = \frac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$$

At the instant of starting, the rotor has not yet moved, so $N = 0$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $N = 0$ into the back EMF equation:**
$$E_b = \frac{P \phi \cdot 0 \cdot Z}{60 A} = 0\ V$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $E_b = 0$ into the KVL equation:**
$$V = 0 + I_a R_a$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the armature current $I_a$:**
$$I_a = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values:**
$$I_a = \frac{220}{0.5} = 440\ A$$

**Step 7 — Compute the multiple of rated current:**
$$\text{Multiple} = \frac{I_{a,start}}{I_{a,rated}} = \frac{440}{22} = 20$$

**Conclusion:** The starting current is **20 times** the rated current, which is dangerous. Hence, an external starting resistance must be inserted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculation of Starter Resistance

**Given:** $V = 220\ V$, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, desired safe starting current $I_{safe} = 1.5 \times I_{rated} = 1.5 \times 22 = 33\ A$.

**Find:** External resistance $R_{ext}$ to be inserted at start.

**Step 1 — Write the modified KVL with external resistance:**
$$V = I_{safe} (R_a + R_{ext})$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $R_{ext}$:**
$$R_a + R_{ext} = \frac{V}{I_{safe}}$$

$$R_{ext} = \frac{V}{I_{safe}} - R_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$R_{ext} = \frac{220}{33} - 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**
$$R_{ext} = 6.667 - 0.5 = 6.167\ \Omega$$

**Conclusion:** An external resistance of **6.167 $\Omega$** must be inserted in series with the armature at start, and then cut out in steps as the motor accelerates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Equation of DC Series Motor (Linear Region)

**Assumption:** Magnetic circuit unsaturated, so $\phi \propto I_a$, i.e., $\phi = k I_a$ for some constant $k$.

**Step 1 — Start from the general speed equation:**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$$

where $k' = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A}$ is the machine construction constant.

**Step 2 — Substitute $\phi = k I_a$:**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \cdot k I_a}$$

**Step 3 — For the linear region, if $I_a R_a$ is small compared to $V$ (approximation for moderate loads):**
$$N \approx \frac{V}{k' k I_a}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $V$ and $k' k$ are constants, so:**
$$N \approx \frac{\text{constant}}{I_a}$$

$$\boxed{N \propto \frac{1}{I_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Torque equation (linear region):**
$$T_a = k_t \phi I_a = k_t (k I_a) I_a = k_t k I_a^2$$

$$\boxed{T_a \propto I_a^2}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This is the **inversely proportional hyperbolic speed-torque characteristic** of a series motor — speed falls rapidly as load increases, providing natural "soft" behavior desirable in traction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Three-Point Starter Cut-Out Resistance Steps

**Scenario:** A 3-point starter has $n$ resistance sections, and the starting current must oscillate between $I_1$ (max) and $I_2$ (min) as the handle is moved from start to run position.

**Step 1 — Initially (handle at position 1), all resistance is in circuit:**
$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_a + R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2 — As motor speeds up, $E_b$ increases, current drops to $I_2$ at the same handle position:**
$$I_2 = \frac{V - E_{b1}}{R_a + R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — When handle is moved to position 2, $R_3$ is cut out, and current again rises to $I_1$ (approximately, because $E_b$ hasn't changed instantaneously):**
$$I_1 \approx \frac{V - E_{b1}}{R_a + R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — General relation between consecutive steps:**
$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_2}{R_3} = \frac{R_3}{R_4} = \cdots = k$$

where $k$ is a constant determined by:
$$k = \sqrt[n]{\frac{V - I_2 R_a}{V - I_1 R_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Each resistance section can be calculated as:**
$$R_1 = (R_a + R_{ext}) - R_a = R_{ext}$$
$$R_2 = \frac{R_1}{k}, \quad R_3 = \frac{R_2}{k} = \frac{R_1}{k^2}$$

**Conclusion:** This geometric progression of starter resistance sections ensures that the current stays within the safe band $I_1$ to $I_2$ throughout acceleration.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — DC Servo Motor Transfer Function (Armature Control)

**Assumption:** Field flux $\phi$ is held constant. Control input is armature voltage $V_a$.

**Step 1 — Armature circuit KVL (with inductance $L_a$):**
$$V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$

where back EMF is:
$$e_b = K_b \omega_m$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical equation (rotor dynamics):**
$$T_m = J \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \omega_m + T_L$$

where the developed torque is:
$$T_m = K_t i_a$$

**Step 3 — Take the Laplace transform of Step 1 (zero initial conditions):**
$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \omega_m(s)$$

**Step 4 — Take the Laplace transform of Step 2 (assume $T_L = 0$):**
$$K_t I_a(s) = (J s + B) \omega_m(s)$$

$$\Rightarrow I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \omega_m(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 3:**
$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B) \omega_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b \omega_m(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $\omega_m(s)$:**
$$V_a(s) = \omega_m(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 7 — Form the transfer function $\omega_m(s)/V_a(s)$:**
$$\frac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)}}$$

This is a **second-order system**, which is the standard model for DC servo motor analysis in KTU control systems papers.

**Step 8 — Damping ratio and natural frequency (if needed):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{R_a B + K_t K_b}{L_a J}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{L_a B + R_a J}{2 \sqrt{L_a J (R_a B + K_t K_b)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Starting Method Comparison

| Method | Components | Current Limit | Suitability | Maintenance |
|--------|------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** | Switch only | None (very high) | Very small DC motors | Low |
| **3-Point Starter** | Resistances + NVR coil + OLR coil | Adjustable by section | DC Shunt & Compound motors | Medium |
| **4-Point Starter** | Resistances + NVR (separate) + OLR | Adjustable | DC Series motors (low field current cases) | Medium |
| **Face-plate Starter** | Variable resistance on face plate | Adjustable | Older industrial installations | High |
| **Automatic Starter** | Contactors + relays + resistors | Programmable | Modern industrial panels | Low |
| **Thyristor Soft Starter** | SCR bridge + firing circuit | Smooth ramp | All modern DC drives | Very Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** While traditional resistance starters are still in the syllabus, modern drives use **thyristor (SCR) chopper control** for starting. Be prepared to compare the two in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DC Series Motor Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
  n1["DC SUPPLY V"] --> n2["ARMATURE TERMINAL A1"]
  n2 --> n3["ARMATURE RESISTANCE Ra"]
  n3 --> n4["BACK EMF SOURCE Eb"]
  n4 --> n5["ARMATURE TERMINAL A2"]
  n5 --> n6["SERIES FIELD WINDING Rse"]
  n6 --> n7["TERMINAL F1"]
  n7 --> n8["NEGATIVE SUPPLY"]
  n8 --> n1
  n9["ARMATURE CURRENT Ia"] -.-> n3
  n10["SAME CURRENT FLOWS THROUGH FIELD"] -.-> n6
```

**Key Inference:** Single current loop — same $I_a = I_{se}$ flows through armature and field.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DC Compound Motor (Cumulative) Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
  n1["DC SUPPLY V POSITIVE"] --> n2["NODE A"]
  n2 --> n3["SHUNT FIELD Rsh"]
  n3 --> n4["NODE B NEGATIVE"]
  n2 --> n5["ARMATURE Ra PLUS Eb"]
  n5 --> n6["NODE C"]
  n6 --> n7["SERIES FIELD Rse"]
  n7 --> n8["NODE D"]
  n8 --> n4
  n9["FLUX SHUNT CONSTANT"] -.-> n3
  n10["FLUX SERIES AIDS SHUNT"] -.-> n7
```

**Key Inference:** Two parallel paths — shunt field is constant, series field is load-dependent and **adds** to shunt flux.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Point Starter Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
  p1["SUPPLY POSITIVE L"] --> p2["STARTING RESISTANCE BANK R1 R2 R3 IN SERIES"]
  p2 --> p3["ARMATURE TERMINAL A"]
  p3 --> p4["ARMATURE"]
  p4 --> p5["TERMINAL A PRIME"]
  p5 --> p6["SERIES OR SHUNT FIELD"]
  p6 --> p7["NEGATIVE SUPPLY"]
  p8["NVR COIL HOLDING HANDLE IN RUN POSITION"] -.-> p2
  p9["OLR COIL FOR OVERLOAD TRIP"] -.-> p2
  p10["HANDLE STARTS AT POSITION 1 ALL R IN CIRCUIT"] --> p11["GRADUAL ACCELERATION"]
  p11 --> p12["HANDLE AT RUN POSITION ALL R CUT OUT"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Point Starter Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
  q1["SUPPLY POSITIVE"] --> q2["STARTING RESISTANCE R1 R2 R3"]
  q2 --> q3["ARMATURE"]
  q3 --> q4["NEGATIVE SUPPLY"]
  q5["NVR COIL CONNECTED DIRECTLY ACROSS SUPPLY INDEPENDENT OF FIELD"] -.-> q2
  q6["SERIES FIELD IN ARMATURE LOOP"] --> q3
  q7["OVERLOAD RELEASE OLR IN SERIES WITH NVR"] --> q5
```

**Key Advantage:** NVR coil is not in the field circuit, so variations in field current do not affect holding torque of the NVR.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — DC Servo Motor Control Architecture (Armature Control)

```mermaid
graph LR
  r1["REFERENCE VOLTAGE VREF"] --> r2["COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION"]
  r3["FEEDBACK FROM TACHOMETER K_b omega"] --> r2
  r2 --> r4["ERROR SIGNAL E"]
  r4 --> r5["POWER AMPLIFIER GAIN K_amp"]
  r5 --> r6["ARMATURE VOLTAGE V_a"]
  r6 --> r7["DC SERVO MOTOR TRANSFER FUNCTION"]
  r7 --> r8["OUTPUT SPEED omega_m"]
  r8 --> r3
  r8 --> r9["OUTPUT SHAFT POSITION theta"]
  r10["DISTURBANCE T_L"] -.-> r7
```

**Key Inference:** Classic closed-loop feedback architecture — servo is inherently a feedback-controlled device.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — Starting Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
  s1["DC MOTOR TO BE STARTED"] --> s2{"MOTOR TYPE"}
  s2 -->|SHUNT OR COMPOUND| s3["USE 3 POINT STARTER"]
  s2 -->|SERIES| s4["FIELD CURRENT IS ARMATURE CURRENT"]
  s4 --> s5{"FIELD CURRENT SUITABLE FOR NVR COIL"}
  s5 -->|YES| s3
  s5 -->|NO VERY LOW OR VERY HIGH| s6["USE 4 POINT STARTER"]
  s3 --> s7["PROVIDES HOLDING TORQUE VIA NVR"]
  s6 --> s8["NVR INDEPENDENT CIRCUIT"]
  s7 --> s9["FINAL ANSWER"]
  s8 --> s9
```

---

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram — Torque-Speed Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
  t1["SHUNT MOTOR N VS T"] -.-> t2["ALMOST CONSTANT SPEED DROOPING SLIGHTLY"]
  t3["SERIES MOTOR N VS T"] -.-> t4["HYPERBOLIC SHARP DROOP HIGH STARTING TORQUE"]
  t5["CUMULATIVE COMPOUND N VS T"] -.-> t6["INTERMEDIATE BETWEEN SHUNT AND SERIES"]
  t7["DIFFERENTIAL COMPOUND N VS T"] -.-> t8["RISING CHARACTERISTIC UNSTABLE"]
```
