# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a DC series motor. Mention any two applications. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A DC series motor is a self-excited DC motor in which the **field winding is connected in series with the armature winding**. The same current flows through both windings, so the field flux is proportional to the armature current (in the linear region of magnetization).

**Two Applications:**
1. **Electric traction** (trains, trams, trolleybuses) — because of its high starting torque.
2. **Cranes and hoists** — because it provides high torque at low speeds under heavy loads.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Diagram/working note (1) + Applications (1).

---

### Question 2: Why is a starter necessary for a DC motor? [KTU University Exam - May 2024] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A starter is necessary for a DC motor because **at the instant of starting, the motor is at rest and hence the back EMF ($E_b$) is zero**. Applying the armature voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{a,start} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

Since the armature resistance $R_a$ is very small (typically 0.1 to 1 $\Omega$), the starting current becomes **10 to 20 times the rated current**. This excessive current can:
1. Damage the armature winding due to overheating.
2. Cause heavy sparking at the commutator and brushes.
3. Produce a large voltage drop affecting other connected equipment.

Therefore, an external starting resistance (starter) is inserted in series with the armature and gradually cut out as the motor accelerates and develops back EMF.

> **Mark Distribution:** Starting current equation (1) + Numerical insight (1) + Three harmful effects (1).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Cumulative Compound Motor and Three-Point Starter [14 Marks]

**Sub-part (a):** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a **cumulative compound DC motor**. Compare its speed-torque characteristics with that of a series and shunt motor. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A cumulative compound DC motor has two field windings on each pole:
- **Shunt field winding:** Made of many turns of thin wire, connected across the armature.
- **Series field winding:** Made of few turns of thick wire, connected in series with the armature.

In the cumulative type, the two fields are connected such that their **fluxes add up** ($\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$).

**Working:**
- When supply is given, the shunt field produces a constant flux $\phi_{sh}$.
- The series field produces a flux $\phi_{se}$ that increases with armature current.
- Total flux = $\phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$ increases with load.
- Torque $T \propto \phi_{total} \cdot I_a$ is higher than a shunt motor of same rating.

**Speed-Torque Comparison:**

| Motor Type | Speed Regulation | Starting Torque | Stability |
|------------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Shunt | Excellent (nearly constant speed) | Moderate | Very stable |
| Series | Poor (large speed drop) | Very high | Stable under load |
| Cumulative Compound | Good (between shunt and series) | High | Stable |

[Insert neat labeled circuit diagram of cumulative compound motor showing series and shunt field with proper polarity markings.]

**Conclusion:** Cumulative compound motor combines the advantages of high starting torque (from series field) and good speed regulation (from shunt field), making it ideal for applications like elevators, rolling mills, and printing presses.

> **Mark Distribution:** Construction (2) + Working (2) + Comparison table (2) + Diagram (1).

---

**Sub-part (b):** Draw the neat circuit diagram of a **three-point starter** for a DC shunt motor and explain its function. Mention the role of the NVR coil and OLR coil. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (Description for drawing):**

The three-point starter consists of:
1. A **starting resistance** divided into several sections (R1, R2, R3, ...).
2. A **movable handle** that slides over the resistance studs.
3. A **No-Volt Release (NVR) coil** connected between the field terminal and the handle's final run position.
4. An **Overload Release (OLR) coil** connected in series with the NVR.
5. A **spring** that returns the handle to OFF position on power failure.

**Working:**
- Initially, the handle is in the OFF/start position — all starting resistances are in the circuit.
- As the handle is moved stud by stud, resistances are gradually cut out.
- In the final RUN position, all resistances are cut out, and the NVR coil holds the handle in position against the spring tension.

**Role of NVR (No-Volt Release) Coil:**
- Connected across the supply through the field winding.
- When supply fails, current through NVR becomes zero, magnetic pull vanishes, and the spring pulls the handle back to OFF.
- This prevents the motor from restarting unexpectedly when supply returns.

**Role of OLR (Overload Release) Coil:**
- Connected in series with NVR.
- During overload, excessive current through the armature also passes through the OLR coil (or a trip mechanism activates it).
- The OLR coil becomes strong enough to release a latch, allowing the spring to return the handle to OFF, protecting the motor.

[Insert neat labeled diagram: supply L → starting resistance bank → armature → field winding → NVR coil → supply N. Also show spring and latch mechanism.]

> **Mark Distribution:** Circuit diagram (2) + Working explanation (2) + NVR role (1.5) + OLR role (1.5).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — DC Servo Motor and Starter Resistance Calculation [14 Marks]

**Sub-part (a):** Explain the construction and working of a **DC servo motor** with a neat block diagram. Why is it preferred over a normal DC motor in control applications? **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A DC servo motor is essentially a DC motor (shunt or separately excited) with the following special design features:
- **Low rotor inertia** — achieved by using a small-diameter, long-length rotor (to reduce $J = mr^2$).
- **High starting torque** — achieved by high flux density and low armature resistance.
- **Linear torque-speed characteristics** — operates in the unsaturated region of the magnetization curve.
- **Low armature inductance** — ensures fast electrical response.
- **Separate field excitation** (constant field) or permanent magnet field for stable operation.

**Working Principle (Armature Control):**
- The field winding is supplied by a **constant DC voltage**, so $\phi$ is constant.
- The control signal is the **armature voltage $V_a$**, which is varied by the controller based on the error signal.
- Developed torque: $T = K_t \phi I_a \propto I_a$ (since $\phi$ is constant).
- Speed: $\omega_m \propto V_a$ (for small $R_a$ drop).

**Block Diagram (Describe):**
```
Reference input → Comparator → Error signal → Power amplifier → Armature voltage $V_a$ → DC Servo Motor → Output speed $\omega_m$ and position $\theta$ → Tachometer/Encoder → Feedback to Comparator
```

**Why Preferred Over Normal DC Motor:**
1. **Fast dynamic response** due to low inertia and low inductance.
2. **Linear characteristics** make mathematical modeling and controller design easy.
3. **Stable operation** over a wide speed range.
4. **Suitable for closed-loop control** because of predictable transfer function.
5. **No dead zone** — develops torque from zero speed itself.

> **Mark Distribution:** Construction (2) + Working with block diagram (2) + Five advantages (1.5) + Conclusion (1.5).

---

**Sub-part (b):** A 220 V, 10 HP DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 $\Omega$ and rated armature current of 38 A. It is to be started with a starter that limits the starting current to **1.5 times the rated value**. Calculate: **(i)** The total resistance to be inserted in the armature circuit at start. **(ii)** The value of the external starting resistance. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Supply voltage: $V = 220\ V$
- Armature resistance: $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- Rated armature current: $I_{a,rated} = 38\ A$
- Permissible starting current: $I_{safe} = 1.5 \times 38 = 57\ A$

---

**Part (i): Total resistance in armature circuit at start**

Using the armature circuit equation at start (with $E_b = 0$):

$$V = I_{safe} \cdot R_{total}$$

$$R_{total} = \frac{V}{I_{safe}} = \frac{220}{57}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$R_{total} = \frac{220}{57} = 3.8596\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{total} \approx 3.86\ \Omega}$$

---

**Part (ii): External starting resistance**

$$R_{ext} = R_{total} - R_a$$

**Substituting values:**

$$R_{ext} = 3.86 - 0.5$$

$$R_{ext} = 3.36\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{ext} \approx 3.36\ \Omega}$$

---

**Verification:**
$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}} = \frac{220}{0.5 + 3.36} = \frac{220}{3.86} = 57.0\ A \checkmark$$

This confirms the calculation. The starter must be designed with multiple resistance sections (typically 5 to 8 studs) to gradually cut out this $3.36\ \Omega$ as the motor accelerates.

> **Mark Distribution:** Data extraction (1) + Part (i) equation and calculation (3) + Part (ii) equation and calculation (2) + Verification (1).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that $E_b = 0$ at start** — Many students apply the running condition formula $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ at start and substitute a non-zero $E_b$. Always remember: at the **instant of starting, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$**.
>
> 2. **Confusing series motor field current with armature current** — In a series motor, $I_a = I_f$, but in a shunt motor, $I_f = V/R_{sh}$ is independent of $I_a$. Students often write $I_a = I_f$ for shunt motors, which is wrong.
>
> 3. **Drawing the NVR coil in the wrong place** — In a 3-point starter, the NVR coil is connected **in series with the shunt field**, not directly across the supply. In a 4-point starter, the NVR is across the supply independently.
>
> 4. **Confusing cumulative and differential compound motors** — Always check the polarity dot markings on the field windings. Cumulative = dots on same side, Differential = dots on opposite sides.
>
> 5. **Servo motor transfer function errors** — Many students forget the back EMF feedback term $K_t K_b$ in the denominator, which is critical for stability analysis.
>
> 6. **Numerical unit errors** — Speed must be in RPM consistently; flux in Weber; torque in N·m. Mixing units (e.g., rad/s with N·m·rad) leads to full mark deduction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Series Motor
- [x] Field winding in series with armature; $I_a = I_f = I_L$.
- [x] Flux $\phi \propto I_a$ (linear region).
- [x] Torque $T \propto I_a^2$ — high starting torque.
- [x] Speed $N \propto 1/I_a$ — inverse hyperbolic characteristic.
- [x] **Never run at no-load** — speed becomes dangerously high (mechanical destruction).
- [x] Used in traction, cranes, hoists.

### Compound Motor
- [x] Two field windings: shunt (parallel) + series (in series with armature).
- [x] **Cumulative** (series aids shunt) → stable, used in elevators, rolling mills.
- [x] **Differential** (series opposes shunt) → unstable, rarely used as motor.
- [x] $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} \pm \phi_{se}$.

### Starters
- [x] Necessary because $E_b = 0$ at start → $I_{a,start} = V/R_a$ is 10-20× rated.
- [x] 3-point starter: NVR in series with field — for shunt/compound motors.
- [x] 4-point starter: NVR across supply — for series motors and special cases.
- [x] NVR coil prevents restart on supply failure.
- [x] OLR coil trips on overload.
- [x] Starter resistance calculated by $R_{ext} = V/I_{safe} - R_a$.

### DC Servo Motor
- [x] Low-inertia rotor for fast response.
- [x] Operates in linear magnetization region.
- [x] Two control methods: **armature control** (field constant) and **field control** (armature constant).
- [x] Transfer function: second-order, $\omega_m/V_a = K_t/[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b]$.
- [x] Time constants: $\tau_e = L_a/R_a$ (electrical), $\tau_m = R_a J/(K_t K_b)$ (mechanical).
- [x] Used in robotics, CNC, aerospace, camera autofocus.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- [x] $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$
- [x] $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
- [x] $I_{a,start} = V/(R_a + R_{ext})$
- [x] $T \propto \phi I_a$
- [x] $R_{ext} = V/I_{safe} - R_a$
- [x] Servo transfer function (2nd order)

### Conversion Factors (Keep Handy)
- [x] 1 HP = 746 W
- [x] 1 N·m = 10.197 kg·cm
- [x] $\omega$ (rad/s) = $2\pi N/60$ where N is in RPM
- [x] Back EMF constant $K_b$ (V·s/rad) and torque constant $K_t$ (N·m/A) are **numerically equal in SI units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** AC Servo Motors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **AC Servo Motor** is a specially designed, **two-phase induction motor** that is optimized for high-performance **closed-loop position control** and **low-power servo applications**. It is essentially a two-phase, reversible induction motor with two stator windings — a **reference (fixed) winding** and a **control (variable) winding** — physically displaced by **90° in space** and electrically excited by voltages that are **90° apart in phase**. Unlike a standard induction motor, it is built to operate in the **low-slip region (near synchronous speed)** with a high-resistance rotor (squirrel-cage or drag-cup) to obtain a stable, linear, and nearly drooping negative torque–speed characteristic suitable for servomechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AC Servo Motors are explicitly listed under Module 1 of *PCBRT502 – Electrical Drives and Control Systems*. Examiners frequently test the construction, working principle, torque equation, torque–speed characteristics, and the comparison with DC Servo Motors. Weightage is usually **7–14 marks** per question paper. ### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy Think of an AC Servo Motor as the **"steering wheel motor" of a robot arm**. Imagine you are driving a remote-controlled toy car: - Your **right hand on the steering wheel** represents the *control signal* — small twist, big response. - The **automatic road sensor** that keeps the car centered represents the *reference signal* — it always wants the car to go straight. - When you turn the steering wheel slightly (small control input), the car's wheels turn a little, and the motor inside the steering system (the servo) reacts **precisely** and **instantly** to that small input. - The motor has very **low inertia** (it is light and quick to respond) and it **obeys your command exactly** — no overshoot, no laziness. In engineering terms: - The **reference winding** = the "constant pull" toward a desired position. - The **control winding** = the "fine-tuning knob" that pushes the shaft to the exact position demanded by the controller. - Together, they create a **rotating magnetic field** that drives the rotor to follow the command. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** The control winding voltage magnitude (or phase) determines the *amount* of torque, and the *direction* of rotation can be reversed simply by **reversing the polarity of the control voltage** — a property no ordinary induction motor possesses. This is what makes the AC Servo Motor unique. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Exam-Relevant) - **Frequency of operation:** 50 Hz, 60 Hz, or 400 Hz (aerospace/military). - **Power rating:** Typically **few watts to a few hundred watts** (fractional horsepower). - **Operating speed:** Up to **3000–6000 rpm** (synchronous at 50 Hz = 1500 rpm, 4-pole). - **Rotor types:** **Squirrel-cage** (industrial) and **Drag-cup** (high-performance, low inertia). - **Number of poles:** Usually **2 or 4 poles**. - **No centrifugal switch is required** because rotor resistance is already high and operation is in low-slip region. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction Breakdown (Bullet Logic) **A. Stator Assembly (Laminated Silicon Steel Core)** - Carries **two independent windings** uniformly distributed and embedded in slots. - **Reference Winding (also called Fixed or Exciter Winding):** - Supplied with a **constant AC voltage** of rated magnitude and frequency. - Also called the *reference phase*. - Fixed supply → acts as the "anchor" of operation. - **Control Winding (also called Variable Winding):** - Supplied with a **variable-magnitude, variable-phase AC voltage**. - The control signal (from a servo amplifier) drives this winding. - Functions as the "command input" of the motor. - The two windings are physically placed **90 electrical degrees apart** in space. - A **capacitor** is often connected in series with one of the windings to ensure the required **90° phase displacement** between the two winding currents. **B. Rotor Assembly (Two Variants)** - **Squirrel-Cage Rotor:** - Slotted laminated core with copper or aluminium bars short-circuited by end rings. - **High rotor resistance** is the design goal (achieved by using high-resistivity brass bars instead of copper). - Robust, cheap, slightly higher inertia. - **Drag-Cup Rotor:** - A thin **non-magnetic metallic cup** (usually aluminium) rotates in the air gap between an inner stationary core and an outer stationary core. - **Extremely low inertia** → very fast response. - Used in precision instrumentation and aerospace servos. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why high rotor resistance?** A high rotor resistance shifts the **maximum torque point toward higher slip (toward zero speed)**. This makes the torque–speed curve have a **negative slope near synchronous speed**, which is mathematically necessary for **closed-loop stability** (a positive slope would cause the system to "run away"). ### 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. The **reference winding** is energized with a constant sinusoidal voltage $V_r$. 2. The **control winding** is energized with a sinusoidal voltage $V_c$ that may differ in **both magnitude and phase** from $V_r$. 3. Because the two windings are **90° apart in space** and the two currents are ideally **90° apart in phase**, they together produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap. 4. This rotating field **induces currents in the rotor conductors** (Faraday's Law). 5. These induced currents interact with the rotating field to produce a **torque** (Lorentz Force / Lenz's Law) that drags the rotor in the **direction of field rotation**. 6. The **magnitude of the torque** is **proportional to the control winding voltage** $V_c$ and the **sine of the phase angle $\beta$ between the two fields**. 7. The **direction of rotation** reverses when the control voltage **reverses polarity** (because the rotating field direction reverses). 8. In a **closed-loop position control system**, an error signal (difference between desired and actual position) is amplified and fed to the control winding. The motor then drives the load to **nullify the error**. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice - **Why no centrifugal switch?** Because the motor is designed to operate in the **low-slip region (near synchronous speed)** as a normal running condition, not as a starting transient. A centrifugal switch is required only in split-phase motors that need to disconnect an auxiliary winding after starting. - **Why 90° spatial displacement between windings?** Because a true **rotating magnetic field** requires two sinusoidally distributed windings displaced by 90° in space and excited by currents 90° apart in time. This is the **Ferraris principle** (1885). - **Why is rotor resistance high?** To obtain a **linear torque-speed characteristic** and to ensure that the slope of the curve is **negative** (drooping) — a prerequisite for **stable servomechanism operation**. - **Why is rotor inertia kept low (drag-cup)?** Because the **mechanical time constant** of the servo system is directly proportional to rotor inertia. Lower inertia → faster dynamic response. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Notation used:** All voltages are RMS, $V_c$ = control voltage, $V_r$ = reference voltage, $\beta$ = torque angle (phase angle between the two stator fields), $s$ = slip, $T$ = electromagnetic torque, $T_m$ = maximum torque, $\omega_s$ = synchronous angular speed, $T_L$ = load torque. | # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning / Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $V_r = V_m \cos(\omega t)$ | Reference winding voltage (fixed amplitude, fixed phase) | | 2 | $V_c = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot k$ | Control winding voltage (variable amplitude factor $k$) | | 3 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rad/s | Synchronous speed; $P$ = number of poles | | 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm | Synchronous speed in RPM | | 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (fraction); $N_r$ = rotor speed | | 6 | $T \propto V_c \cdot \sin(\beta)$ | Torque is proportional to control voltage and sine of torque angle | | 7 | $T = \dfrac{K \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Standard induction motor torque (Kloss equation form) | | 8 | $T_{max} \propto \dfrac{V^2}{R_2}$ at $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Maximum torque and slip of max torque — **high $R_2$ shifts $s_{max}$ toward 1** | | 9 | $T_m = \dfrac{K \cdot V^2}{2 X_2}$ | Maximum torque (independent of $R_2$) | | 10 | $T_{starting} \approx T_{max}$ for $R_2 \gg X_2$ | When rotor resistance is very high, starting torque approaches max torque — **servo motor property** | | 11 | $\tau_m = J \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = T - T_L$ | Mechanical dynamic equation; $J$ = moment of inertia | | 12 | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t \cdot K_b}{J \cdot R}}$ | Natural frequency of a simple servo loop | | 13 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{K_t K_b J R}}$ | Damping ratio of the servo system | | 14 | $\dfrac{V_c}{V_r} = \alpha$ | Voltage ratio (signal level); $\alpha = 1$ → circular field, $\alpha = 0$ → pulsating field (no rotation) | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial robotics:** Joint position control in robotic arms (e.g., FANUC, KUKA). - **CNC machine tools:** Feed-drive positioning of the tool head. - **Aerospace:** Flight control surface actuation (ailerons, elevators, rudders). - **Antenna positioning:** Radar and satellite dish tracking systems. - **Automatic door / valve actuators:** HVAC and process industries. - **Military servos:** Gun turret aiming, missile fin control. - **Printers and plotters:** Paper-feed and head-positioning mechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > AC Servo Motors are preferred over DC Servo Motors in applications requiring **spark-free operation**, **high reliability in explosive environments**, and **low maintenance** (no brushes/commutator to wear out). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Torque Equation of an AC Servo Motor **Statement:** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed by a two-phase AC servo motor, showing its dependence on control voltage and torque angle. **Step 1 — Define the Two Stator Voltages** Let the reference winding be supplied with a fixed sinusoidal voltage and the control winding with a variable sinusoidal voltage, the two being displaced by 90° in space and 90° in time (ideal case). $$ V_r(t) = V_{mr} \cdot \cos(\omega t) $$ $$ V_c(t) = V_{mc} \cdot \sin(\omega t) $$ where $V_{mr}$ and $V_{mc}$ are the peak amplitudes of the reference and control winding voltages, respectively. The corresponding currents lag by their respective winding impedances; for the balanced ideal case, the currents are also in quadrature. **Step 2 — Define the Resultant Air-Gap Flux Density** The two windings, displaced by 90° in space, produce two sinusoidally distributed flux density waves, $B_1(\theta, t)$ along the reference axis and $B_2(\theta, t)$ along the control axis (90° spatial offset). $$ B_1(\theta, t) = B_1 \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta) $$ $$ B_2(\theta, t) = B_2 \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta) $$ The net flux density at any point $\theta$ and time $t$ is the superposition: $$ B(\theta, t) = B_1 \cos(\omega t) \cos(\theta) + B_2 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta) $$ **Step 3 — Verify the Rotating Field Nature** To confirm rotation, evaluate $B$ at $\theta = \omega t$ (a point moving synchronously): $$ B(\theta = \omega t, t) = B_1 \cos^2(\omega t) + B_2 \sin^2(\omega t) $$ For a **circular rotating field** ($B_1 = B_2 = B$): $$ B(\omega t, t) = B[\cos^2(\omega t) + \sin^2(\omega t)] = B = \text{constant} $$ This proves the field rotates at synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s$ with constant amplitude $B$. **Step 4 — Express Torque Using the Field-Theory Form** The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in a two-phase machine is given by the cross-product of the two stator MMF waves, integrated over the air gap. For a 2-pole, 2-phase machine, the torque reduces to: $$ T(t) = K \cdot B_1(t) \cdot B_2(t) \cdot \sin(\beta) $$ where $\beta$ is the spatial angle between the two fields (torque angle) and $K$ is a machine constant depending on geometry. **Step 5 — Substitute the Time-Varying Fluxes** Substituting the peak flux densities (proportional to peak voltages) into the torque expression: $$ T(t) = K \cdot V_{mr} \cdot V_{mc} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\beta) $$ **Step 6 — Apply the Trigonometric Identity** Using $\sin(2x) = 2 \sin x \cos x$: $$ T(t) = \frac{K \cdot V_{mr} \cdot V_{mc}}{2} \cdot \sin(2\omega t) \cdot \sin(\beta) $$ **Step 7 — Average the Torque Over One Electrical Cycle** Since the rotor mechanical time constant is much larger than the electrical time period ($T_{mech} \gg T_{elec}$), the rotor "sees" only the **average torque**: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T(t)\, dt = \frac{K \cdot V_{mr} \cdot V_{mc}}{2} \cdot \sin(\beta) \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin(2\omega t)\, dt}_{= \, 0} $$ Wait — the average of $\sin(2\omega t)$ over a complete cycle is zero. This is because the rotating field is **symmetric** and the two components are balanced. The correct approach is to express the torque in terms of the **slip-induced relative motion** between the rotor and the field. **Step 7 (Corrected) — Include the Rotor Slip and Re-derive the Average Torque** The relative angular velocity between the rotating field and the rotor is $\omega_{slip} = s \cdot \omega_s$, where $s$ is the slip. The torque angle $\beta$ therefore varies with time as: $$ \beta(t) = s \cdot \omega_s \cdot t + \beta_0 $$ The instantaneous torque becomes: $$ T(t) = K \cdot V_{mr} \cdot V_{mc} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(s \omega_s t + \beta_0) $$ For the **quasi-steady** analysis, the rotor experiences the time-averaged torque. Using the orthogonality of the rotating fields, the **average electromagnetic torque** reduces to: $$ T_{avg} = K' \cdot V_r \cdot V_c \cdot \sin(\beta) $$ where $K'$ is a machine constant (proportional to $R_2/s$), $V_r$ and $V_c$ are RMS voltages, and $\beta$ is the steady-state torque angle. **Step 8 — Final Compact Form (Kloss-like Equation for Servo)** $$ T = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot V_c \cdot V_r \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \cdot \sin(\beta) $$ Or, more compactly, for a fixed reference voltage $V_r$: $$ \boxed{T = K_t \cdot V_c \cdot \sin(\beta)} $$ where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** of the motor (units: N·m / V). This linear relation between $T$ and $V_c$ is the single most important property exploited in **closed-loop position control**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AC Servo vs DC Servo Motor | Parameter | AC Servo Motor | DC Servo Motor | |---|---|---| | **Construction** | Two stator windings, no commutator | Stator + armature winding + commutator + brushes | | **Commutator / Brushes** | **Absent** → maintenance-free | **Present** → periodic maintenance, sparking | | **Rotor** | Squirrel-cage or drag-cup (non-magnetic) | Armature winding on laminated core | | **Inertia** | **Low** (drag-cup) to medium (squirrel-cage) | Higher (armature + winding) | | **Power-to-weight ratio** | Moderate | Higher (especially for fractional HP) | | **Output power** | Fractional HP (few watts to ~750 W typical) | Up to several kW | | **Torque-speed characteristic** | Nearly linear with negative slope | Linear with negative slope | | **Frequency response** | Excellent at 60/400 Hz | Limited by armature inductance and commutation | | **Efficiency** | Moderate (60–80%) | Higher (75–90%) | | **Cooling** | Self-cooled (small) | Often fan-cooled | | **Cost** | Lower (no commutator) | Higher (precision commutator) | | **Reliability** | Higher (no brush wear) | Lower (brush wear limits life) | | **Use in explosive atmospheres** | **Yes** (no sparking) | **No** (brush sparking) | | **Control complexity** | Needs phase-shift network or amplifier | Simpler (armature voltage control) | | **Application** | Robotics, CNC, aerospace, instrumentation | Printers, small actuators, toys | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: AC Servo Motor in a Closed-Loop Position Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Desired Input Stage"] setpoint["Setpoint Position
Reference Signal"] end subgraph COMPARATOR["Error Detection"] subtractor["Summing Junction
Comparator"] end subgraph AMPLIFIER["Signal Conditioning"] amp["Servo Amplifier
High Gain DC Amplifier"] end subgraph AC_MOTOR["AC Servo Motor Assembly"] refWind["Reference Winding
Fixed AC Supply"] capBank["Phase Shift
Capacitor Network"] ctrlWind["Control Winding
Variable AC Input"] rotor["Drag Cup or
Squirrel Cage Rotor"] shaft["Output Shaft"] end subgraph FEEDBACK["Feedback Path"] sensor["Position Sensor
Potentiometer or LVDT"] fbAmp["Feedback Amplifier"] end subgraph LOAD["Mechanical Output"] gearTrain["Gear Reduction
Train"] load["Mechanical Load
Robotic Joint Antenna"] end setpoint -->|Desired Position| subtractor sensor -->|Actual Position| subtractor subtractor -->|Error Signal e t| amp amp -->|Amplified Control Voltage| ctrlWind refWind --> capBank capBank -->|Phase Shifted 90 deg| ctrlWind ctrlWind -->|Rotating Magnetic Field| rotor refWind -->|Rotating Magnetic Field| rotor rotor --> shaft shaft --> sensor shaft --> gearTrain gearTrain --> load sensor --> fbAmp fbAmp --> subtractor ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Architecture of an AC Servo Motor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR["STATOR ASSEMBLY Laminated Silicon Steel"] R["Reference Winding
Fixed AC Supply
Constant Magnitude"] C["Control Winding
Variable AC Supply
Amplitude Modulated"] end subgraph GAP["AIR GAP Region"] F1["Flux Wave 1
Along Reference Axis"] F2["Flux Wave 2
Along Control Axis"] RF["Resultant Rotating
Magnetic Field
Vector Sum at omega s"] end subgraph ROTOR["ROTOR ASSEMBLY"] RC["Rotor Conductors
Squirrel Cage Bars
or Drag Cup Wall"] IR["Induced Rotor
Currents by Faraday Law"] end subgraph OUTPUT["MECHANICAL OUTPUT"] TR["Developed Torque
Proportional to Vc sin beta"] SH["Shaft Rotation
Direction Reversible"] end R --> F1 C --> F2 F1 --> RF F2 --> RF RF -->|Cuts Conductors| RC RC --> IR IR -->|Lorentz Force
Interaction with Field| TR TR --> SH ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Torque-Speed Characteristic Curves ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TYPICAL["Family of Torque Speed Curves for Different Control Voltages"] A1["Vc equals 0.2 Vr
Low Control Voltage

Full Control Voltage

Stability Achieved

Synchronous Speed at Zero Torque

Along Real Axis
Phase Zero degrees"] VC["Control Voltage Vc
Along Imaginary Axis
Phase 90 degrees Lagging or Leading"] BETA["Torque Angle Beta
Spatial Angle Between
Two Flux Waves"] end VR -->|90 Degree Spatial
Displacement| BETA VC -->|90 Degree Electrical
Phase Shift| BETA BETA -->|Proportional to Torque| T["Developed Torque T
T equals Kt Vc sin Beta"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Block 4.1 shows the *external* control loop (error signal → amplifier → motor → load → feedback). Block 4.2 shows the *internal* electromagnetic energy conversion. Block 4.3 shows the *static* torque-speed behavior. Block 4.4 shows the *phasor* geometry that produces the rotating field. Together they cover the complete KTU Module 1 syllabus on AC Servo Motors. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Define an AC Servo Motor. List any four of its applications.* **Model Answer:** An **AC Servo Motor** is a two-phase induction motor designed for low-power servo applications, having two stator windings (reference and control) displaced by 90° in space, and a high-resistance rotor (squirrel-cage or drag-cup) that operates in the low-slip region with a linear, negatively-sloped torque–speed characteristic. **Four applications:** 1. Position control in robotic arms and CNC machine tools. 2. Flight control surface actuation in aerospace (ailerons, elevators, rudders). 3. Antenna positioning in radar and satellite communication systems. 4. Automatic door openers, valve actuators, and instrumentation drives. **[3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four applications = full marks]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Why is the rotor resistance of an AC Servo Motor made deliberately high? What is the effect on the torque–speed characteristic?* **Model Answer:** The rotor resistance is made **deliberately high** to obtain a **negatively-sloped, nearly linear torque–speed characteristic** in the operating region (low slip near synchronous speed). A high $R_2$ shifts the **maximum-torque slip** $s_{max} = R_2 / X_2$ toward unity, so the motor operates on the **stable side** of the torque–speed curve throughout its working range. **Effect on characteristic:** - The slope $\partial T / \partial N$ becomes **negative** (a prerequisite for closed-loop stability). - The curve becomes **linear** over a wide operating range. - The **starting torque** becomes comparable to the **maximum torque** (because $T_{max}$ becomes independent of $R_2$ but $T_{start}$ rises). **[3 marks: 1 for "why" + 2 for "effect on characteristic"]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working principle of a two-phase AC Servo Motor with neat diagrams. Discuss why the rotor resistance is kept high and why no centrifugal switch is required. **Model Solution:** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with two windings — *Reference (fixed)* and *Control (variable)* — placed 90 electrical degrees apart in space. A series capacitor is often used to ensure 90° phase shift between the two currents. - **Rotor:** Either a *squirrel-cage* (high-resistivity brass bars) or a *drag-cup* (thin aluminium cup between two stationary cores). The rotor has **no commutator, no brushes, no windings**. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - Reference winding is fed a constant AC voltage; control winding is fed a variable-magnitude AC voltage from a servo amplifier. - The two out-of-phase currents in spatially-displaced windings produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap. - This rotating field cuts the rotor conductors, inducing currents (Faraday's Law). - Interaction between induced rotor currents and the stator field produces a torque that drags the rotor in the direction of field rotation. - **Reversing the control voltage polarity reverses the rotation direction** — a property unique to servo motors. **Why high rotor resistance (1 mark):** To shift $s_{max} = R_2 / X_2$ toward higher values, ensuring a **negatively-sloped torque-speed curve** in the normal operating region. This is essential for **closed-loop stability** (a positive slope would cause system instability). **Why no centrifugal switch (1 mark):** The motor is designed to operate in the **low-slip region (near synchronous speed) under normal running conditions**, not just during starting. A centrifugal switch is required only when an auxiliary winding must be disconnected after starting — this is not the case in a servo motor, where the control winding remains energized throughout operation. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the torque equation of a two-phase AC Servo Motor and show that the average torque is proportional to the control winding voltage and the sine of the torque angle. Draw the torque-speed characteristics for different control voltages. **Model Solution:** **Derivation (5 marks):** Let the reference and control winding voltages be: $$ V_r = V_{mr} \cos(\omega t), \quad V_c = V_{mc} \sin(\omega t) $$ The two air-gap flux density waves are: $$ B_1(\theta, t) = B_1 \cos(\omega t) \cos(\theta), \quad B_2(\theta, t) = B_2 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta) $$ Net flux: $$ B(\theta, t) = B_1 \cos(\omega t)\cos(\theta) + B_2 \sin(\omega t)\sin(\theta) $$ Evaluating at $\theta = \omega t$ shows constant amplitude (rotating field). The instantaneous torque (from field cross-product) is: $$ T(t) = K \cdot B_1(t) \cdot B_2(t) \cdot \sin\beta $$ Averaging over one electrical cycle, using the slip-dependent relative motion: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{K \cdot V_r \cdot V_c \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2} \cdot \sin\beta $$ For a fixed reference voltage $V_r$ and high rotor resistance, the relation simplifies to: $$ \boxed{T = K_t \cdot V_c \cdot \sin\beta} $$ This proves the average torque is **directly proportional to control voltage** and **sine of the torque angle**. **Torque-Speed Characteristic (2 marks):** The family of curves is a set of nearly-straight, negatively-sloped lines passing through $(N_s, 0)$ for $V_c = 0$. As $V_c$ increases from 0.2$V_r$ to 1.0$V_r$, the torque at any given speed increases linearly. All curves meet at synchronous speed with zero torque. The negative slope ensures **stable closed-loop operation**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare AC Servo Motors with DC Servo Motors under the following heads: construction, commutation, torque-speed characteristic, maintenance, applications, and suitability for explosive environments. **Model Solution:** | Head | AC Servo Motor | DC Servo Motor | |---|---|---| | **Construction** | Two stator windings, no commutator | Stator + armature winding + commutator + brushes | | **Commutation** | Brushless — no sparking | Brush-commutator — produces sparks | | **Torque-Speed** | Linear, negative slope | Linear, negative slope | | **Maintenance** | Very low (no brushes) | Periodic (brush replacement) | | **Applications** | Robotics, CNC, aerospace, instrumentation | Small actuators, toys, printers | | **Explosive Environment** | **Suitable** (no sparking) | **Not suitable** (sparking risk) | **Additional Remarks (2 marks):** - AC Servo Motors have **lower rotor inertia** (especially drag-cup type), giving **faster dynamic response**. - DC Servo Motors have **higher efficiency** and **higher power-to-weight ratio** for the same rating. - AC Servo Motors require a **phase-shift capacitor or amplifier** to generate the quadrature excitation, adding to controller complexity. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A 50 Hz, 4-pole, two-phase AC Servo Motor has a rotor resistance of 10 Ω and standstill rotor reactance of 5 Ω. The reference winding is supplied with 100 V (RMS) at 50 Hz. Calculate (i) the synchronous speed, (ii) the slip at maximum torque, (iii) the maximum torque per phase constant $K = 1.0$, and (iv) the starting torque when the control voltage is 80 V. **Model Solution:** **(i) Synchronous speed:** $$ N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ \omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s} $$ **(ii) Slip at maximum torque:** $$ s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0 $$ (Note: $s_{max} > 1$ confirms that the motor operates on the stable, low-slip side for all $0 \le s \le 1$.) **(iii) Maximum torque:** $$ T_{max} = \frac{K \cdot V^2}{2 X_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 100^2}{2 \times 5} = 1000 \text{ N·m} $$ (The maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance $R_2$.) **(iv) Starting torque at $V_c = 80$ V and $s = 1$:** For a two-phase machine, the relevant per-phase induced EMF is proportional to the control voltage. The starting torque formula is: $$ T_{start} = \frac{K \cdot V_c^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} $$ Substituting: $$ T_{start} = \frac{1.0 \times 80^2 \times 10}{10^2 + 5^2} = \frac{64000}{125} = 512 \text{ N·m} $$ **Verification:** Starting torque ($512$ N·m) is less than maximum torque ($1000$ N·m), as expected. The ratio $T_{start}/T_{max} = 512/1000 = 0.512$, consistent with a moderately high rotor resistance. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in synchronous speed formula** when using $N_s = 120f/P$ — some students write $60f/P$. This gives 750 rpm instead of 1500 rpm → full mark deduction. > 2. **Confusing per-phase and total torque** — for a two-phase motor, the total torque is the sum of contributions from both phases, not a single-phase value. Examiners expect explicit per-phase analysis. > 3. **Not stating the assumption of high rotor resistance** in the derivation — this is the foundational property of an AC servo motor and omitting it costs 1 mark. > 4. **Drawing the torque-speed curve with a positive slope** — this is the most common and most penalized error. The slope MUST be negative. > 5. **Forgetting to mention "no centrifugal switch"** in construction questions — this is a favourite KTU differentiator between a normal induction motor and a servo motor. > 6. **Writing the torque equation as $T = K V_c$ instead of $T = K V_c \sin\beta$** — examiners specifically test whether students know the role of the torque angle. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — AC Servo Motors (Module 1, PCBRT502)** - [x] An AC Servo Motor is a **two-phase induction motor** with reference and control windings placed **90° apart in space**. - [x] The two windings are excited by voltages **90° apart in phase** (a series capacitor helps achieve this). - [x] Rotor types: **Squirrel-cage** (industrial) and **Drag-cup** (high-performance, low inertia). - [x] **Rotor resistance is deliberately high** to obtain a **negatively-sloped, linear torque-speed curve**. - [x] **No centrifugal switch** is required — the control winding stays energized during running. - [x] Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$ rpm; Angular speed: $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ rad/s. - [x] Slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. - [x] Slip at maximum torque: $s_{max} = R_2 / X_2$. - [x] Maximum torque: $T_{max} = K V^2 / (2 X_2)$ — **independent of $R_2$**. - [x] Average torque equation: $T = K_t \cdot V_c \cdot \sin\beta$ (linear in $V_c$). - [x] Reversing the control voltage polarity reverses the rotation direction. - [x] AC Servo Motors are **brushless, spark-free, low-maintenance, and reliable**. - [x] They are preferred in **explosive atmospheres, aerospace, robotics, and CNC applications**. - [x] Compared to DC Servo Motors: **lower efficiency, lower power-to-weight, but higher reliability and lower maintenance**. - [x] The torque-speed curve is a **family of negatively-sloped lines** through $(N_s, 0)$, parameterized by $V_c$. - [x] For stable closed-loop operation, the slope $\partial T / \partial N$ must be **negative** — a design imperative. - [x] KTU exam weightage: typically **7–14 marks per question**; expect derivations, comparisons, and numerical problems. ---

topics.title: Stepper Motor - Variable Reluctance - Permanent Magnet - Hybrid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motor - Variable Reluctance - Permanent Magnet - Hybrid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stepper Motor?

A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromagnetic device that converts a train of input electrical pulses (typically a square-wave DC sequence) into discrete, fixed angular increments (steps) of mechanical rotation. Unlike conventional DC or AC motors that rotate continuously, the shaft of a stepper motor rotates in precise, quantifiable **step angles** that are directly proportional to the number of input pulses. Because of this inherent **open-loop positional capability**, stepper motors are often called *"digital motors"* or *"pulse motors"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):** A stepper motor is an electromechanical energy conversion device whose output shaft moves in discrete angular steps, the magnitude and direction of which are functions of the digital input pulse train and stator excitation sequence, respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Compass and the Magnets" 🧭

Imagine you have a **non-magnetic iron disc** (the rotor) free to rotate on a smooth axle, surrounded by four **electromagnets** fixed at 90° intervals (the stator poles). Now connect these magnets to a battery one at a time in the order: Top → Right → Bottom → Left → Top...

Each time you activate an electromagnet, the iron disc will *snap* into alignment with that magnet because the magnetic flux always seeks the path of **minimum reluctance** (least resistance to flow). One pulse = one snap. The number of snaps per revolution is the **step resolution**. Replace the iron disc with a *permanently magnetized* disc, and the disc will not just snap, but will also actively *rotate toward* or *away from* the energized pole — like a compass needle obeying an external magnet.

### 1.3 The Three KTU 2024 Classified Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) classifies stepper motors into three principal families. The underlying physics — *torque generation* — differs in each.

| Family | Rotor Anatomy | Operating Principle | Inherent Step Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron salient poles, **no magnet** | Rotor moves to **minimize magnetic reluctance** of the flux path | **5° – 15°** |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially (or radially) magnetized **permanent magnet** rotor | Rotor aligns its **N–S poles** with the energized stator poles (attraction/repulsion) | **45° – 90°** |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Two soft-iron salient pole cups sandwiching an **axially magnetized PM** | Combines *minimum reluctance* torque *and* *permanent magnet* torque | **0.9° – 5°** |

### 1.4 Why Stepper Motors are Indispensable in Modern Engineering

- **Open-loop positional accuracy** — No encoder or feedback sensor is required for position control.
- **Excellent start–stop–reverse response** — Inherent dynamic braking on de-energization.
- **Direct digital compatibility** — Microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, PLC) outputs can drive them directly through a translator/driver IC (e.g., ULN2003, DRV8825, TMC2209).
- **High torque at zero speed (holding torque)** — Crucial for robotic joints, CNC Z-axes, 3D-printer extruders, disk-drive head positioners, and biomedical syringe pumps.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Snapshot (2024–2025):** Over **70 %** of the world's 3D printers and **45 %** of desktop CNC routers use **NEMA-17 hybrid stepper motors (1.8° step)**. Industrial automation prefers **NEMA-23 / NEMA-34** for higher torque loads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Magnetic Reluctance Foundation (Common to all three types)

For *any* magnetic circuit, the **reluctance** $\mathcal{R}$ is the magnetic analogue of electrical resistance:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu A}$$

where $l$ is the mean flux path length, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ is the permeability of the core material.

**Key physical insight:**
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the **permeability of free space** (a constant you should memorize).
- The *inductance* of a coil is $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}$, where $N$ is the number of turns.
- Therefore, **inductance is *inversely* proportional to reluctance**: when the rotor moves such that $\mathcal{R}$ decreases, $L$ *increases*.

> [!NOTE]
> The energy stored in a magnetic field is $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$. This is the *co-energy* (numerically equal to the stored energy in a linear magnetic system) from which the **electromagnetic torque** is derived via the principle of virtual work.

## 2.2 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Construction
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel stack with *m*-phase concentrated windings wound around salient poles.
- **Rotor:** Laminated silicon-steel cylinder with $N_r$ salient teeth and *no permanent magnet* and *no field winding*.
- **Excitation:** Phases are energized sequentially (e.g., A → B → C → A … for a 3-phase motor).

### 2.2.2 Working Principle
When phase A is excited, the rotor teeth align with the stator A-pole teeth because this position yields the **lowest magnetic reluctance** and thus the **highest inductance** (and the lowest stored magnetic energy for a given current). When phase B is excited next, the rotor rotates by one tooth-pitch to align with B-poles.

**Step angle (full-step, single-stack):**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N}$$

where $N$ is the total number of stable detent positions (= steps) per revolution. For a single-stack, $m$-phase VR stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \, N_r}$$

**Number of rotor teeth for a 3-stack VR stepper:**

$$N_r = \frac{360°}{m \, \theta_s}$$

### 2.2.3 Developed Torque in a VR Stepper
The torque in a VR motor arises *solely* from the variation of inductance with rotor position. From the **co-energy method**:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

If $L(\theta)$ is approximated as a sinusoid: $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$, then

$$T_e = -\frac{1}{2} \, N_r \, L_1 \, i^2 \, \sin(N_r \theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient property of VR torque:** $T_e \propto i^2$. This means the torque is **independent of the direction of current**. Consequently, a VR stepper can be operated with a **unipolar** (single-polarity) drive — a major cost advantage.

### 2.2.4 Limitations
- **No detent (residual) torque** when unexcited → the rotor is *free to rotate* manually.
- **Low holding torque** compared to PM and hybrid designs.
- **Coil current must be maintained** to hold position; otherwise the rotor drifts to the lowest-reluctance *unenergized* position.

---

## 2.3 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Construction
- **Stator:** Laminated stack with *m* phase windings, similar to VR.
- **Rotor:** Cylindrical **permanent magnet** (typically rare-earth ferrite in low-cost, Nd-Fe-B in high-torque versions), axially magnetized with alternating N–S poles around its circumference.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle
Unlike VR, the PM stepper's rotor *always* produces a constant magnetic field. When a stator phase is energized, the stator field either **attracts** the opposite rotor pole or **repels** the like pole. The rotor thus experiences an *active alignment torque* even with no current, which is why PM steppers exhibit a strong **detent torque**.

**Step angle:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2p \cdot m}$$

where $p$ is the number of pole pairs on the rotor and $m$ is the number of stator phases. For a typical 2-phase, 2-pole-pair PM stepper: $\theta_s = 360°/(2 \times 2 \times 2) = 45°$.

### 2.3.3 Developed Torque
The torque equation involves the interaction between the *permanent-magnet flux* $\phi_m$ and the *stator current* $i$. A simplified static form is:

$$T_e = K_t \, i \, \sin(\theta)$$

where $K_t$ is the motor torque constant (V·s/rad = N·m/A).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient property of PM torque:** $T_e \propto i$ (linear with current and **sign-dependent**). This mandates a **bipolar** (H-bridge) drive to control direction — a slight cost penalty but offering full four-quadrant torque.

### 2.3.4 Advantages
- **High detent torque** — the rotor resists motion even when unpowered (a passive safety feature).
- **Better damping** and lower resonance susceptibility.
- **Higher torque-to-inertia ratio** than VR.

### 2.3.5 Disadvantages
- **Coarse step angle** (typically $\geq 7.5°$); not suitable for high-resolution applications.
- **Limited high-speed performance** due to high rotor inertia (magnet mass).
- Rare-earth magnets can **demagnetize** if the stator current exceeds a critical value.

---

## 2.4 Hybrid Stepper Motor — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 Construction (the de facto industry standard)
The hybrid motor is a *meticulous* blend of the VR and PM principles:

1. **Two rotor cups** (south-facing and north-facing) made of laminated silicon steel, each with $N_r$ fine teeth.
2. The teeth of the two cups are **offset by half a tooth-pitch** (i.e., 180°/N_r in mechanical angle).
3. An **axially magnetized permanent magnet** sits between the two cups, magnetizing the front cup as N and the rear cup as S.
4. The **stator** has $2m$ poles (typically 8 poles for a 2-phase motor), each pole carrying a toothed structure of $N_r$ teeth.
5. Phase A poles and phase B poles are **interleaved** around the stator periphery, with the B-poles' teeth **offset by 1/4 of the rotor tooth-pitch** from the A-poles' teeth.

### 2.4.2 Working Principle — The "Two-Stage Alignment"
When phase A is excited:
- The N-rotor-cup teeth are pulled into alignment with A-pole teeth (minimum reluctance for the N-flux).
- Simultaneously, the S-rotor-cup teeth are pulled into alignment with the *opposite* A-pole teeth.
- Result: a powerful *bidirectional* alignment torque.

When the excitation is switched to phase B, the rotor must rotate by **one quarter of a tooth-pitch** so that the rotor teeth now line up with the B-pole teeth. This tiny displacement is the **step angle**.

**Step angle formula (for 2-phase, 2-cup hybrid):**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{4 \, N_r} = \frac{90°}{N_r}$$

Equivalently, in compact form (generalized for $m$ phases and $N_r$ teeth per cup):

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \, m \, N_r}$$

For a typical hybrid with $N_r = 50$ teeth per cup: $\theta_s = 90°/50 = \mathbf{1.8°}$ (i.e., **200 steps per revolution**).

### 2.4.3 Developed Torque
The hybrid motor produces torque from *both* reluctance variation and magnet-stator interaction:

$$T_e = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}_{\text{Reluctance component}} + \underbrace{K_t \, i \, \sin(\theta)}_{\text{Magnet component}}$$

The two terms **add constructively** in the hybrid design, yielding the highest torque-to-volume ratio among all three types.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry rule of thumb:** A NEMA-17 (42 mm frame) hybrid stepper delivers **~0.4 to 0.6 N·m** of holding torque — roughly **2× to 3×** that of an equivalently sized VR motor.

### 2.4.4 Advantages
- **Fine step resolution** (down to 0.9° / 400 steps per rev).
- **High holding torque** and **high detent torque**.
- **Excellent positioning accuracy** (±5 % non-accumulative, no closed loop required).
- Operates smoothly with **microstepping** (1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/256 subdivision).

### 2.4.5 Limitations
- Most expensive of the three types.
- Requires **two-phase bipolar drive** (H-bridge per phase).
- Higher rotor inertia than VR (magnet mass), but typically lower than pure-PM.

---

## 2.5 The Unified KTU 2024 Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** formula you may need in a KTU 2024 ESE / internal assessment for this topic. **Memorize this table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable to |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = l / (\mu A)$ | A·turns/Wb (or H$^{-1}$) | All three |
| 2 | Inductance | $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}$ | H (Henry) | All three |
| 3 | Stored magnetic energy | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ | J (Joule) | All three |
| 4 | Co-energy (linear case) | $W' = W = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ | J | All three |
| 5 | Electromagnetic torque (general) | $T_e = \partial W' / \partial \theta \big\vert_{i=\text{const}}$ | N·m | All three |
| 6 | VR-motor torque | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL/d\theta$ | N·m | VR, Hybrid |
| 7 | PM-motor torque | $T_e = K_t \, i \, \sin\theta$ | N·m | PM, Hybrid |
| 8 | Step angle (general) | $\theta_s = 360° / N$ | degrees (°) | All three |
| 9 | VR step angle (single-stack) | $\theta_s = 360° / (m \, N_r)$ | ° | VR |
| 10 | PM step angle | $\theta_s = 360° / (2 p \, m)$ | ° | PM |
| 11 | Hybrid step angle (2-cup) | $\theta_s = 360° / (2 m \, N_r)$ | ° | Hybrid |
| 12 | Shaft speed (rpm) | $n = 60 \, f / N$ | rpm | All three |
| 13 | Shaft angular speed | $\omega = 2 \pi f / N$ | rad/s | All three |
| 14 | Synchronous (step) frequency | $f = n \, N / 60$ | Hz (pulses/s) | All three |
| 15 | Resolution (steps/rev) | $N = 360° / \theta_s$ | dimensionless | All three |
| 16 | Torque–speed (idealised) | $T \approx T_h (1 - \omega / \omega_{\max})$ | N·m | All three |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, $m$ = number of stator phases, $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth, $p$ = number of pole pairs on PM rotor, $f$ = pulse frequency in Hz, $N$ = total number of steps per revolution, $T_h$ = holding torque.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Preferred Stepper Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 3D printers (Prusa, Ender, Bambu) | Hybrid 1.8° | Fine resolution, high holding torque |
| CNC routers / laser engravers | Hybrid 0.9°–1.8° | Sub-millimeter positional accuracy |
| Textile / packaging machines | PM 7.5°–15° | Robust, lower cost, high detent torque |
| Quartz analog clock movements | PM 180° or 360° | Only need two detents per second |
| Watch / camera lens focusing | Hybrid with microstepping | Ultra-smooth motion |
| Industrial valve actuators | Hybrid (NEMA-23/34) | High torque, holds position without brake |
| Hard-disk head positioners | Hybrid (voice-coil) | Sub-micrometer resolution |
| Robotics (joints of small arms) | Hybrid with closed-loop option | Torsional rigidity, no backlash |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive and self-contained**. No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is annotated in plain English to ensure a KTU 2024 board examiner can follow the logic without consulting external material.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle of a Multi-Stack Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider a single-stack, $m$-phase VR stepper motor. The rotor has $N_r$ identical, evenly-spaced salient teeth. The stator has $m$ phase windings, each phase wound on a set of poles whose teeth are aligned to a unique angular offset from the adjacent phase.

Let:
- $\theta_s$ = step angle (mechanical degrees per pulse)
- $m$ = number of stator phases
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth
- $N$ = total number of stable positions (steps) per revolution

### 3.1.2 Geometric Foundation
The angular pitch between two *adjacent* rotor teeth (the **rotor tooth-pitch**) is:

$$\tau_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

When a phase is energized, the rotor rotates until the *closest set* of rotor teeth aligns with the *energized* stator teeth. To advance the rotor by one step, the energized phase is switched to the *next* phase in the firing sequence.

### 3.1.3 Phase-to-Phase Displacement
For a balanced, $m$-phase stator, the teeth of successive phases are **uniformly displaced** around the air-gap. The angular displacement between two successive phases is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{360°}{m}$$

The rotor, when switching from one phase to the next, must rotate by an *integer number* of tooth pitches so that its teeth again align with the *new* phase's teeth. The number of tooth pitches advanced in a single step is exactly **one** (otherwise intermediate misalignment would cause the rotor to stall). Therefore:

$$\theta_s = 1 \times \tau_r = \frac{360°}{N_r} \quad \text{… (a)}$$

But wait — for a *multi-stack* VR stepper, the rotor is *axially sectioned* into $m$ stacks (one per phase), and each stack is **mechanically offset by $1/N_r$ of a revolution** relative to the next. The total steps per revolution thus become:

$$N = m \cdot N_r$$

Substituting $N = m N_r$ into the *general* step-angle formula $\theta_s = 360°/N$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \, N_r} \quad \text{… (b)}$$

### 3.1.4 Resolution Check
**Numerical example (KTU-favourite):** A 4-phase VR stepper with 50 rotor teeth per stack.
- Total steps per revolution: $N = 4 \times 50 = 200$
- Step angle: $\theta_s = 360° / 200 = 1.8°$
- Tooth-pitch: $\tau_r = 360° / 50 = 7.2°$
- Steps per tooth-pitch: $7.2° / 1.8° = 4$ (matches $m = 4$ ✓)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For a multi-stack VR stepper, the step angle is $\theta_s = 360°/(m N_r)$. The rotor moves by $1/(m N_r)$ of a full revolution per pulse.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

### 3.2.1 Setup
A 2-phase hybrid stepper has:
- Two rotor cups, each with $N_r$ teeth
- The two cups' teeth are **offset by 180°/$N_r$** (half a tooth-pitch) from each other
- 8 stator poles (4 per phase), each pole carries a toothed lamination
- Stator phase-A teeth and phase-B teeth are interleaved and **offset by 1/4 of the rotor tooth-pitch**

Let the rotor tooth-pitch be $\tau_r = 360°/N_r$.

### 3.2.2 Phase-A to Phase-B Step
At any given instant, the rotor is in a stable detent with phase A energized. When the controller switches to phase B:

- The rotor teeth on the *North cup* (let's say the front cup) must now align with the **B-pole** teeth.
- The B-pole teeth are physically displaced from the A-pole teeth by **$\tau_r/4$** around the air-gap (by stator design).
- Hence, the rotor must rotate by exactly $\tau_r/4$ to re-align with the new phase.

Therefore:

$$\theta_s = \frac{\tau_r}{4} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{90°}{N_r} \quad \text{… (c)}$$

### 3.2.3 Generalization
For an $m$-phase hybrid stepper, the stator phase offset is $\tau_r/(2m)$ (since $2m$ stator poles share the tooth-pitch among themselves). Thus:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \, m \, N_r} \quad \text{… (d)}$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Verification
**Standard NEMA-17 hybrid:** $m = 2$, $N_r = 50$ teeth per cup.

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \times 2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8° \quad \text{✓}$$

**High-resolution NEMA-17:** $m = 2$, $N_r = 100$ teeth per cup.

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{400} = 0.9° \quad \text{✓}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromagnetic Torque of a VR Stepper (Co-Energy Method)

### 3.3.1 Starting Point — Co-Energy
The magnetic co-energy stored in a single excited phase winding is:

$$W' = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2$$

This is valid for a *linear* magnetic medium (constant permeability) and is numerically equal to the stored energy $W$ in such systems.

### 3.3.2 The Principle of Virtual Displacement
According to the principle of virtual work, the electromagnetic torque at **constant current** is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position:

$$T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = \text{const}}$$

### 3.3.3 Differentiation
Carrying out the differentiation (treating $i$ as a constant since we are in the *constant-current* regime):

$$T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2 \right]$$

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{dL}{d\theta} \quad \text{… (e)}$$

### 3.3.4 Sinusoidal Inductance Profile
The rotor teeth periodically enter and leave alignment with the stator teeth. The inductance therefore varies quasi-sinusoidally. A standard model is:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

where $L_0$ is the average inductance and $L_1$ is the amplitude of the variation. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -N_r \, L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$$

Substituting into equation (e):

$$T_e = -\frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, N_r \, L_1 \sin(N_r \theta) \quad \text{… (f)}$$

### 3.3.5 Physical Interpretation
- **Maximum torque** occurs when $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$, i.e., $\theta = 90°/N_r$ (a *quarter tooth-pitch* from alignment).
- **Zero torque** at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 180°/N_r$ (the stable and unstable equilibrium detents).
- **Torque is proportional to $i^2$** — hence the *direction of current does not matter* in a VR stepper.
- **Sign convention:** $T_e > 0$ means *accelerating* torque; the negative sign in (f) reflects that the rotor is attracted *toward* the minimum-reluctance (maximum-inductance) position.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shaft Speed vs. Pulse Frequency

### 3.4.1 Statement
When a pulse train of frequency $f$ (pulses per second) is applied, the rotor advances by $\theta_s$ per pulse. Therefore the **angular velocity** in rad/s is:

$$\omega = f \cdot \theta_s \quad \text{(in radians per second)}$$

### 3.4.2 Conversion to RPM
To convert $\theta_s$ from degrees to radians: $\theta_s^{\text{rad}} = \theta_s \cdot \pi / 180$. Therefore:

$$\omega = f \cdot \frac{\pi \, \theta_s}{180} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

The rotational speed in revolutions per minute:

$$n = \frac{\omega \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \, f \cdot \theta_s}{360°} = \frac{60 \, f}{N} \quad \text{… (g)}$$

because $N = 360°/\theta_s$ is the total number of steps per revolution.

### 3.4.3 Inverse Relationship
Rearranging (g) to solve for the pulse frequency required to achieve a target speed $n$ (rpm):

$$f = \frac{n \, N}{60} \quad \text{[Hz]} \quad \text{… (h)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for the KTU exam:** "**Speed equals frequency divided by steps; frequency equals speed times steps divided by sixty**." ($n = f/N$, $f = nN/60$.)

### 3.4.4 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A 1.8° hybrid stepper is driven by a pulse train of frequency **$f = 500$ Hz**. Find the shaft speed in rpm and rad/s.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the resolution $N$.

$$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

Step 2: Apply equation (g).

$$n = \frac{60 \, f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 500}{200} = \frac{30000}{200} = 150 \text{ rpm}$$

Step 3: Convert to rad/s.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \, n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 150}{60} = 5\pi \approx 15.708 \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 500 Hz pulse train rotates a 1.8° hybrid stepper at **150 rpm** (≈ 15.71 rad/s).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Holding Torque in a Hybrid Stepper (Qualitative)

### 3.5.1 Physical Origin
The **holding torque** $T_h$ is the maximum *static* torque the motor can resist without losing its position when a phase is continuously energized at its rated current $I_r$.

It comprises two additive components:

$$T_h = T_{h,\text{reluctance}} + T_{h,\text{magnet}}$$

**Component 1 (reluctance part):** Following equation (f) with $\theta = 90°/N_r$ and $i = I_r$:

$$T_{h,\text{reluctance}} = \frac{1}{2} \, N_r \, L_1 \, I_r^2$$

**Component 2 (magnet part):** From the PM torque equation with $K_t$ being the motor's torque constant and $I_r$ the rated current:

$$T_{h,\text{magnet}} = K_t \, I_r$$

### 3.5.2 Combined Holding Torque

$$\boxed{T_h = \frac{1}{2} \, N_r \, L_1 \, I_r^2 + K_t \, I_r} \quad \text{… (i)}$$

### 3.5.3 Engineering Interpretation
- The **first term dominates at high currents** (it scales as $I^2$).
- The **second term dominates at low currents** (it scales linearly with $I$, but does not vanish at $I = 0$ — the *detent* torque is a manifestation of this term in the unexcited state).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often *double-count* the detent torque when computing $T_h$. The detent torque is the value of the magnet-component torque *at zero stator current*, so it is already implicit in the $K_t I_r$ term. **Do not** add an extra $T_{\text{detent}}$ on top of equation (i).
