# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid** diagrams use a **block-level functional architecture** style, mapping the physical and electrical interactions of each stepper-motor type. Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 constraints, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold/italic tags inside the labels).

---

## 4.1 Diagram A — Generic Stepper Motor Drive System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[Microcontroller or PLC]
    OSC[Pulse Generator Clock]
    TRX[Translator or Direction Logic]
    DRV[H Bridge Bipolar Driver]
    PSU[DC Power Supply 12V to 48V]
    STP[Stepper Motor VR PM or Hybrid]
    LOAD[Mechanical Load Shaft Coupling]
    SENS[Optional Encoder for Closed Loop]

    OSC --> TRX
    MCU --> TRX
    TRX --> DRV
    PSU --> DRV
    DRV --> STP
    STP --> LOAD
    SENS -.feedback.-> MCU
```

**Interpretation of the signal flow:**
- The **microcontroller** issues a STEP pulse train and a DIRection logic level.
- The **translator** (e.g., L297, TMC2209 internal logic) sequences the four H-bridge switches.
- The **H-bridge driver** delivers bi-directional current pulses to the phase windings.
- The **stepper motor** converts these pulses into discrete angular steps delivered to the **mechanical load**.
- The **optional encoder** closes the loop for *servo-stepper* (closed-loop hybrid) applications.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Classification of Stepper Motors (Topology Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Stepper Motor Families per KTU 2024 Module 1]

    ROOT --> VR[Variable Reluctance VR]
    ROOT --> PM[Permanent Magnet PM]
    ROOT --> HB[Hybrid HB]

    VR --> VR1[Rotor Soft Iron Salient Poles]
    VR --> VR2[No Magnet on Rotor]
    VR --> VR3[Torque Proportional to I squared]
    VR --> VR4[Step Angle 5 to 15 degrees]

    PM --> PM1[Rotor Axially Magnetized Permanent Magnet]
    PM --> PM2[Detent Torque Present at I zero]
    PM --> PM3[Torque Proportional to I linear]
    PM --> PM4[Step Angle 45 to 90 degrees]

    HB --> HB1[Two Rotor Cups with N_r Teeth Each]
    HB --> HB2[Axial PM Magnet Between Cups]
    HB --> HB3[Reluctance Plus Magnet Torque Additive]
    HB --> HB4[Step Angle 0.9 to 5 degrees]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Hybrid Stepper Functional Architecture (Construction Decomposition)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STATOR_ASSEMBLY
        SP_A[Stator Phase A Poles 4 teeth each]
        SP_B[Stator Phase B Poles 4 teeth each]
        SW_A[Stator Winding Phase A Bipolar]
        SW_B[Stator Winding Phase B Bipolar]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR_ASSEMBLY
        RC_N[Rotor Cup N Pole Side N_r teeth]
        RC_S[Rotor Cup S Pole Side N_r teeth offset by half pitch]
        PM_CORE[Axially Magnetized Permanent Magnet NdFeB]
    end

    subgraph SHAFT_AND_BEARING
        SH[Non Magnetic Stainless Steel Shaft]
        BRG[Ball Bearings at Both Ends]
    end

    PM_CORE --- RC_N
    PM_CORE --- RC_S
    RC_N --- SH
    RC_S --- SH
    SH --- BRG

    SW_A --> SP_A
    SW_B --> SP_B
    SP_A -.magnetic coupling.-> RC_N
    SP_B -.magnetic coupling.-> RC_S
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **stator winding** sets up a controlled magnetic field in the **stator poles**.
- The **stator poles'** teeth interact with the **rotor cups'** teeth via the air-gap flux.
- The **permanent magnet** biases both rotor cups in opposite polarities, ensuring the additive reluctance-plus-magnet torque action.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Sequencing Logic for a 2-Phase Stepper (Wave / Full-Step Mode)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Step 0 Phase A ON Phase B OFF]
    S1[Step 1 Phase A OFF Phase B ON]
    S2[Step 2 Phase A ON Phase B OFF Reverse Polarity]
    S3[Step 3 Phase A OFF Phase B ON Reverse Polarity]
    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S0
```

**Sequence interpretation:**
- Each box represents one *step* of the rotor.
- The rotor advances by $\theta_s$ per transition.
- **Direction reversal** is achieved by reversing the order in which the H-bridge switches are toggled.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Step Angle and Pulse Counter Relationship (Data Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CLOCK[Input Pulse Train Frequency f Hz]
    COUNTER[Edge Triggered Up Down Counter]
    DAC[Digital to Angle Translator theta s per pulse]
    ANGLE[Mechanical Shaft Angle theta mechanical]
    POSITION[Position equals Counter Value times theta s]

    CLOCK --> COUNTER
    COUNTER --> DAC
    DAC --> ANGLE
    ANGLE --> POSITION
```

**Engineering insight:** This block diagram captures the *open-loop positional certainty* of a stepper motor — the absolute angular position is **directly readable from the pulse counter** without any physical position sensor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks are tagged per the official 2024 scheme: Part A carries **3 marks each** (no choice), Part B carries **14 marks each** (with internal choice between OR options). Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define a stepper motor. What is meant by step angle? A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 50 teeth on each rotor cup. Calculate its step angle and resolution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stepper motor is a brushless synchronous motor that rotates in discrete angular steps, one step for each input electrical pulse, with the step magnitude determined by the motor's internal construction.

The **step angle** $\theta_s$ is the angular displacement of the rotor per input pulse, given by $\theta_s = 360°/N$, where $N$ is the total number of steps per revolution.

For a 2-phase hybrid stepper with $N_r = 50$ teeth per rotor cup:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \, m \, N_r} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

**Resolution** = $N = 200$ steps/revolution. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between the construction and working of a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor and a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | VR Stepper | PM Stepper |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotor** | Soft iron with salient teeth, *no magnet* | Axially magnetized permanent magnet, salient poles are N–S of the magnet itself |
| **Torque origin** | Tries to minimize magnetic reluctance of the flux path | Aligns its permanent N–S poles with the energized stator poles (attraction/repulsion) |
| **Torque–current** | $T \propto i^2$ (unipolar drive sufficient) | $T \propto i$ (bipolar drive required) |
| **Detent torque** | **Zero** — rotor free to rotate when unpowered | **High** — rotor resists motion even when unpowered |
| **Step angle** | $5°–15°$ (fine) | $45°–90°$ (coarse) |

**Applications:** VR — textile machines and packaging-line actuators; PM — quartz analog clocks and instrument panel drives. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE convention, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module-section. The two questions below are **structurally independent** and represent a typical *internal choice* pattern.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Variable Reluctance Stepper Deep-Dive*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** *With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a multi-stack Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor. Derive the expression for its step angle in terms of the number of rotor teeth $N_r$ and the number of phases $m$. **\[7 Marks\]***

**(b)** *A 3-stack, 3-phase VR stepper motor has 40 teeth on each rotor stack. The motor is driven by a pulse train of frequency 240 Hz. Compute:* **(i)** *the step angle, **(ii)** *the resolution, **(iii)** *the shaft speed in rpm and rad/s, and **(iv)** *the number of pulses required to rotate the shaft by 1080°. **\[7 Marks\]***

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Construction (Block Diagram Refer Section 4.3):** A multi-stack VR stepper has:
- A common shaft on which $m$ identical rotor stacks are mounted.
- Each stack has $N_r$ salient teeth on a soft-iron lamination.
- Each rotor stack is **mechanically displaced by $360°/(m N_r)$** from the previous one.
- A corresponding stator stack (with phase winding) surrounds each rotor stack.
- The phase windings are excited in a cyclic sequence (A → B → C → A … for $m = 3$).

**Working Principle:**
- When phase A is energized, the rotor aligns such that the **A-stack rotor teeth are in minimum-reluctance position** with the A-stator teeth.
- The energy in the magnetic field is $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L_A(\theta) i_A^2$, and the system seeks the **minimum energy** configuration.
- Switching to phase B causes the rotor to rotate by **one tooth-pitch on the B-stack**, which corresponds to a mechanical displacement of $360°/N$ in the shaft.
- Repeating the sequence A → B → C → A produces continuous rotation in steps.

**Step Angle Derivation:**
- The rotor tooth-pitch is $\tau_r = 360°/N_r$.
- With $m$ stacks, each successive phase advances the rotor by **$1/N_r$ revolution per stack**, and there are $m$ stacks.

$$\therefore \; \theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \, N_r} \quad \text{(derived in Section 3.1, equation b)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:** 3-stack ($s = 3$), 3-phase ($m = 3$) VR stepper, $N_r = 40$ teeth per stack, pulse frequency $f = 240$ Hz.

**(i) Step angle:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{s \, m \, N_r} = \frac{360°}{3 \times 3 \times 40} = \frac{360°}{360} = 1°/\text{pulse}$$

**(ii) Resolution (steps per revolution):**

$$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{1°} = 400 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**(iii) Shaft speed:**

$$n = \frac{60 \, f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 240}{400} = \frac{14400}{400} = 36 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 36}{60} = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iv) Pulses to rotate by 1080°:**

$$1080° = 3 \times 360° = 3 \text{ full revolutions}$$

$$\text{Pulses} = 3 \times N = 3 \times 400 = 1200 \text{ pulses}$$

> **Answer summary:** (i) $1°$ (ii) 400 steps/rev (iii) 36 rpm / 3.77 rad/s (iv) 1200 pulses. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Hybrid Stepper Mastery*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** *Explain the construction and working principle of a hybrid stepper motor with the help of a labeled functional block diagram. **\[7 Marks\]***

**(b)** *Compare VR, PM, and hybrid stepper motors on the basis of (i) step angle, (ii) detent torque, (iii) drive circuit (unipolar / bipolar), and (iv) typical applications. A NEMA-17 hybrid stepper has 50 teeth per rotor cup. Calculate its step angle and the pulse frequency required to achieve a shaft speed of 90 rpm. **\[7 Marks\]***

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Construction (Refer Section 4.3 Diagram C):**
A hybrid stepper consists of:
1. **Two rotor cups** made of laminated silicon steel, each carrying $N_r$ fine teeth. The cups are mounted on a non-magnetic shaft with their teeth **offset by half a tooth-pitch** (180°/$N_r$).
2. An **axially magnetized cylindrical permanent magnet** (typically Nd-Fe-B) sandwiched between the two rotor cups. The magnet polarizes one cup as N and the other as S.
3. A **stator** with $2m$ poles (typically 8 for $m=2$ phases), each pole carrying a toothed lamination. The phase-A and phase-B stator teeth are **interleaved** and **offset by 1/4 of the rotor tooth-pitch**.
4. **Two phase windings** (A and B), each wound on alternate stator poles, requiring a **bipolar H-bridge drive**.

**Working Principle:**
- When phase A is energized, the A-stator teeth attract the rotor teeth on the N-cup and repel those on the S-cup (or vice versa, depending on polarity), producing a strong *alignment* torque.
- The rotor settles at the position where the A-stator teeth are perfectly aligned with the N-cup rotor teeth.
- Switching to phase B causes the rotor to rotate by **one quarter of a tooth-pitch** $\tau_r/4$ to align with the B-stator teeth.
- The step angle is therefore $\theta_s = \tau_r/4 = 90°/N_r$.

**Why hybrid is superior:** The torque is the **sum** of a reluctance component (proportional to $i^2$) and a magnet component (proportional to $i$), yielding **2× to 3× higher holding torque** than a comparably-sized VR or PM motor, and step resolutions down to **0.9°**.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Step angle** | 5° to 15° | 45° to 90° | **0.9° to 5°** (finest) |
| **(ii) Detent torque** | **Zero** | **High** (residual PM torque) | **Medium** (controlled residual) |
| **(iii) Drive circuit** | Unipolar (single-polarity switching) | Bipolar (H-bridge) | **Bipolar** (H-bridge, often with microstepping) |
| **(iv) Applications** | Textile, packaging, low-cost automation | Clocks, instruments, printers | **3D printers, CNC, robotics, biomedical** |

**Calculation (3 marks):** Given $N_r = 50$, 2-phase hybrid.

**Step angle:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \, m \, N_r} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

**Pulse frequency for 90 rpm:**

$$N = 360°/\theta_s = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

$$f = \frac{n \, N}{60} = \frac{90 \times 200}{60} = \frac{18000}{60} = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

> **Answer summary:** $\theta_s = 1.8°$, $f = 300$ Hz. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Wrong step-angle formula** — Mixing up $\theta_s = 360°/(N_r)$ (single-stack, one-phase at a time) with $\theta_s = 360°/(m N_r)$ (multi-stack). The KTU paper will explicitly state *multi-stack* or *single-stack*. **Read the question carefully.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for hybrid** — The hybrid stepper formula has a factor of **$2 \, m$** in the denominator, not just $m$. Using $360°/(m N_r) = 3.6°$ instead of $1.8°$ for a NEMA-17 loses all 7 marks of the sub-question.
>
> 3. **Unit-mismatch in speed conversion** — When asked for shaft speed in *both* rpm and rad/s, students often give rpm only. KTU strictly awards the 1-mark unit-conversion sub-step separately.
>
> 4. **Sign error in torque equation** — Writing $T_e = +\tfrac{1}{2} i^2 (dL/d\theta)$ instead of the derivative form $\partial W'/\partial \theta$. The negative sign in $-\tfrac{1}{2} N_r L_1 i^2 \sin(N_r \theta)$ reflects the *attractive* nature of the minimum-reluctance torque. A ½-mark deduction is typical for sign error.
>
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram** — A *neat, labeled* functional block diagram is mandatory for full marks in the 7-mark construction question. An unlabeled diagram fetches at most 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing $p$ (pole pairs) with $P$ (poles)** — In the PM stepper formula $\theta_s = 360°/(2p \cdot m)$, the factor $2p$ is the *number of poles*. Writing $2P$ where $P = 2p$ is an error costing 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Skipping the co-energy derivation in the 7-mark derivation question** — Direct use of the formula $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 dL/d\theta$ without *deriving* it from $W' = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ and the principle of virtual work fetches only 2 of the 4 derivation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Read this section **the night before the exam** for a 5-minute consolidated revision.

- ✅ A **stepper motor** is a *pulse-driven*, *open-loop positionable*, *brushless* synchronous motor.
- ✅ **Three families:** VR (no magnet), PM (magnet only), Hybrid (salient poles *plus* magnet).
- ✅ **Step angle (general):** $\theta_s = 360°/N$, where $N$ = steps per revolution.
- ✅ **VR step angle:** $\theta_s = 360°/(m N_r)$ for single-stack; with $s$ stacks: $\theta_s = 360°/(s m N_r)$.
- ✅ **PM step angle:** $\theta_s = 360°/(2 p \, m)$, where $p$ = pole pairs on PM rotor.
- ✅ **Hybrid step angle (2-cup, 2-phase):** $\theta_s = 360°/(4 N_r) = 90°/N_r$.
- ✅ **NEMA-17 standard hybrid:** $N_r = 50$ → $\theta_s = 1.8°$ → **200 steps/rev**.
- ✅ **Shaft speed (rpm):** $n = 60 f / N$. **Shaft speed (rad/s):** $\omega = 2\pi f / N$.
- ✅ **Holding torque** of a hybrid: $T_h = \tfrac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I_r^2 + K_t I_r$ (reluctance + magnet components add).
- ✅ **VR torque:** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 dL/d\theta \propto i^2$ → **unipolar** drive OK.
- ✅ **PM torque:** $T_e = K_t i \sin\theta \propto i$ → **bipolar H-bridge** drive needed.
- ✅ **Detent torque:** *zero* for VR, *high* for PM, *medium-controllable* for hybrid.
- ✅ **Hybrid's superiority** stems from the *additive* nature of reluctance torque and magnet torque.
- ✅ **Microstepping** (1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/256) is a *drive-level* technique, not a motor-level feature — most common in hybrid steppers.
- ✅ **Maximum step rate (slew rate):** Typically 1000–10000 pulses/s; beyond this, torque drops sharply due to winding inductance.
- ✅ **Pull-out torque** decreases with stepping frequency — critical for high-speed applications.
- ✅ **Constant of nature to memorize:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- ✅ **KTU-typical exam numbers:** 1.8°, 0.9°, 200 steps/rev, 50 teeth/cup, 2 phases, 1.5 A rated current, 200–500 Hz pulse frequency.
- ✅ **Diagram discipline:** Always draw the functional block diagram in 7-mark construction questions — and **label every block**.

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Stepper Motor (VR, PM, Hybrid).**
> *Wishing you the very best for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BLDC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BLDC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC):** A *Brushless Direct Current* motor is a synchronous electric machine in which the rotor consists of **permanent magnets** and the stator carries **polyphase armature windings**. Electronic commutation, performed by an inverter driven by rotor-position feedback, replaces the mechanical brush-commutator arrangement of a conventional DC motor. The motor produces a **trapezoidal back-EMF** waveform and is supplied with quasi-rectangular stator currents, behaving externally as a DC machine while being internally an AC synchronous machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Nomenclature Watch:** A BLDC is technically a *permanent magnet synchronous machine (PMSM)* with **trapezoidal** back-EMF. The sinusoidal-BEMF counterpart is the **PMSM** proper. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats them as **distinct machines** because of their different torque ripple behaviour and control strategies.

**Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**

- **Number of pole pairs (P):** Typically **4, 6, 8, 10** in commercial BLDCs.
- **Supply voltage:** Common ratings are **12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 310 V DC bus**.
- **Speed range:** Up to **100,000 rpm** (small sizes); 1500–6000 rpm for industrial sizes.
- **Efficiency:** Typically **85–95 %**, far above brushed DC motors (**75–80 %**).
- **Power density:** **3–5 kW/kg** for high-end BLDCs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Compass with a Smart Choreographer"

Imagine a **magnetic compass needle** (the rotor magnet) placed inside a circular room whose walls have **three doors** (the three stator phases). A **choreographer** (the controller) watches the needle through a camera (Hall sensor) and shouts directions: *"Door A — push the needle north! Door B — now push east! Door C — now push south!"* 

Each shout creates a **rotating magnetic field** in the room, and the compass needle obediently chases the strongest push. Because the choreographer is **always one step ahead** of the needle, the needle keeps spinning smoothly. The choreographer is the **inverter + controller**, the doors are the **stator windings**, and the camera is the **Hall-effect rotor position sensor**. No brushes are needed because the choreographer does the switching that mechanical brushes used to do in an old brushed DC motor.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "brush-LESS"?** In a brushed DC motor, the **brushes + commutator** mechanically switch the current direction in the rotor windings. In a BLDC, the rotor is a permanent magnet (no winding, no commutator, no brushes). Switching is done **electronically** in the stator, which is why BLDCs last far longer, run quieter, and need almost zero maintenance.

---

## 1.3 Why BLDC for KTU 2024?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBRT502, Module 1):** BLDC motors are covered because they bridge **Power Electronics**, **Control Systems**, and **Electrical Machines** — three pillars of an electrical-drives course. KTU examiners frequently test:
> 1. Constructional differences vs. brushed DC and induction motors.
> 2. The role of the **six-step (trapezoidal) commutation** sequence.
> 3. **EMF and torque equations** with numerical substitution.
> 4. **Closed-loop speed control** using PI controllers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture

| Component | Description | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Stator (Armature)** | Laminated silicon-steel core with **three-phase** concentrated/distributed windings (A, B, C) | Produces rotating magnetic field when electronically commutated |
| **Rotor** | **Surface-mounted** or **interior** permanent magnets (NdFeB / SmCo / Ferrite) | Provides constant excitation flux; no winding |
| **Position Sensors** | **3 Hall-effect sensors** placed 120° electrical apart (or encoders/resolvers for sinusoidal) | Feed rotor angle to controller for commutation logic |
| **Inverter** | **3-phase bridge** with 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs (Q1–Q6) | Performs electronic commutation |
| **Controller** | Microcontroller / DSP / FPGA | Generates gate pulses based on Hall sensor inputs |

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The BLDC operates on the **Lorentz force principle**: a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force **F = BIL sin θ**. By sequentially energising stator phase pairs, a **rotating stator field** is generated that "drags" the permanent-magnet rotor with it.

**Step 1 — Rotor Position Sensing**
The three Hall sensors detect the polarity of the rotor magnet. The 3-bit Hall code (e.g., 101, 100, 110, 010, 011, 001) changes **six times per electrical revolution**, defining **six commutation sectors** of **60° electrical** each.

**Step 2 — Electronic Commutation**
For each 60° sector, **two of the three** phases are energised (one from the positive DC bus, one from the negative bus); the third phase is left floating. The energised pair is chosen such that the resulting stator MMF vector is always **90° ahead** of the rotor magnet axis, producing **maximum positive torque**.

**Step 3 — Trapezoidal Back-EMF and Current**
Because the rotor flux distribution is shaped (often using **arc-magnet** or **concentrated winding** design), the phase back-EMF is **trapezoidal**. Quasi-square-wave currents of **120° electrical** duration are injected — this is the defining feature of **trapezoidal BLDC** control.

**Step 4 — Continuous Rotation**
As the rotor turns, the Hall code updates, the controller commands the next inverter leg pair, and the stator field "leaps" to the next 60° sector. The rotor chases the field, producing smooth continuous torque.

---

## 2.3 EMF and Torque Equations — The Physics

The instantaneous phase EMF for a trapezoidal BLDC is:

$$E_{ph} = k_e \, \omega_m \, f_{trapezoid}(\theta)$$

where **f_trapezoid(θ)** is the trapezoidal waveform of normalised amplitude ±1 across the 120° conduction band.

The **average EMF constant** $k_e$ (V·s/rad) and **torque constant** $k_t$ (N·m/A) satisfy the SI-unit identity:

$$k_t = k_e$$

This is a direct consequence of energy conservation (the SI form of the machine constant, often quoted as $K_b = K_t$ when units are consistent).

The developed electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_e = k_t \, I_{ph}$$

For a 3-phase machine with two phases conducting at any instant:

$$T_e = 2 \, k_t \, I_{dc}$$

because the DC-link current $I_{dc}$ equals the phase current $I_{ph}$ (current flows from the DC bus through one phase and back through another).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the table below. Most KTU numerical problems in this module use **2–3 of these equations** in sequence.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase Back-EMF | $E_{ph} = k_e \, \omega_m$ | V (peak) | $\omega_m$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Torque | $T_e = k_t \, I_{ph}$ | N·m | $k_t = k_e$ in SI |
| 3 | Torque (2-phase conduction) | $T_e = 2 \, k_t \, I_{dc}$ | N·m | DC-link current form |
| 4 | DC Voltage Equation | $V_{dc} = 2 \, E_{ph} + 2 \, I_{ph} \, R_{ph} + 2 \, V_{sw}$ | V | 2 switches + 2 phases in series |
| 5 | No-load Speed | $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_{dc} - 2V_{sw}}{k_e}$ | rad/s | $I_{ph} \rightarrow 0$ |
| 6 | Rated Speed | $\omega_r = \dfrac{V_{dc} - 2V_{sw} - 2 I_{rated} R_{ph}}{k_e}$ | rad/s | Full-load |
| 7 | Mechanical Output | $P_{out} = T_e \, \omega_m$ | W | Useful shaft power |
| 8 | Electrical Input | $P_{in} = V_{dc} \, I_{dc}$ | W | DC side |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | |
| 10 | Pole-pair EMF (generalised) | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{P \, N \, \Phi \, Z}{60 \, A}$ | V | P=poles, N=rpm, Φ=Wb, Z=conductors, A=parallel paths |
| 11 | Electrical to mechanical freq. | $f_e = \dfrac{P \, N}{120}$ | Hz | $N$ in rpm |
| 12 | Commutation Sector | $\theta_{sector} = \dfrac{360°}{6 \, P}$ | electrical ° | 60° electrical per sector |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical rule for KTU:** The constants $k_e$ and $k_t$ are numerically equal **only in SI units**. If the problem gives $k_e$ in V/krpm and $k_t$ in N·m/A, you must convert before equating.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

BLDCs are the **workhorse of modern motion control**:

- **EV traction** (e.g., Tesla Model 3 rear unit — permanent-magnet synchronous variant).
- **Aerospace actuators and flight-control surface motors**.
- **HVAC blowers, washing-machine drums, ceiling fans** (energy-efficient BLDC variants).
- **CNC spindle drives, robotic joints**.
- **Drones and quadcopters** (high power-to-weight ratio).
- **Hard-disk drives, CD/DVD spindles** (early commercial BLDC application).

The same equations above power **everything from a 5 W drone motor to a 250 kW EV traction machine** — only the geometry and cooling scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Back-EMF Constant from First Principles

### Starting Point — Faraday's Law for a Rotating Coil

The instantaneous EMF induced in a single conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ through a flux density $B$ is:

$$e = B \, l \, v$$

For a rotor rotating at angular velocity $\omega_m$ at mean radius $r$:

$$v = \omega_m \, r$$

Therefore for **one conductor**:

$$e_{cond} = B \, l \, \omega_m \, r$$

### Step 1 — Sum Over All Series Conductors Per Phase

If a phase has $Z_{ph}$ series conductors, and only a fraction $f_{w}$ of them are simultaneously under the magnet arc (the **winding factor**, combining distribution $k_d$ and pitch $k_p$, with typical value 0.9–0.95 for concentrated windings), the total phase EMF (peak) becomes:

$$E_{ph,peak} = B \, l \, r \, \omega_m \, Z_{ph} \, f_{w}$$

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Flux Per Pole

The flux per pole is $\Phi = B \, A_{pole} = B \, l \, \tau$, where $\tau$ is the pole pitch. Substituting $B \, l = \Phi / \tau$:

$$E_{ph,peak} = \frac{\Phi}{\tau} \, r \, \omega_m \, Z_{ph} \, f_{w}$$

### Step 3 — Use the Geometric Relation $r / \tau = 1 / (2 \pi)$ (for a 2-pole machine)

For a $P$-pole machine, the pole pitch in mechanical radians is $\tau = 2 \pi r / P$, so $r / \tau = P / (2 \pi)$:

$$E_{ph,peak} = \frac{\Phi \, P \, \omega_m \, Z_{ph} \, f_{w}}{2 \pi}$$

### Step 4 — Group Constants into $k_e$

Defining the EMF constant:

$$k_e = \frac{\Phi \, P \, Z_{ph} \, f_{w}}{2 \pi}$$

We obtain the clean expression:

$$\boxed{E_{ph,peak} = k_e \, \omega_m}$$

The mechanical angular velocity in rad/s is related to the synchronous electrical frequency $\omega_e$ by $\omega_e = (P/2) \, \omega_m$, so equivalently:

$$E_{ph,peak} = \frac{2 k_e}{P} \, \omega_e$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Constant from Energy Balance

### Step 1 — Power Conversion Identity

In a lossless motor, **electrical input mechanical power output**:

$$P_{elec} = P_{mech}$$

$$e \, i = T_e \, \omega_m$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Phase EMF

For one phase:

$$(k_e \, \omega_m) \, I_{ph} = T_{e,ph} \, \omega_m$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $\omega_m$ (assuming non-zero speed)

$$T_{e,ph} = k_e \, I_{ph}$$

### Step 4 — Identify $k_t = k_e$

$$\boxed{T_{e,ph} = k_t \, I_{ph}, \quad k_t = k_e}$$

The result is **fundamental**: the same constant that converts speed to EMF also converts current to torque. This is the **electromechanical reciprocity principle**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Voltage Equation and Speed-Torque Characteristic

### Step 1 — KVL Around the DC Bus → Inverter → 2 Phases

During each 60° sector, two phases conduct, each with resistance $R_{ph}$. Two switches (one high-side, one low-side) carry the current, each with a forward drop $V_{sw}$:

$$V_{dc} = V_{sw,Q1} + I_{ph} R_{ph} + E_{ph,1} + E_{ph,2} + I_{ph} R_{ph} + V_{sw,Q2}$$

### Step 2 — Recognise That the Two Phases Contribute Equal and Opposing EMFs in the Loop, and Series Resistance Doubles

For a balanced BLDC, the two active phase EMFs are equal in magnitude, so the **net series EMF** in the DC loop is $2 E_{ph,peak}$:

$$V_{dc} = 2 E_{ph,peak} + 2 I_{ph} R_{ph} + 2 V_{sw}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $E_{ph,peak} = k_e \omega_m$ and $I_{ph} = T_e / k_t$

$$V_{dc} = 2 k_e \omega_m + 2 \frac{R_{ph}}{k_t} T_e + 2 V_{sw}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\omega_m$ as a Function of $T_e$

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}}{2 k_e} - \frac{R_{ph}}{k_e k_t} T_e}$$

This is the **linear speed-torque characteristic** of a BLDC — analogous to a DC shunt motor's straight line, with slope $-R_{ph}/(k_e k_t)$.

### Step 5 — No-Load Speed (Set $T_e = 0$)

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}}{2 k_e}$$

### Step 6 — Starting / Stall Torque (Set $\omega_m = 0$)

$$T_{stall} = \frac{(V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}) \, k_t}{2 R_{ph}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Variant)

**Problem:**
A 4-pole BLDC motor has $k_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, phase resistance $R_{ph} = 0.5 \, \Omega$, and is supplied from $V_{dc} = 48$ V through an inverter with switch drop $V_{sw} = 1$ V. Calculate (a) no-load speed in rpm, (b) speed at $T_e = 1.5$ N·m, and (c) starting current.

**Solution (a):**

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{48 - 2(1)}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{46}{0.10} = 460 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{nl} = \omega_{nl} \times \frac{60}{2 \pi} = 460 \times 9.5493 = 4392.7 \text{ rpm}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\omega = 460 - \frac{0.5}{0.05 \times 0.05} \times 1.5 = 460 - \frac{0.5}{0.0025} \times 1.5 = 460 - 300 = 160 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N = 160 \times 9.5493 = 1527.9 \text{ rpm}$$

**Solution (c):** At stall, $\omega = 0$, so:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}}{2 R_{ph}} = \frac{48 - 2}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{46}{1.0} = 46 \text{ A}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner pattern:** KTU numericals almost always expect answers in **SI units throughout**, with the final rpm conversion done in the **last step**. Do not mix N·m with N·cm or rad/s with rpm mid-calculation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trapezoidal vs. Sinusoidal BLDC Control

| Parameter | Trapezoidal (BLDC proper) | Sinusoidal (PMSM) |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF shape | Trapezoidal (flat top 120°) | Sinusoidal |
| Current waveform | Quasi-square, 120° conduction | Sinusoidal, 180° conduction |
| Switching events / electrical cycle | **6** (one per sector) | Continuous PWM |
| Torque ripple | High (≈ 13–15 %) | Low (≈ 1–3 %) |
| Sensor requirement | 3 Hall sensors (low resolution) | Resolver / encoder (high resolution) |
| Controller complexity | Simple 6-step logic | Field-Oriented Control (FOC) |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** (this topic) | Moderate (covered later) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: BLDC Drive Power & Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC["DC BUS Vdc"] --> INV["3 PHASE VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5 Q6"]
    INV --> MA["MOTOR PHASE A"]
    INV --> MB["MOTOR PHASE B"]
    INV --> MC["MOTOR PHASE C"]
    MA --> RTM["BLDC ROTOR PERMANENT MAGNET"]
    MB --> RTM
    MC --> RTM
    RTM --> SH["SHAFT LOAD"]
    RTM -. rotor position .-> H1["HALL SENSOR 1"]
    RTM -. rotor position .-> H2["HALL SENSOR 2"]
    RTM -. rotor position .-> H3["HALL SENSOR 3"]
    H1 --> DEC["HALL SIGNAL DECODER 3 BIT CODE"]
    H2 --> DEC
    H3 --> DEC
    DEC --> CTRL["CONTROL LOGIC COMMUTATION TABLE"]
    CTRL --> GPW["GATE PULSE GENERATOR PWM"]
    REF["SPEED REFERENCE Nref"] --> PI["PI CONTROLLER"]
    TAC["TACHOSENSOR ACTUAL SPEED"] --> PI
    PI --> GPW
    GPW --> INV
    GPW -. gate pulses .-> INV
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- **Solid arrows (-->):** Power flow from DC bus → inverter → 3 phases → rotor → shaft.
- **Dotted arrows (.->):** Feedback signals (rotor position and actual speed).
- **PI block:** Compares $N_{ref}$ with measured $N_{act}$ and produces the duty-cycle command.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Six-Step Commutation Sequence State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1
    S1["SECTOR 1 HA HIGH HB LOW Q1 Q4 ON PHASE A to PHASE B"] --> S2
    S2["SECTOR 2 HA HIGH HC LOW Q1 Q6 ON PHASE A to PHASE C"] --> S3
    S3["SECTOR 3 HB HIGH HC LOW Q3 Q6 ON PHASE B to PHASE C"] --> S4
    S4["SECTOR 4 HB HIGH HA LOW Q3 Q2 ON PHASE B to PHASE A"] --> S5
    S5["SECTOR 5 HC HIGH HA LOW Q5 Q2 ON PHASE C to PHASE A"] --> S6
    S6["SECTOR 6 HC HIGH HB LOW Q5 Q4 ON PHASE C to PHASE B"] --> S1
    S6 --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Commutation key for KTU:** Each state transitions to the next based on a change in the 3-bit Hall code. The 6 states cycle in a fixed sequence as the rotor turns one electrical revolution. There are $P$ electrical revolutions per mechanical revolution, so the sequence repeats $P$ times per shaft turn.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: BLDC Within the KTU Drives Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DRV["ELECTRICAL DRIVE SYSTEM"] --> M["MOTOR BLDC PMSM IM SRM DC"]
    M --> BLDC["BLDC TRAPEZOIDAL THIS MODULE"]
    BLDC --> CON["CONSTRUCTION STATOR WINDING ROTOR PM SENSORS"]
    BLDC --> WORK["WORKING 6 STEP COMMUTATION"]
    BLDC --> EMF["EMF EQUATION E = ke omega"]
    BLDC --> TRQ["TORQUE EQUATION T = kt I"]
    BLDC --> CTL["CONTROL OPEN LOOP CLOSED LOOP PI FOC"]
    BLDC --> APP["APPLICATIONS EV DRONE HVAC CNC FAN"]
    CTL --> PWM["PWM TECHNIQUES"]
    CTL --> FB["HALL SENSOR FEEDBACK"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of BLDC motors over conventional brushed DC motors.**

**Model Answer (3 points × 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher efficiency (85–95 %)** because there are no brush and commutator losses, and the rotor has no winding copper loss.
2. **Longer life and lower maintenance** — no brushes to wear out, so the motor can run 10,000+ hours vs. 1,000–3,000 hours for brushed DC.
3. **Better speed range and thermal management** — rotor inertia is much lower (only magnets), and heat is dissipated from the stator (where the windings are), allowing simpler cooling.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, KTU expects **three crisp points** with at least one numeric example or value to score full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of Hall sensors in a BLDC motor?**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. The three **Hall-effect sensors** mounted on the stator at 120° electrical intervals detect the **rotor magnet polarity** as the shaft rotates.
2. They generate a **3-bit digital code (Ha, Hb, Hc)** that updates six times per electrical revolution, signalling the **rotor position** to the controller.
3. The controller decodes this code and energises the **appropriate pair of inverter switches** to produce a stator magnetic field that is always 90° ahead of the rotor — enabling **electronic commutation** that replaces mechanical brushes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Brushless DC motor**. Discuss the role of the three-phase inverter and Hall sensors in electronic commutation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core carrying a **three-phase concentrated winding** (phases A, B, C). Slots house the windings. The stator generates the rotating magnetic field.
- **Rotor:** Made of **permanent magnets** (typically **NdFeB**) mounted on the surface (surface-mounted PMSM) or buried inside (interior PMSM). The rotor has no windings, no commutator, and no brushes.
- **Sensors:** **Three Hall-effect sensors** mounted 120° electrical apart on the stator, producing a 3-bit code reflecting rotor position.
- **Inverter:** A **three-phase bridge inverter** with **6 MOSFETs / IGBTs (Q1–Q6)** — Q1, Q3, Q5 are high-side; Q2, Q4, Q6 are low-side.

**Working (4 marks):**

1. The Hall sensors detect the rotor angle and feed a **3-bit code** to the controller.
2. For each of the **six 60° electrical sectors**, the controller commands a specific pair of switches ON — one high-side and one low-side — such that the **stator MMF vector** is 90° ahead of the rotor magnet.
3. The energised phase pair creates a magnetic field that **drags the rotor** in the direction of field rotation (Lorentz force on stator current × rotor flux).
4. As the rotor advances, the Hall code changes, the next switch pair turns ON, and the field "jumps" to the next sector. The rotor tracks the rotating field at synchronous speed, producing **smooth continuous torque** with **trapezoidal back-EMF**.

**Q.A. (b)** A 6-pole BLDC motor has a **back-EMF constant $k_e = 0.08$ V·s/rad**, phase resistance **$R_{ph} = 0.4 \, \Omega$**, and runs from a **$V_{dc} = 60$ V** supply with switch drop $V_{sw} = 1.5$ V. Calculate: (i) the no-load speed in rpm, (ii) the speed at a load torque of **2 N·m**, and (iii) the starting current. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always convert the formula for 2-phase conduction explicitly — the KTU examiner checks this.

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}}{2 k_e} = \frac{60 - 2(1.5)}{2 \times 0.08} = \frac{57}{0.16} = 356.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{nl} = 356.25 \times \frac{60}{2 \pi} = 3402.7 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Speed at $T_e = 2$ N·m:**

Using $\omega = \omega_{nl} - \dfrac{R_{ph}}{k_e k_t} T_e$ with $k_t = k_e = 0.08$:

$$\omega = 356.25 - \frac{0.4}{0.08 \times 0.08} \times 2 = 356.25 - \frac{0.4}{0.0064} \times 2 = 356.25 - 125 = 231.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N = 231.25 \times 9.5493 = 2208.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**(iii) Starting (stall) current:**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc} - 2 V_{sw}}{2 R_{ph}} = \frac{60 - 3}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{57}{0.8} = 71.25 \text{ A}$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B. (a)** Derive the **EMF equation and torque equation of a BLDC motor** from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**EMF Equation Derivation (4 marks):**

- **Assumption 1:** The flux distribution in the air gap is rectangular (flat-topped), giving a trapezoidal back-EMF.
- **Assumption 2:** The rotor magnets are surface-mounted, with a winding factor $f_w$ accounting for non-ideal pitch/distribution.
- The instantaneous EMF in a single conductor of length $l$ moving at velocity $v = \omega_m r$ in flux density $B$ is $e = B l \omega_m r$.
- Integrating over $Z_{ph}$ series conductors per phase under the magnet arc, and substituting the flux per pole $\Phi = B \, l \, \tau$ and the geometric ratio $r/\tau = P/(2 \pi)$:

$$E_{ph,peak} = \frac{\Phi \, P \, Z_{ph} \, f_w}{2 \pi} \, \omega_m = k_e \, \omega_m$$

**Torque Equation Derivation (3 marks):**

- From energy conservation: $P_{elec} = P_{mech} \Rightarrow e \, i = T_e \, \omega_m$.
- Substituting $e = k_e \omega_m$ and $i = I_{ph}$:

$$k_e \, \omega_m \, I_{ph} = T_e \, \omega_m \Rightarrow T_e = k_e \, I_{ph} = k_t \, I_{ph}$$

Hence the **electromechanical reciprocity** $k_t = k_e$ in SI units.

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the **six-step (trapezoidal) commutation sequence** of a BLDC motor with reference to a state table. How does this sequence differ from the commutation in a brushed DC motor? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Six-Step Commutation (5 marks):**

The Hall sensor outputs (Ha, Hb, Hc) form a 3-bit Gray code. As the rotor turns one electrical revolution, six distinct 60° sectors are defined. In each sector, exactly **two of the three phases are energised** (one from the +DC bus, one to the −DC bus) such that the stator MMF vector leads the rotor by 90° electrical.

| Sector | Hall Code (Ha Hb Hc) | Active Switches | Phases ON | Current Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 0 1 | Q1, Q4 | A, B | +DC → A → B → −DC |
| 2 | 1 0 0 | Q1, Q6 | A, C | +DC → A → C → −DC |
| 3 | 0 1 0 | Q3, Q6 | B, C | +DC → B → C → −DC |
| 4 | 0 1 1 | Q3, Q2 | B, A | +DC → B → A → −DC |
| 5 | 0 0 1 | Q5, Q2 | C, A | +DC → C → A → −DC |
| 6 | 1 1 0 | Q5, Q4 | C, B | +DC → C → B → −DC |

**Difference from Brushed DC (2 marks):**

- In a **brushed DC motor**, commutation is **mechanical**: brushes physically touch commutator segments and switch the rotor winding current. Switching is **automatic** but **lossy** (brush drop, sparking, wear).
- In a **BLDC**, commutation is **electronic**: a **microcontroller/DSP** reads the Hall code and triggers the **inverter switches** in software. There is **no physical contact**, so there are no brush drops, no sparking, and **much higher reliability** and lifespan.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the DC voltage equation.** Many students write $V_{dc} = E_{ph} + I R_{ph}$ instead of $V_{dc} = 2 E_{ph} + 2 I_{ph} R_{ph}$. KTU has a "2-phase conduction" rule that examiners test. **Lose 1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $k_e$ and $k_t$ units.** $k_e$ in V/krpm is **NOT** equal to $k_t$ in N·m/A numerically — convert to V·s/rad and N·m/A respectively.
> 3. **Skipping the Hall-sensor explanation** in construction questions. KTU's Module 1 specifically tests "electronic commutation"; without Hall sensors, the answer is incomplete.
> 4. **Not stating "6 sectors × 60° = 360° electrical"** explicitly. Examiners award marks for the *sequence*, not just the term.
> 5. **Mixing BLDC and PMSM terminology.** The KTU 2024 syllabus treats them as distinct. Trapezoidal EMF = BLDC; Sinusoidal EMF = PMSM.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for the night before the exam):**

- [ ] **BLDC = inside-out DC motor:** rotor is PM, stator is wound, commutation is **electronic** via 6-switch inverter.
- [ ] **Six-step commutation, 60° sectors**, two phases ON at any time, MMF vector 90° ahead of rotor.
- [ ] **Hall sensors (3, 120° apart)** give the 3-bit rotor-position code to the controller.
- [ ] **Back-EMF:** $E_{ph} = k_e \, \omega_m$ (peak, trapezoidal, 120° flat-top).
- [ ] **Torque:** $T_e = k_t \, I_{ph}$, and in 2-phase conduction $T_e = 2 k_t I_{dc}$.
- [ ] **Electromechanical reciprocity:** $k_t = k_e$ in SI units.
- [ ] **DC voltage equation:** $V_{dc} = 2 E_{ph} + 2 I_{ph} R_{ph} + 2 V_{sw}$ (note the **2s**).
- [ ] **Speed-torque line:** $\omega_m = \omega_{nl} - \dfrac{R_{ph}}{k_e k_t} T_e$ — **linear**, like a DC shunt motor.
- [ ] **No-load speed:** $\omega_{nl} = (V_{dc} - 2V_{sw}) / (2 k_e)$.
- [ ] **Stall / starting current:** $I_{stall} = (V_{dc} - 2V_{sw}) / (2 R_{ph})$.
- [ ] **Conversion:** $\omega$ (rad/s) → $N$ (rpm) by $N = \omega \cdot 60 / (2\pi)$.
- [ ] **Trapezoidal vs. Sinusoidal:** BLDC = trapezoidal EMF + quasi-square current; PMSM = sinusoidal EMF + sinusoidal current.
- [ ] **Advantages:** high efficiency, long life, low maintenance, high power density, silent operation.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** higher cost (electronics + magnets), more complex controller, sensor failure risk.
- [ ] **Applications:** EV traction, drones, HVAC fans, washing machines, CNC spindles, hard-disk drives.
- [ ] **Pole pairs:** $f_e = P N / 120$; each electrical revolution = $360°/P$ mechanical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ DC-DC CONVERTERS (CHOPPERS) — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC-DC Converter (Chopper):** A DC-DC converter, popularly known as a **Chopper**, is a static power electronic device/circuit that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage directly. It employs high-frequency **forced-commutated** power semiconductor switches (historically Thyristor/SCR with commutation circuits, but in modern drives almost exclusively IGBT, Power MOSFET, or GTO) to achieve this voltage transformation without any intermediate AC link.

In the context of **Electrical Drives and Control Systems (PCBRT502)**, choppers form the *backbone of DC motor speed control in traction systems, hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), battery-operated vehicles, robotics, and renewable DC microgrids*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Intuition

Imagine your kitchen tap:
- The **input pipe** delivers water at a *constant, high pressure* (analogous to fixed DC source, e.g., 240V battery).
- The **output faucet** controls how much water actually reaches your glass (analogous to variable DC output to a motor).
- You do **not** change the source pressure; you simply **turn the tap on and off rapidly**. The longer the tap stays open per cycle, the more water flows in total.

A **chopper does exactly the same thing electrically**:
- The semiconductor switch ($S$) is the *tap* — turned **ON** for a time $T_{on}$ and **OFF** for a time $T_{off}$.
- The **duty cycle** ($D = T_{on}/T$, where $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$) decides the *average* output voltage.
- Higher $D$ → tap open longer → more "average flow" → higher average voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Chopper** = Old name using Thyristor + commutation circuit.
> - **DC-DC Converter** = Modern name using self-commutated devices (IGBT/MOSFET).
> - Both terms are **interchangeable** in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 Why Choppers? — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Function of Chopper |
|---|---|
| **DC Traction (Locomotives, Trams)** | Smooth, efficient speed/torque control of series DC motors |
| **Battery-Powered EVs & HEVs** | Buck/boost battery voltage to match motor bus voltage |
| **Solar PV MPPT Systems** | Track maximum power point by varying effective load |
| **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** | Compact, high-efficiency DC voltage regulation |
| **Robotics & Servo Drives** | Fast, four-quadrant DC motor control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three classical "reactor" properties** that allow a chopper to deliver a *smooth, ripple-controlled* DC output are the **Inductor (L)**, **Capacitor (C)**, and **Freewheeling Diode (FWD)**. These three components act as the *reservoir* of the water-tap analogy.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Operating Modes — Quadrant Classification

A chopper-fed drive can operate in **four quadrants** based on the direction of motor current ($I_a$) and motor voltage ($V_a$):

| Quadrant | Mode | Voltage Sign | Current Sign | Drive Behaviour | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **I** | Forward Motoring | + | + | Motor accelerates forward | Driving the vehicle |
| **II** | Forward Braking (Regenerative) | + | − | Motor acts as generator | Plugging / Regen |
| **III** | Reverse Motoring | − | − | Motor accelerates reverse | Reverse driving |
| **IV** | Reverse Braking (Regenerative) | − | + | Motor acts as generator | Reverse braking |

> [!NOTE]
> **First-Quadrant Chopper** is the **most frequently asked** topology in KTU exams. Master it first.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Choppers (by Power Flow)

1. **Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter)** — $V_o < V_s$
2. **Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter)** — $V_o > V_s$
3. **Step-Up/Down Chopper (Buck-Boost)** — $V_o$ can be less or greater than $V_s$
4. **Two-Quadrant (Class A) Chopper** — Both $V_o$ polarities possible, single current direction
5. **Four-Quadrant (Class E) Chopper** — Full regenerative bidirectional drive

---

## 2.3 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Used:** $V_s$ = Source DC Voltage (V), $T$ = Chopping period (s), $T_{on}$ = Switch ON time (s), $T_{off}$ = Switch OFF time (s), $D$ = Duty cycle, $L$ = Load inductance (H), $R$ = Load resistance ($\Omega$), $I_a$ = Armature current (A).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Chopping Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ |
| 2 | Duty Cycle (Step-Down) | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le D \le 1$ |
| 3 | Average Output Voltage (Buck) | $V_{o(avg)} = D \cdot V_s$ | V | Linear with $D$ |
| 4 | RMS Output Voltage (Buck) | $V_{o(rms)} = V_s\sqrt{D}$ | V | Used for heating/illumination loads |
| 5 | Average Output Voltage (Boost) | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$ | V | $D < 1$ strictly |
| 6 | Average Output Voltage (Buck-Boost) | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$ | V | Output is **inverted** |
| 7 | Critical (Boundary) Inductance | $L_{c} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{2} \cdot \left(1 - D\right)$ for Buck | H | Below $L_c$ → discontinuous conduction |
| 8 | Output Current Ripple (Buck) | $\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D \cdot (1 - D)}{f \cdot L}$ | A | Maximum at $D = 0.5$ |
| 9 | Average Load Current | $I_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_{o(avg)}}{R}$ | A | For purely resistive load |
| 10 | Power Delivered to Load | $P_{load} = V_{o(rms)}^{2} / R$ | W | RMS value used |
| 11 | Effective Input Resistance (Boost) | $R_{in} = \dfrac{R}{(1 - D)^{2}}$ | $\Omega$ | Used in MPPT modelling |
| 12 | Chopping Period (Time) | $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ | s | Always positive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** Never use vertical bars $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. The vertical bar will break the table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction — The "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** Load current $I_L(t)$ **never falls to zero** during a switching cycle. The inductor "stores" energy continuously. This is the **desirable** mode for most drives.

> [!WARNING]
> **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** Load current $I_L(t)$ **reaches zero** for a finite interval within $T$. This happens when:
> 1. Load inductance $L$ is *too small* (below critical $L_c$).
> 2. Load resistance $R$ is *too large* (light load).
> 3. Switching frequency $f$ is *too low*.

DCM is undesirable in drives because it produces:
- Non-linear voltage transfer ratio
- High peak currents (damaging to switches)
- Poor speed regulation

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- The **Buck formula** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ is literally the control law of every **EV accelerator pedal** — pressing the pedal increases $D$, which increases the motor voltage and hence the speed.
- The **Boost formula** $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ is used in **regenerative braking** of EVs to push recovered kinetic energy back into a higher-voltage battery.
- The **Buck-Boost** topology is the heart of **solar PV MPPT controllers** because PV panels can be at lower or higher voltage than the battery bus.
- The **critical inductance** formula determines the *minimum inductor size* to avoid DCM — a key design constraint for SMPS engineers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of a Step-Down (Buck) Chopper

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions (KTU Standard):** Source $V_s$ is ideal ripple-free DC. Switch $S$ is ideal (zero voltage drop when ON, infinite when OFF). Diode $D_F$ is ideal. Load is RL.

### Step 1: Define the chopping period

$$T = T_{on} + T_{off}$$

### Step 2: Instantaneous output voltage $v_o(t)$

When switch $S$ is **ON** (interval $0 \le t \le T_{on}$), the source is directly connected to the load:

$$v_o(t) = V_s$$

When switch $S$ is **OFF** (interval $T_{on} \le t \le T$), the freewheeling diode $D_F$ conducts, and assuming the load inductance maintains current:

$$v_o(t) = 0$$

(Strictly, for an $R\text{-}L$ load there is a small negative spike across $L\cdot di/dt$, but for average-voltage analysis it is neglected in first-quadrant operation.)

### Step 3: Write the average over one period $T$

By definition of the average value of a periodic function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o(avg)} &= \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] \\[6pt]
&= V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} \\[6pt]
&= V_s \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final Compact Result

$$V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D \quad \text{[Volts]}$$

**Logical interpretation:** The average output voltage is a **linear fraction** of the source voltage, scaled by the duty cycle. As $D \to 1$, $V_o \to V_s$ (switch always ON — full voltage). As $D \to 0$, $V_o \to 0$ (switch always OFF — no voltage).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Output Voltage of a Step-Down Chopper

The RMS value is required when the load is **heating** or **lighting** in nature (e.g., lamp dimmer, resistance heater) because heating depends on $I^{2}R$ dissipation.

### Step 1: Definition of RMS

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^{2}(t) \, dt}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the piecewise waveform

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o(rms)}^{2} &= \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s^{2} \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0^{2} \, dt \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s^{2} \cdot T_{on} \right] \\[6pt]
&= V_s^{2} \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Take the square root

$$V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $V_{o(rms)} > V_{o(avg)}$ always (because the squaring operation weights high values more). This is why a lamp dimmer uses the RMS relation, not the average.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Output Voltage of a Step-Up (Boost) Chopper

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boost Chopper Topology:** Source $V_s$ in series with inductor $L$. Switch $S$ in shunt (parallel) with the load. Diode $D$ in series with the load. The output capacitor $C$ smooths the load voltage.

### Step 1: Switch ON phase ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$)

The switch shorts the diode out. The inductor charges from the source:

$$v_L = V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_s = L \cdot \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

The diode is reverse-biased, so the capacitor $C$ supplies the load (assuming $RC \gg T$, capacitor voltage ≈ constant).

$$v_C = V_o$$

### Step 2: Switch OFF phase ($T_{on} \le t \le T$)

The switch opens. Current path: $V_s \to L \to D \to C \parallel R \to$ back to $V_s$. Both source and inductor deliver energy to the load:

$$v_L = V_s - V_o$$

### Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance (Inductor Steady-State)

In **steady-state periodic operation**, the average voltage across an inductor over one cycle must be **zero** (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up or down to infinity). This is the **volt-second balance principle**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off} &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s \cdot T_{on} + V_s \cdot T_{off} - V_o \cdot T_{off} &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s (T_{on} + T_{off}) - V_o \cdot T_{off} &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s \cdot T &= V_o \cdot T_{off}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_o$

$$
V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T_{off}} = V_s \cdot \frac{1}{1 - D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$. As $D \to 1$ (switch almost always ON), $V_o \to \infty$ in theory. In practice, parasitic resistances cap this gain around $D \approx 0.85$–$0.9$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical (Minimum) Load Inductance for Continuous Conduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $L_c$ is the smallest inductance value that just barely keeps the load current *from falling to zero* at the end of the OFF period. Below $L_c$, the current hits zero — discontinuous conduction begins.

### Step 1: Expression for load current ripple

For a Buck chopper feeding an R-L load, the peak-to-peak ripple in inductor current is:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot (1 - D)}{f \cdot L}$$

### Step 2: Boundary condition

At the **boundary** of continuous conduction, the minimum current just touches zero:

$$I_{min} = I_{o(avg)} - \frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0$$

$$I_{o(avg)} = \frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot (1 - D)}{2 f L}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D$ and $I_{o(avg)} = V_{o(avg)}/R$

$$
\frac{V_s \cdot D}{R} = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot (1 - D)}{2 f L}
$$

### Step 4: Cancel and solve for $L$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{(1 - D)}{2 f L} \\[6pt]
L_c &= \frac{R \cdot (1 - D)}{2 f}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU Textbook Form):** $\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{R(1 - D)}{2 f}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** Always choose $L \ge 2 L_c$ in practice to provide a safety margin and ensure CCM operation over the full load range.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Sample Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A step-down chopper is supplied from a **240 V DC** source. The chopper operates at a chopping frequency of **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of **0.4** and feeds a resistive load of **$10 \Omega$**. Find:
> (i) Average output voltage
> (ii) RMS output voltage
> (iii) Average and RMS load current
> (iv) Power delivered to the load

**Solution:**

Given: $V_s = 240\,\text{V}$, $f = 1000\,\text{Hz}$, $D = 0.4$, $R = 10\,\Omega$.

**(i) Average Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D = 240 \times 0.4 = 96\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) RMS Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{D} = 240 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 240 \times 0.6325 = 151.79\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Average and RMS Load Current:**

$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{96}{10} = 9.6\,\text{A}
$$

$$
I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{151.79}{10} = 15.18\,\text{A}
$$

**(iv) Power Delivered to Load:**

$$
P_{load} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{2}}{R} = \frac{(151.79)^{2}}{10} = 2304.0\,\text{W} \approx 2.3\,\text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The RMS is greater than the average, as expected. Power uses RMS because heating is governed by $I^{2}R$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a DC-DC Converter Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DC VOLTAGE SOURCE Vs"] --> nodeB["HIGH FREQUENCY SWITCH S"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FREEWHEELING DIODE DF"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["OUTPUT LC FILTER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DC LOAD R AND L"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["GATE DRIVE AND PWM CONTROLLER"]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeG["FEEDBACK SENSOR VOLTAGE OR CURRENT"]
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH["DUTY CYCLE D REFERENCE"]
    nodeH --> nodeF

    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeH fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram:** The closed loop (nodeE → nodeG → nodeF → nodeB) is the **regulation feedback**. The reference $D$ (nodeH) is set by the user (e.g., accelerator pedal position). This is exactly how a closed-loop chopper-fed DC drive works.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mode Sequencing of a Buck Chopper

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateON: Start of cycle
    StateON: SWITCH ON, DIODE OFF, Vo EQUALS Vs
    StateON --> StateOFF: Time equals T_on
    StateOFF: SWITCH OFF, DIODE CONDUCTS, Vo APPROX 0
    StateOFF --> StateON: Time equals T_off
    StateON: Output = Vs
    StateOFF: Output = 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The waveform pattern over time is a rectangular pulse train** with amplitude $V_s$, width $T_{on}$, period $T$, and average value $V_s \cdot D$.

---

