# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART — A (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a chopper. List any two applications of choppers in DC drives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A chopper is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by high-frequency switching action. It uses forced-commutated semiconductor switches (IGBT/MOSFET/SCR with commutation circuit).

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Speed control of DC motors** in traction systems (locomotives, EVs).
2. **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** for efficient DC voltage regulation.
3. Battery-operated electric vehicles and robotics servo drives.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is meant by duty cycle? What is its significance in a chopper?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The duty cycle $D$ of a chopper is defined as the ratio of the ON-time of the switch to the total chopping period:
> $D = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off}) = T_{on} / T$.
>
> **Significance:** The duty cycle directly controls the average output voltage. For a buck chopper, $V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D$. By varying $D$ between 0 and 1, the output voltage can be varied from 0 to $V_s$ smoothly and efficiently. It is the **primary control parameter** in modern chopper drives.

---

## PART — B (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **working principle of a step-down (buck) chopper** feeding a resistive load. Derive the expression for average and RMS output voltage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A step-down chopper is supplied from a **220 V DC** source and operates at **$f = 500$ Hz** with a duty cycle of **$D = 0.6$**. The load resistance is **$R = 5\,\Omega$** and load inductance is **$L = 15$ mH**. Determine:
> (i) Average and RMS output voltage
> (ii) Average and RMS load current
> (iii) Power delivered to load
> (iv) Whether the chopper is operating in continuous conduction mode. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part A (a) (7 marks)

**Circuit Diagram (text representation):**

```
   +---[ S ]---+---o---+----[ L ]----+----+
   |            |       |             |    |
  V_s          D_F      |            R    C
   |            |       |             |    |
   +------------+-------+-------------+----+
   ON time:  S closed, D_F reverse biased → Vo = Vs
   OFF time: S open,   D_F conducts     → Vo ≈ 0
```

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- During the **ON period** ($T_{on}$), the controlled switch $S$ is closed. The source $V_s$ is directly applied across the load. The freewheeling diode $D_F$ is reverse-biased.
- During the **OFF period** ($T_{off}$), the switch $S$ is opened. The load current freewheels through the diode $D_F$. For a purely resistive load, the load voltage falls to zero.
- The chopping frequency $f = 1/T$ is chosen high enough (typically > 200 Hz) to ensure the human eye or motor mechanical inertia does not notice the ripple.

**Derivation of Average Voltage (2 marks):**
- As derived in Section 3.1: $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t)\, dt = V_s \cdot D$

**Derivation of RMS Voltage (2 marks):**
- As derived in Section 3.2: $V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^{2}(t)\, dt} = V_s \sqrt{D}$

#### Model Answer — Part A (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V, $f = 500$ Hz, $D = 0.6$, $R = 5\,\Omega$, $L = 15$ mH.

**(i) Average and RMS Output Voltage (2 marks):**
$$
V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D = 220 \times 0.6 = 132\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{D} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.6} = 220 \times 0.7746 = 170.4\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Average and RMS Load Current (2 marks):**
$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{132}{5} = 26.4\,\text{A}
$$

$$
I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{170.4}{5} = 34.08\,\text{A}
$$

**(iii) Power Delivered to Load (1.5 marks):**
$$
P_{load} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{2}}{R} = \frac{(170.4)^{2}}{5} = 5807.2\,\text{W} \approx 5.81\,\text{kW}
$$

**(iv) Continuous Conduction Check (1.5 marks):**
First, calculate the critical inductance:
$$
L_c = \frac{R(1 - D)}{2 f} = \frac{5 \times (1 - 0.6)}{2 \times 500} = \frac{5 \times 0.4}{1000} = 0.002\,\text{H} = 2\,\text{mH}
$$

Since $L = 15$ mH $> L_c = 2$ mH, the chopper operates in **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)**. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **working of a step-up (boost) chopper**. Derive the expression for the average output voltage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A boost chopper has an input voltage of **$V_s = 50$ V** and supplies a load of **$R = 20\,\Omega$**. The chopping frequency is **$f = 2$ kHz** and the duty cycle is **$D = 0.5$**. Determine:
> (i) Average output voltage
> (ii) Average load current
> (iii) Average source current
> (iv) Peak-to-peak ripple in inductor current **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Question B (a) (7 marks)

**Circuit Description (text form):**

```
         +---[ L ]---+---[ D ]---+----+
         |            |           |    |
        V_s          (switch)    C    R  (load)
         |            |           |    |
         +------------+-----------+----+
```

The inductor $L$ is in series with the source, the switch $S$ shunts the load, and the diode $D$ blocks reverse current into the source during the OFF time.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- **ON state ($T_{on}$):** The switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased. The inductor stores energy from the source: $v_L = V_s$. The capacitor $C$ discharges into the load.
- **OFF state ($T_{off}$):** The switch opens. The inductor opposes the change in current, so its induced EMF adds to the source. Both $V_s$ and $L$ deliver energy through the diode to the load and capacitor: $v_L = V_s - V_o$.

**Derivation of Average Output Voltage (4 marks):**
Apply volt-second balance on the inductor (as derived in Section 3.3):
$$
\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt = 0
$$

$$
V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off} = 0
$$

Solving:
$$
V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}
$$

#### Model Answer — Question B (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_s = 50$ V, $R = 20\,\Omega$, $f = 2000$ Hz, $D = 0.5$.

**(i) Average Output Voltage (2 marks):**
$$
V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D} = \frac{50}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{50}{0.5} = 100\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Average Load Current (2 marks):**
$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{100}{20} = 5\,\text{A}
$$

**(iii) Average Source Current (1.5 marks):**
By power balance (assuming lossless converter): $V_s \cdot I_s = V_o \cdot I_o$
$$
I_s = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{100 \times 5}{50} = 10\,\text{A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalently:** $I_s = \dfrac{I_o}{1 - D} = \dfrac{5}{0.5} = 10$ A. Same result.

**(iv) Peak-to-Peak Inductor Current Ripple (1.5 marks):**
During the ON period, the inductor sees $V_s$ across it:
$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s \cdot D}{f \cdot L}
$$

Without $L$ value given, express in general form. Assuming the current is continuous, the ripple is:

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{L} = \frac{50 \times 0.5 \times (1/2000)}{L} = \frac{12.5 \text{ mH}}{L}\,\text{[in mA]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If a specific $L$ (e.g., $L = 5$ mH) is later specified, simply substitute. The method is what is graded.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Average vs. RMS:** Average voltage is used for **motor speed / DC measurement**, while RMS is used for **heating / power**. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Always write the unit (V, A, Hz, mH, etc.) in the box. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Sign error in Buck-Boost:** The output voltage is **inverted** in polarity for the classical buck-boost. Many students forget this.
> 4. **Volt-second balance is a must:** Never write "by inspection" in derivations. Always show the integral.
> 5. **Critical inductance check:** If the question does not explicitly ask for CCM/DCM check, you can skip it. If it does, forgetting it is a guaranteed 1.5-mark loss.
> 6. **Boost formula division by zero:** At $D = 1$, the formula diverges. Always state $D < 1$.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Must-Remember Facts on DC-DC Converters for KTU 2024:**
>
> - [x] **Chopper = DC-DC Converter**; the two terms are interchangeable in KTU papers.
> - [x] **Duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T$ is the **primary control knob**.
> - [x] **Buck (Step-Down):** $V_{o(avg)} = V_s \cdot D$; **Boost (Step-Up):** $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$.
> - [x] **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = D V_s/(1-D)$; output polarity is **inverted**.
> - [x] **RMS Buck:** $V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{D}$ — used for heating/lighting loads.
> - [x] **Average Buck:** $V_{o(avg)} = V_s D$ — used for motor/DC measurement.
> - [x] **Critical Inductance (Buck):** $L_c = R(1-D)/(2f)$; choose $L \ge 2 L_c$ in design.
> - [x] **CCM** is the **desirable** mode; **DCM** occurs when $L < L_c$ or $R$ is too large.
> - [x] **Volt-second balance** is the key analytical tool for deriving boost/buck-boost equations.
> - [x] **Four-Quadrant Operation** is achieved by combining choppers in H-bridge configurations (Class E).
> - [x] **PWM control** (constant $f$, variable $D$) is preferred over **FM control** (variable $f$) due to easier filter design.
> - [x] **Freewheeling Diode (FWD)** is essential to provide a path for inductive load current during the OFF period.
> - [x] **Applications:** EV traction, HEV regen braking, solar MPPT, SMPS, robotics.
> - [x] **Power calculation always uses RMS:** $P = V_{o(rms)}^{2}/R$.
> - [x] **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Derive $V_{o(avg)}$ and verify with numerical example" — practice this format.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Step Down and Step Up Choppers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Step Down and Step Up Choppers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Chopper?

A **DC Chopper** is a high-speed static power electronic switch (typically realized using IGBT, MOSFET, GTO, or BJT) that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a **variable DC output voltage** by rapidly chopping the input waveform at a high switching frequency. The output voltage is controlled by varying the **duty cycle** (the fraction of time the switch remains ON).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A chopper is a power electronic device that converts fixed DC input to a variable DC output voltage directly without any intermediate AC link. Choppers are widely classified based on the direction of current and voltage flow into quadrants of operation (Class A, B, C, and D choppers).

## 1.2 Classification of Choppers Based on Quadrant Operation

The classification depends on the **polarity of output voltage** and **direction of load current**:

| Chopper Class | Quadrant Operation | Voltage Polarity | Current Direction | Functional Name |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Class A** | Quadrant I (First) | Positive ($V_o > 0$) | Positive ($I_o > 0$) | **Step-Down Chopper** |
| **Class B** | Quadrant II (Second) | Positive ($V_o > 0$) | Negative ($I_o < 0$) | **Step-Up Chopper** |
| **Class C** | Quadrant I & II | Positive ($V_o > 0$) | Both directions | Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class A + Class B) |
| **Class D** | All Four Quadrants | Both polarities | Both directions | Four-Quadrant Chopper (Full H-Bridge) |

## 1.3 The Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter) — Conceptual Intuition

**Analogy:** Imagine a **water tap with a quick-pressing valve**. When you press the valve (switch ON), water flows out. When you release it (switch OFF), water stops. If you press the valve very rapidly and adjust the duration of each press, you can control the *average* flow of water coming out of the tap. The longer the press-duration relative to the total cycle, the higher the average flow.

**Engineering Translation:**
- The **water source** = Fixed DC input voltage ($V_s$)
- The **valve** = Power semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET)
- The **average water flow** = Average output voltage ($V_o$)
- The **press duration ratio** = Duty cycle ($\alpha$)

A Step-Down Chopper produces an output voltage that is **less than** the input voltage, hence the name "step-down." It is mathematically equivalent to the **Buck Converter** in power electronics literature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** The load current through the freewheeling inductor never falls to zero during the OFF period. This is the normal operating mode assumed in KTU derivations.

## 1.4 The Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter) — Conceptual Intuition

**Analogy:** Imagine a **mechanical spring-loaded water pump**. When the pump piston is pushed in (switch ON), water is drawn into a chamber and a spring is compressed, storing energy. When the piston is released (switch OFF), the compressed spring pushes the stored water out with **more force than was originally applied** — because the spring's stored energy is added to the incoming flow.

**Engineering Translation:**
- The **spring** = Inductor ($L$) that stores energy in its magnetic field
- The **one-way valve** = Diode ($D$) that prevents reverse current flow
- The **pumped water** = Output DC voltage ($V_o$)
- The output voltage becomes **higher** than the input because the inductor's stored energy adds to the source voltage

A Step-Up Chopper produces an output voltage that is **greater than** the input voltage, hence the name "step-up." It is mathematically equivalent to the **Boost Converter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Step-Down:** In a Step-Up Chopper, the inductor is placed in *series with the source*, not in series with the load. The energy storage mechanism is fundamentally different.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-Down Chopper — Circuit Topology & Operation

### 2.1.1 Circuit Components
The basic step-down chopper consists of:
1. **DC Source ($V_s$):** Provides fixed input DC voltage.
2. **Power Switch ($S$):** Typically an IGBT or MOSFET — controlled by a PWM gating signal.
3. **Freewheeling Diode ($D_F$):** Provides a path for inductor current when the switch opens.
4. **Filter Inductor ($L$):** Smooths the load current ripple.
5. **Filter Capacitor ($C$):** Smooths the output voltage ripple.
6. **Load ($R$):** Represents the DC motor armature or resistive load.

### 2.1.2 Two Modes of Operation

**Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$):**
- The switch $S$ is closed; the freewheeling diode $D_F$ is reverse-biased.
- The full source voltage $V_s$ appears across the load.
- Inductor $L$ stores energy; current rises linearly.
- The KVL equation: $V_s = V_L + V_o$, therefore $V_L = V_s - V_o$.

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($T_{on} \le t \le T$):**
- The switch $S$ is open; the inductor opposes the sudden change in current by reversing its polarity.
- The freewheeling diode $D_F$ becomes forward-biased and conducts.
- The inductor releases its stored energy to the load.
- The KVL equation: $0 = V_L + V_o$, therefore $V_L = -V_o$.

### 2.1.3 The Critical Concept of Volt-Second Balance (Steady-State Condition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volt-Second Balance Theorem:** In **steady-state** operation, the average voltage across an inductor over one complete switching cycle must equal **zero**. Otherwise, the inductor current would either keep increasing (saturating the core) or decreasing to zero — neither of which is sustainable.

Mathematically: $\int_0^T V_L \, dt = 0$

This principle is the foundation for deriving the voltage transfer ratio of both choppers.

### 2.1.4 Duty Cycle Definition
$$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

where $T$ = Total switching period, $T_{on}$ = ON duration, $T_{off}$ = OFF duration.

Therefore: $T_{off} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot T$

## 2.2 Step-Up Chopper — Circuit Topology & Operation

### 2.2.1 Circuit Components
The basic step-up chopper consists of:
1. **DC Source ($V_s$)**
2. **Inductor ($L$):** Placed in **series with the source** (key difference from step-down).
3. **Power Switch ($S$):** Connected between the inductor-capacitor node and ground.
4. **Diode ($D$):** Forward-biased only when the switch is OFF.
5. **Filter Capacitor ($C$):** Maintains a steady output voltage.
6. **Load ($R$):** Across the output.

### 2.2.2 Two Modes of Operation

**Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$):**
- Switch $S$ is closed; diode $D$ is reverse-biased.
- The source $V_s$ appears directly across the inductor.
- Inductor current rises linearly, storing energy: $V_L = V_s$.
- The capacitor $C$ discharges into the load (isolated from source).
- The load is supplied **solely** by the capacitor's stored charge.

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($T_{on} \le t \le T$):**
- Switch $S$ opens; the inductor opposes the current change by reversing its polarity.
- The induced voltage adds to the source voltage: the series combination $V_s + V_L$ forward-biases the diode $D$.
- Current now flows through $D$ to charge $C$ and supply the load.
- KVL: $V_s + V_L = V_o$, therefore $V_L = V_o - V_s$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

### Step-Down Chopper Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Duty Cycle | $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ | ON-time fraction of total period |
| Average Output Voltage | $V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s$ | Voltage transfer ratio |
| Peak-to-Peak Current Ripple | $\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s(1-\alpha)}{fL}$ | Across inductor |
| Critical Inductance | $L_{min} = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f}$ | Boundary of CCM/DCM |
| Average Load Current | $I_o = \frac{\alpha \cdot V_s}{R}$ | Assuming ideal switch |
| Switching Frequency | $f = 1/T$ | Hz |
| RMS Switch Current | $I_{S,rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} \cdot I_o$ | Through semiconductor |
| Average Switch Current | $I_{S,avg} = \alpha \cdot I_o$ | Through semiconductor |

### Step-Up Chopper Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Duty Cycle | $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ | ON-time fraction |
| Average Output Voltage | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | Always $> V_s$ |
| Peak-to-Peak Inductor Current Ripple | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot \alpha}{fL}$ | During charging |
| Average Inductor Current | $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-\alpha}$ | Continuous input current |
| Average Load Current | $I_o = \dfrac{V_s}{R(1-\alpha)}$ | Output current |
| Critical Inductance | $L_{min} = \dfrac{\alpha(1-\alpha)^2 R}{2f}$ | Boundary of CCM/DCM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables. In LaTeX expressions inside table cells, use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation to avoid breaking the markdown parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Step-Down Choppers** are used in: DC motor speed control (armature voltage control), electric vehicle battery management, SMPS power supplies, LED drivers, and regenerative braking energy recovery.
- **Step-Up Choppers** are used in: hybrid electric vehicles (boosting battery voltage for traction inverter), solar PV MPPT systems, HID lamp ballasts, and PFC (Power Factor Correction) front-ends.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-Down Chopper — Exhaustive Derivation of Output Voltage

### Step 1: Define the Switching Cycle

Let $T$ = total switching period = $T_{on} + T_{off}$.

The switch closes at $t = 0$ and opens at $t = T_{on}$.

### Step 2: Apply KVL During ON Period ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$)

During this interval, the switch is closed. The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased (off). Current flows from $V_s$ through the switch and inductor to the load. The loop equation is:

$$V_s = V_L + V_o$$

where $V_L$ = voltage across inductor, $V_o$ = output voltage across load.

Solving for inductor voltage:
$$V_L = V_s - V_o$$

The inductor voltage is **positive** during this period. Since $V_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$, we get:
$$L \frac{di}{dt} = V_s - V_o$$

Therefore, the current rise during the ON period:
$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L}$$

Integrating over $T_{on}$:
$$\Delta I_{rise} = \frac{(V_s - V_o)}{L} \cdot T_{on}$$

### Step 3: Apply KVL During OFF Period ($T_{on} \le t \le T$)

The switch is now open. The inductor current cannot change instantaneously, so the inductor reverses its EMF polarity to maintain current flow. The freewheeling diode $D_F$ becomes forward-biased and provides a closed loop for the inductor current through the load.

The KVL around the new loop (load + diode + inductor):
$$0 = V_L + V_o$$

Solving:
$$V_L = -V_o$$

The inductor voltage is now **negative**. Substituting into the inductor equation:
$$L \frac{di}{dt} = -V_o$$

Therefore, the current fall during the OFF period:
$$\frac{di}{dt} = -\frac{V_o}{L}$$

Integrating over $T_{off}$:
$$\Delta I_{fall} = \frac{V_o}{L} \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Volt-Second Balance

In **steady-state**, the inductor current at the end of one cycle equals the current at the start of the next cycle. This means the **rise in current during $T_{on}$ must equal the fall during $T_{off}$**:

$$\Delta I_{rise} = \Delta I_{fall}$$

$$\frac{(V_s - V_o)}{L} \cdot T_{on} = \frac{V_o}{L} \cdot T_{off}$$

Multiplying both sides by $L$:
$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Expansion

Expanding the left side:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} - V_o \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

Moving the $V_o$ term from LHS to RHS:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{on} + V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

Factoring $V_o$ on the RHS:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot (T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T$$

### Step 6: Final Simplification

Dividing both sides by $T$:
$$V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} = V_o$$

Recognizing $\alpha = T_{on}/T$:
$$\boxed{V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Since $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, we always have $0 \le V_o \le V_s$. This proves that the output voltage is always **less than or equal to** the input voltage — confirming the "step-down" behavior.

---

## 3.2 Step-Up Chopper — Exhaustive Derivation of Output Voltage

### Step 1: Define the Switching Cycle

Same as before: $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$, and $\alpha = T_{on}/T$.

### Step 2: Apply KVL During ON Period ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$)

The switch $S$ is closed, connecting the inductor directly across the source. The diode $D$ is reverse-biased (off) because the cathode voltage is approximately $V_o$ (held by capacitor) which is higher than $V_s$. The load is supplied by the capacitor's stored charge.

Loop equation (only source and inductor in the loop):
$$V_s = V_L$$

Therefore:
$$V_L = V_s = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

The current rise during $T_{on}$:
$$\Delta I_{rise} = \frac{V_s}{L} \cdot T_{on}$$

### Step 3: Apply KVL During OFF Period ($T_{on} \le t \le T$)

The switch opens. The inductor current cannot change instantaneously, so it generates an induced EMF with polarity reversed. The diode $D$ becomes forward-biased because the voltage at its anode (which is $V_s + V_L$) exceeds its cathode voltage ($V_o$).

The current now flows from source $V_s$, through the inductor $L$ (where it adds its stored energy), then through the diode $D$, and into the parallel combination of capacitor $C$ and load $R$.

Loop KVL:
$$V_s + V_L = V_o$$

Solving for inductor voltage:
$$V_L = V_o - V_s$$

Since $V_o > V_s$ (which is what we are proving), $V_L$ is positive during this period too — meaning the inductor is still being charged, but at a slower rate. The current falls because the available voltage headroom is smaller than $V_s$ alone.

Current fall during $T_{off}$:
$$\Delta I_{fall} = \frac{V_o - V_s}{L} \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Volt-Second Balance

Setting rise = fall:
$$\frac{V_s}{L} \cdot T_{on} = \frac{V_o - V_s}{L} \cdot T_{off}$$

Multiplying both sides by $L$:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = (V_o - V_s) \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Expansion

Expanding RHS:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{off} - V_s \cdot T_{off}$$

Moving the second term on RHS to LHS:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + V_s \cdot T_{off} = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

Factoring $V_s$ on the LHS:
$$V_s \cdot (T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

$$V_s \cdot T = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 6: Final Simplification

Dividing both sides by $T_{off}$:
$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T_{off}}$$

Since $T_{off} = T - T_{on} = T(1 - \alpha)$:
$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T(1-\alpha)} = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Since $0 \le \alpha < 1$, the denominator $(1-\alpha)$ is less than 1, making $V_o > V_s$. As $\alpha \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ theoretically (but practically limited by parasitic resistances). This confirms the "step-up" behavior.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Step-Down Chopper

**Problem:** A step-down chopper has input voltage $V_s = 220$ V, load resistance $R = 10$ $\Omega$, switching frequency $f = 1$ kHz, and duty cycle $\alpha = 0.4$. Calculate: (a) Average output voltage, (b) Average load current, (c) RMS output voltage.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using $V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s$:
$$V_o = 0.4 \times 220 = 88 \text{ V}$$

**(b)** Using $I_o = V_o / R$:
$$I_o = \frac{88}{10} = 8.8 \text{ A}$$

**(c)** The output is a chopped DC waveform. During $T_{on}$, $v_o = V_s = 220$ V; during $T_{off}$, $v_o = 0$ V.

The RMS value:
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o^2 \, dt} = \sqrt{\frac{V_s^2 \cdot T_{on}}{T}} = V_s \sqrt{\alpha}$$

$$V_{rms} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 220 \times 0.6325 = 139.14 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Step-Up Chopper

**Problem:** A step-up chopper has input voltage $V_s = 50$ V, duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$, and load $R = 20$ $\Omega$. Calculate: (a) Output voltage, (b) Output current, (c) Average input current.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using $V_o = V_s / (1-\alpha)$:
$$V_o = \frac{50}{1-0.6} = \frac{50}{0.4} = 125 \text{ V}$$

**(b)** Using $I_o = V_o / R$:
$$I_o = \frac{125}{20} = 6.25 \text{ A}$$

**(c)** Input power = Output power (ideal case). Therefore:
$$V_s \cdot I_{in} = V_o \cdot I_o$$
$$I_{in} = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{125 \times 6.25}{50} = \frac{781.25}{50} = 15.625 \text{ A}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Step-Down Chopper — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a Step-Down Chopper, including the current paths during both ON and OFF modes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SourceA[DC Source Vs] --> SwitchA1[Power Switch S - ON Mode]
    SwitchA1 --> IndA1[Inductor L - Charging]
    IndA1 --> CapA[Filter Capacitor C]
    CapA --> LoadA[Load R - V_o delivered]
    LoadA --> SourceA

    SwitchA1 -.Switch opens.-> SwitchA2[Power Switch S - OFF Mode]
    SwitchA2 --> DiodeA[Freewheeling Diode D_F - Forward Biased]
    DiodeA --> IndA2[Inductor L - Discharging]
    IndA2 --> CapA
    CapA --> LoadA
    LoadA --> DiodeA

    PWMMod[PWM Generator - alpha control] --> SwitchA1
    PWMMod -.alpha increases.-> VoutA[Output V_o increases proportionally]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Top Path:** Active when switch is ON — current flows from source through switch, charges inductor, supplies load.
- **Bottom Path:** Active when switch is OFF — inductor releases stored energy through the freewheeling diode to maintain continuous load current.
- **PWM Generator:** External control block that sets the duty cycle $\alpha$.

## 4.2 Step-Up Chopper — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SourceB[DC Source Vs] --> IndB1[Inductor L - Charging from Source]
    IndB1 --> SwitchB1[Power Switch S - CLOSED Mode]
    SwitchB1 --> GroundB[Ground Node]

    CapB[Filter Capacitor C - Pre-charged] --> LoadB[Load R - V_o delivered]
    LoadB --> GroundB
    CapB -.Diode reverse biased.-> DiodeB[Diode D - BLOCKED]

    IndB1 -.Switch opens.-> IndB2[Inductor L - Polarity Reversed]
    IndB2 --> DiodeB
    DiodeB -.Now forward biased.-> CapB
    CapB --> LoadB
    LoadB --> GroundB
    SourceB --> IndB2

    SourceB -.Series with.-> IndB2

    PWMB[PWM Generator - alpha control] --> SwitchB1
    PWMB -.alpha increases.-> VoutB[Output V_o increases inversely with 1 minus alpha]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Mode 1 (Switch Closed):** Source charges inductor; capacitor alone supplies the load.
- **Mode 2 (Switch Open):** Inductor voltage adds to source voltage; both flow through the now-conducting diode to charge the capacitor and supply the load.

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Step-Down vs. Step-Up Choppers

| Feature | Step-Down Chopper | Step-Up Chopper |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Other Name | Buck Converter | Boost Converter |
| Inductor Position | Series with Load | Series with Source |
| Freewheeling Diode | In parallel with Load | In series with Load path |
| Switch Position | Between Source and Load | Between Inductor node and Ground |
| Output Voltage Formula | $V_o = \alpha V_s$ | $V_o = V_s / (1-\alpha)$ |
| Output Range | $0$ to $V_s$ | $V_s$ to $\infty$ |
| Energy Storage Role | Smooths current | Boosts voltage |
| Quadrant | Quadrant I | Quadrant II |
| Typical Application | Motor speed control | Battery voltage booster |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a chopper. List the different methods of chopper classification.*

**Model Answer:**

A chopper is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by switching at high frequency.

Choppers can be classified based on the following criteria:

1. **Based on Quadrant Operation:** Class A (1st quadrant), Class B (2nd quadrant), Class C (1st and 2nd quadrants), Class D (all four quadrants).
2. **Based on Voltage Step:** Step-down (buck) and Step-up (boost) choppers.
3. **Based on Isolation:** Isolated and non-isolated choppers.
4. **Based on Switching Devices:** BJT chopper, MOSFET chopper, IGBT chopper, GTO chopper.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by continuous and discontinuous conduction modes of a chopper?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** The inductor current never falls to zero during a complete switching cycle. The current waveform is continuous and always positive (for step-down) or always flowing in one direction (for step-up).

- **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** The inductor current falls to zero and remains at zero for some portion of the switching cycle before the next ON period begins. This typically occurs at low load currents or when the switching frequency is too low.

The boundary between CCM and DCM is determined by the **critical inductance** $L_{min}$, given by:

$$L_{min} = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f} \quad \text{(Step-Down)}$$

$$L_{min} = \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)^2 R}{2f} \quad \text{(Step-Up)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Step-Down Chopper** with a resistive load. Derive the expression for the average output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A step-down chopper is supplied from a DC source of $V_s = 200$ V. The chopper operates at a switching frequency of $f = 500$ Hz with a duty cycle of $\alpha = 0.3$. The load resistance is $R = 5$ $\Omega$. Calculate:
   - (i) Average output voltage
   - (ii) Average output current
   - (iii) RMS output voltage
   - (iv) Peak-to-peak inductor current ripple if $L = 10$ mH *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. The step-down chopper consists of a DC source $V_s$, a controlled switch (IGBT/MOSFET), a freewheeling diode $D_F$, filter inductor $L$, and a load resistor $R$.

**Working Principle:**
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** Current flows from $V_s$ through the closed switch and inductor $L$ to the load. The inductor stores energy and current rises linearly.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** The inductor maintains current flow through the freewheeling diode, supplying the load while releasing stored energy.

**Derivation:** Using volt-second balance as derived in Section 3.1:

$$V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \cdot V_s$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Average output voltage:
$$V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.3 \times 200 = 60 \text{ V}$$

**(ii)** Average output current:
$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{60}{5} = 12 \text{ A}$$

**(iii)** RMS output voltage:
$$V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\alpha} = 200 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 200 \times 0.5477 = 109.54 \text{ V}$$

**(iv)** Inductor current ripple. Using:
$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s(1-\alpha)}{fL}$$

Substituting values:
$$\Delta I_L = \frac{200 \times (1 - 0.3)}{500 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{200 \times 0.7}{5} = \frac{140}{5} = 28 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently make the mistake of using the wrong formula for current ripple. Always use $\Delta I_L = V_s(1-\alpha)/(fL)$ for step-down — the $(1-\alpha)$ term is critical. Marks will be deducted if you confuse this with the step-up formula which uses $\alpha$ instead of $(1-\alpha)$. Also, ensure consistent units (convert mH to H).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Step-Up Chopper** with a resistive load. Derive the expression for the average output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A step-up chopper has an input voltage of $V_s = 100$ V. The duty cycle is $\alpha = 0.5$ and the switching frequency is $f = 2$ kHz. The load resistance is $R = 50$ $\Omega$. Calculate:
   - (i) Average output voltage
   - (ii) Average output current
   - (iii) Average input current (assuming ideal, lossless chopper)
   - (iv) Inductor ripple current if $L = 5$ mH *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.2 for the complete derivation.

**Working Principle:**
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** The inductor $L$ is connected directly across the source $V_s$. The inductor charges with current rising linearly. The diode $D$ is reverse-biased and the capacitor supplies the load.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** The inductor releases its stored energy. Since the inductor opposes current change, its voltage polarity reverses. The series combination of source and inductor voltage forward-biases the diode, which now conducts to charge the capacitor and supply the load.

**Derivation:** Using volt-second balance:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = (V_o - V_s) \cdot T_{off}$$
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Average output voltage:
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha} = \frac{100}{1-0.5} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200 \text{ V}$$

**(ii)** Average output current:
$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{200}{50} = 4 \text{ A}$$

**(iii)** For an ideal (lossless) chopper: $P_{in} = P_{out}$

$$V_s \cdot I_{in} = V_o \cdot I_o$$
$$I_{in} = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{200 \times 4}{100} = 8 \text{ A}$$

**(iv)** Inductor ripple current. For step-up chopper during ON period:
$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s \cdot \alpha}{f \cdot L}$$

Substituting values:
$$\Delta I_L = \frac{100 \times 0.5}{2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Duty Cycle Direction:** Students often confuse the formula $V_o = V_s/(1-\alpha)$. Remember: as $\alpha$ increases, $V_o$ **increases** (not decreases as in step-down).
> 2. **Inductor Ripple Formula:** In a step-up chopper, the inductor charges during the ON period from $V_s$, so $\Delta I_L = V_s \cdot \alpha / (fL)$. Many students wrongly use the same formula as step-down.
> 3. **Power Conservation:** Always state the assumption that the chopper is lossless when equating input and output power. Otherwise, you must include efficiency $\eta$.
> 4. **Unit Conversion:** Convert mH to H before substituting into the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Step-Down Chopper (Buck):** $V_o = \alpha V_s$ — used to *reduce* voltage. Operates in Quadrant I.
- **Step-Up Chopper (Boost):** $V_o = V_s / (1-\alpha)$ — used to *increase* voltage. Operates in Quadrant II.
- **Duty Cycle:** $\alpha = T_{on}/T$; ranges from 0 to 1.
- **Switching Period:** $T = T_{on} + T_{off} = 1/f$.
- **Volt-Second Balance:** The fundamental principle — average inductor voltage over one cycle equals zero in steady state.
- **Freewheeling Diode (Step-Down):** Provides a path for inductor current when the switch is open — critical for continuous load current.
- **Boost Diode (Step-Up):** Becomes forward-biased only when the switch opens, allowing the inductor's stored energy to flow to the load.
- **Current Ripple:** $\Delta I_L = V_s(1-\alpha)/(fL)$ for step-down; $\Delta I_L = V_s\alpha/(fL)$ for step-up.
- **Critical Inductance:** Below this value, the chopper enters Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM).
- **RMS Output (Step-Down):** $V_{rms} = V_s\sqrt{\alpha}$ — important for heating calculations.
- **Quadrant Classification:** Class A (Step-Down), Class B (Step-Up), Class C (Two-Quadrant), Class D (Four-Quadrant).
- **Energy Storage:** Inductor is the key energy-storage element in both choppers.
- **Power Conservation:** For ideal choppers, $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ (lossless assumption).
- **Common Applications:** DC motor speed control, SMPS, electric vehicles, solar PV systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat circuit diagram** first, label all components ($V_s$, $S$, $D_F$, $L$, $C$, $R$), and then write the two KVL equations — one for ON mode and one for OFF mode. Apply volt-second balance to derive the output voltage formula. For numericals, convert all units (mH → H, kHz → Hz) **before** substituting into formulas. This systematic approach guarantees full marks in KTU evaluations.
