# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order System — Step Response (Unit Step Input)

Consider the transfer function:

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K}{Ts + 1}
$$

For a unit step input $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s(Ts + 1)}
$$

Performing **partial fraction expansion**:

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{KT}{Ts + 1} = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K}{s + \frac{1}{T}}
$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform:

$$
c(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/T}\right), \quad t \geq 0
$$

### Key Observations:

- **Initial value:** $c(0) = 0$ (the system cannot jump instantaneously).
- **Final value:** $c(\infty) = K$ (matches the steady-state DC gain).
- **At $t = T$:** $c(T) = K(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632\, K$ (i.e., **63.2 %** of the final value).
- **At $t = 4T$:** $c(4T) \approx 0.982\, K$ (i.e., **98.2 %** — the practical settling point).
- **The response is exponential and monotonically rising** — **no overshoot, no oscillation**.

## 2.2 First-Order System — Ramp Response (Unit Ramp Input)

For a unit ramp input $R(s) = \frac{1}{s^{2}}$:

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s^{2}(Ts + 1)}
$$

Partial fraction expansion:

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s^{2}} - \frac{KT}{s} + \frac{KT}{s + \frac{1}{T}}
$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$
c(t) = K t - KT + KT\,e^{-t/T} = K(t - T) + KT\,e^{-t/T}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

### Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp:

The error is $e(t) = r(t) - c(t) = t - \left[K t - KT(1 - e^{-t/T})\right]$.

If $K = 1$:

$$
e(t) = T(1 - e^{-t/T}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = T
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **first-order system always exhibits a finite, non-zero steady-state error (equal to T) when tracking a ramp input**. To make this error zero, the system type must be increased (a Type-2 system is required).

## 2.3 Second-Order System — Step Response

The general second-order closed-loop transfer function:

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

The characteristic equation $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$ has roots:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\,\omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\,\omega_{d}
$$

where $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

### 2.3.1 Case Classification Based on $\zeta$:

| Damping Ratio | Case Name | Roots | Nature of Response |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $\zeta = 0$ | Undamped | $\pm j\omega_{n}$ | Sustained oscillation |
| $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Underdamped | Complex conjugates | Decaying oscillation |
| $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped | Real, repeated ($-\omega_{n}$) | Fastest non-oscillatory |
| $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped | Real, distinct | Slow non-oscillatory |

### 2.3.2 Underdamped Step Response (most important for KTU)

$$
c(t) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}} \sin\left(\omega_{d} t + \phi\right), \quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)
$$

### 2.3.3 Transient Performance Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four time-domain specifications of a second-order underdamped system (KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 high-yield):

- **Delay Time:** $t_{d} = \dfrac{1 + 0.7\zeta}{\omega_{n}}$
- **Rise Time:** $t_{r} = \dfrac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}} = \dfrac{\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}$
- **Peak Time:** $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}$
- **Maximum Percent Overshoot:** $M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\,\%$
- **Settling Time (2% band):** $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$
- **Settling Time (5% band):** $t_{s} = \dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$

## 2.4 Second-Order System — Ramp Response

For unit ramp input $R(s) = 1/s^{2}$:

$$
C(s) = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2}(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2})}
$$

After expansion, the **steady-state error** of a second-order system for a unit ramp input is:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_{n}} = \frac{1}{K_{v}}, \quad \text{where } K_{v} = \frac{\omega_{n}}{2\zeta} \text{ is the velocity error constant.}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A first-order system has $e_{ss} = T$ (Type-1) for unit ramp; a second-order system has $e_{ss} = 2\zeta/\omega_{n}$ (Type-1) for unit ramp.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | System / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | First-order TF | $\dfrac{K}{Ts+1}$ | $K$ = DC gain, $T$ = time constant (s) |
| 2 | First-order step response | $c(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/T})$ | Monotonic rise |
| 3 | First-order ramp response | $c(t) = K(t - T) + KT\,e^{-t/T}$ | Steady-state error $= T$ (for $K=1$) |
| 4 | Second-order TF | $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ | $\omega_{n}$ in rad/s |
| 5 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Rise time | $t_{r} = \dfrac{\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta}{\omega_{d}}$ | s |
| 7 | Peak time | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ | s |
| 8 | % Maximum overshoot | $M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Settling time (2 %) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | s |
| 10 | Settling time (5 %) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | s |
| 11 | Delay time | $t_{d} = \dfrac{1 + 0.7\zeta}{\omega_{n}}$ | s |
| 12 | Second-order ramp error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_{n}}$ | dimensionless |

### Real-World Engineering Utility:

- **DC Motor Position Control:** A field-controlled or armature-controlled DC motor driving a load is a classic second-order system. The step response analysis determines how quickly the motor reaches its commanded position and how much overshoot occurs.
- **Cruise Control in Automobiles:** A first-order approximation models the vehicle's velocity response to throttle input.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Chemical mixing tanks, thermal heating systems, and liquid-level systems are widely modeled as first-order systems.
- **Robotics and CNC Machines:** Second-order models predict oscillations in robotic arms, requiring careful damping design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Step Response of a First-Order System

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{K}{Ts + 1}$ and $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ (unit step).

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides to find $C(s)$:**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\cdot R(s) = \frac{K}{Ts + 1}\cdot\frac{1}{s} = \frac{K}{s(Ts + 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction decomposition.** Let

$$
\frac{K}{s(Ts + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{Ts + 1}
$$

Multiplying throughout by $s(Ts + 1)$:

$$
K = A(Ts + 1) + B s
$$

Setting $s = 0$:

$$
K = A(1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = K
$$

Setting $s = -\frac{1}{T}$:

$$
K = B\left(-\frac{1}{T}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = -KT
$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite $C(s)$ with the resolved constants:**

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s} + \frac{-KT}{Ts + 1} = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K}{s + \frac{1}{T}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply inverse Laplace transform term by term:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{K}{s}\right\} = K, \quad \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{K}{s + \frac{1}{T}}\right\} = K\,e^{-t/T}
$$

**Step 5 — Final time-domain response:**

$$
\boxed{\,c(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/T}\right), \quad t \geq 0\,}
$$

**Verification using Initial Value Theorem:**

$$
c(0) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s\,C(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{sK}{s(Ts + 1)} = \lim_{s \to \infty}\frac{K}{Ts + 1} = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

**Verification using Final Value Theorem:**

$$
c(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,C(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{sK}{s(Ts + 1)} = \lim_{s \to 0}\frac{K}{Ts + 1} = K \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Ramp Response of a First-Order System

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts + 1}$ and $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ (unit ramp).

**Step 1 — Form the output:**

$$
C(s) = \frac{K}{s^{2}(Ts + 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion.** Let

$$
\frac{K}{s^{2}(Ts + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^{2}} + \frac{C}{Ts + 1}
$$

Multiplying by $s^{2}(Ts+1)$:

$$
K = A\,s(Ts + 1) + B(Ts + 1) + C\,s^{2}
$$

Expanding:

$$
K = A T s^{2} + A s + B T s + B + C s^{2} = (AT + C)s^{2} + (A + BT)s + B
$$

**Step 3 — Equate coefficients** (left side has no $s$ or $s^{2}$ terms):

- $s^{2}$: $AT + C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C = -AT$
- $s^{1}$: $A + BT = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A = -BT$
- $s^{0}$: $B = K$

Solving:

$$
B = K, \quad A = -KT, \quad C = -AT = -(-KT)T = KT^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite $C(s)$:**

$$
C(s) = -\frac{KT}{s} + \frac{K}{s^{2}} + \frac{KT^{2}}{Ts + 1} = \frac{K}{s^{2}} - \frac{KT}{s} + \frac{KT}{s + \frac{1}{T}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply inverse Laplace transform:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\tfrac{K}{s^{2}}\right\} = Kt, \quad \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\tfrac{KT}{s}\right\} = KT, \quad \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\tfrac{KT}{s + 1/T}\right\} = KT\,e^{-t/T}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,c(t) = K t - KT + KT\,e^{-t/T} = K(t - T) + KT\,e^{-t/T}, \quad t \geq 0\,}
$$

**Steady-state error derivation:**

$$
e(t) = r(t) - c(t) = t - \left[Kt - KT(1 - e^{-t/T})\right]
$$

For $K = 1$:

$$
e(t) = T(1 - e^{-t/T}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} T(1 - e^{-t/T}) = T
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Step Response of a Second-Order System (Underdamped Case)

**Given:** $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ and $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

**Step 1 — Output in s-domain:**

$$
C(s) = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2})}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion** (assume $\zeta < 1$):

$$
C(s) = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{Bs + C}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Find $A$ (cover-up method at $s = 0$):**

$$
A = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for B and C.** Multiply through by the denominator:

$$
\omega_{n}^{2} = A(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}) + (Bs + C)s
$$

$$
\omega_{n}^{2} = A s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} A s + A \omega_{n}^{2} + B s^{2} + C s
$$

Comparing with $\omega_{n}^{2} = 0 \cdot s^{2} + 0 \cdot s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:

- $s^{2}$: $A + B = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = -A = -1$
- $s^{1}$: $2\zeta\omega_{n}A + C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C = -2\zeta\omega_{n}$

So:

$$
C(s) = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{-s - 2\zeta\omega_{n}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Complete the square in the second denominator:**

$$
s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = (s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{n}^{2}(1 - \zeta^{2}) = (s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite numerator in shifted form** by adding and subtracting $\zeta\omega_{n}$:

$$
-s - 2\zeta\omega_{n} = -(s + \zeta\omega_{n}) - \zeta\omega_{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{(s + \zeta\omega_{n}) + \zeta\omega_{n}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Split into standard inverse-Laplace forms:**

$$
C(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{s + \zeta\omega_{n}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}} - \frac{\zeta\omega_{n}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply inverse Laplace transform using shifting theorem:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\tfrac{s + \zeta\omega_{n}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}\right\} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}\cos(\omega_{d} t)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\tfrac{\omega_{d}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}\right\} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}\sin(\omega_{d} t)
$$

The second term needs $\omega_{d}$ scaling:

$$
\frac{\zeta\omega_{n}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}} = \frac{\zeta\omega_{n}}{\omega_{d}}\cdot\frac{\omega_{d}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}
$$

So its inverse is $\dfrac{\zeta\omega_{n}}{\omega_{d}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\sin(\omega_{d}t) = \dfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\sin(\omega_{d}t)$.

**Step 9 — Final time-domain response:**

$$
\boxed{\,c(t) = 1 - e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}\left[\cos(\omega_{d} t) + \frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d} t)\right]\,}
$$

This can be combined into a single sinusoid using the identity $A\cos\theta + B\sin\theta = \sqrt{A^{2} + B^{2}}\sin(\theta + \phi)$:

$$
c(t) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin\left(\omega_{d} t + \phi\right), \quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Overshoot and Peak Time

**Setting up the derivative.** Overshoot occurs at the first peak, where $\dfrac{dc}{dt} = 0$.

$$
\frac{dc}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d} t + \phi)\right] = 0
$$

This requires $\sin(\omega_{d} t + \phi) = 0$, i.e., $\omega_{d} t + \phi = n\pi$. The **first peak** corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$
\omega_{d} t_{p} + \phi = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{p} = \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}}
$$

For underdamped systems, $\phi$ is small and often ignored for large $\omega_{n}$, giving the famous approximation:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\,}
$$

**Substituting $t = t_{p}$ into $c(t)$:**

$$
c(t_{p}) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin(\pi) = 1
$$

Wait — $\sin(\pi) = 0$? Yes, but the *envelope* $\dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}$ is what defines the peak. The peak of the **envelope** is at $n = 1$ of the *shifted* form. Recomputing directly:

The maximum value of $c(t)$ is reached at the first instant the sinusoid passes through zero, where the magnitude of the decaying term is largest. Plugging $t_{p} = \pi/\omega_{d}$ and simplifying:

$$
M_{p} = c(t_{p}) - 1 = -\frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}\pi/\omega_{d}}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin(\pi + \phi)
$$

Since $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin\phi = -\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$:

$$
M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\boxed{\,\%M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\,}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Settling Time (2 % Criterion)

The response stays within 2 % of the final value when the envelope decays to $\pm 2\,\%$:

$$
\frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{s}}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}} = 0.02
$$

Assuming $\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} \approx 1$ (valid for moderate $\zeta$):

$$
e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{s}} = 0.02
$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$
-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{s} = \ln(0.02) = -3.912
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{s} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}\,}
$$

Similarly, the 5 % criterion gives $t_{s} \approx 3/(\zeta\omega_{n})$.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A second-order system has $\omega_{n} = 4$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.5$. Compute $t_{r},\, t_{p},\, t_{s},\, \%M_{p}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 4\sqrt{1 - 0.25} = 4\sqrt{0.75} = 3.464 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak time:**

$$
t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416}{3.464} = 0.907 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Rise time:** with $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = \pi/3 = 1.047$ rad,

$$
t_{r} = \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416 - 1.047}{3.464} = \frac{2.0946}{3.464} = 0.605 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Settling time (2%):**

$$
t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \frac{4}{0.5 \times 4} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage overshoot:**

$$
\%M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100 = e^{-0.5\pi/\sqrt{0.75}} \times 100 = e^{-1.814} \times 100 = 16.3\,\%
$$

**Answer:** $t_{p} = 0.907$ s, $t_{r} = 0.605$ s, $t_{s} = 2.0$ s, $\%M_{p} = 16.3\,\%$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 First-Order System Response Flow (Step & Ramp)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Signal Selector] --> A1{Choose Test Input}
    A1 -- Unit Step r of t equals 1 --> A2[R of s equals 1 by s]
    A1 -- Unit Ramp r of t equals t --> A3[R of s equals 1 by s squared]
    A2 --> A4[First Order Transfer Function K by T s plus 1]
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Output C of s]
    A5 -- Step --> A6[Inverse Laplace]
    A5 -- Ramp --> A7[Inverse Laplace]
    A6 --> A8[c of t equals K times 1 minus e to power minus t by T]
    A7 --> A9[c of t equals K t minus K T plus K T e to power minus t by T]
    A8 --> A10[Steady state value K]
    A9 --> A11[Steady state error equals T for K equals 1]
```

## 4.2 Second-Order System Damping Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Second Order System Analysis] --> B1{Damping Ratio Zeta}
    B1 -- Zeta equals 0 --> B2[Undamped]
    B1 -- 0 less than Zeta less than 1 --> B3[Underdamped]
    B1 -- Zeta equals 1 --> B4[Critically Damped]
    B1 -- Zeta greater than 1 --> B5[Overdamped]
    B2 --> B6[Sustained Oscillation at Omega n]
    B3 --> B7[Decaying Oscillation with Overshoot]
    B4 --> B8[Fastest Non Oscillatory Response]
    B5 --> B9[Slow Exponential Rise Two Time Constants]
    B3 --> B10[Compute t r t p t s M p]
    B10 --> B11[Rise Time pi minus phi by omega d]
    B10 --> B12[Peak Time pi by omega d]
    B10 --> B13[Settling Time 4 by zeta omega n]
    B10 --> B14[Overshoot e power minus zeta pi by root of 1 minus zeta squared times 100]
```

## 4.3 Standard Test Signal Catalogue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Standard Test Signals Catalogue] --> C1[Step r of t equals A]
    C0 --> C2[Ramp r of t equals A t]
    C0 --> C3[Impulse r of t equals delta of t]
    C0 --> C4[Parabolic r of t equals A t squared by 2]
    C1 --> C1a[Laplace A by s]
    C2 --> C2a[Laplace A by s squared]
    C3 --> C3a[Laplace A]
    C4 --> C4a[Laplace A by s cubed]
    C1a --> D0[Applied to First and Second Order Systems]
    C2a --> D0
    C3a --> D0
    C4a --> D0
```

## 4.4 Transient Response Specification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Step Response of Underdamped Second Order System] --> E1[Specifications Block]
    E1 --> E1a[Delay Time t d equals 1 plus 0 point 7 zeta by omega n]
    E1 --> E1b[Rise Time t r equals pi minus phi by omega d]
    E1 --> E1c[Peak Time t p equals pi by omega d]
    E1 --> E1d[Maximum Overshoot M p percent formula]
    E1 --> E1e[Settling Time t s equals 4 by zeta omega n for 2 percent]
    E1 --> E1f[Settling Time t s equals 3 by zeta omega n for 5 percent]
    E0 --> E2[Steady State Response]
    E2 --> E2a[Steady State Error e ss equals 2 zeta by omega n for unit ramp]
    E2 --> E2b[Steady State Value equals 1 for unit step]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define "time constant" of a first-order system and state its significance.

**Model Answer:**
The **time constant T** of a first-order system is defined as the time required for the step response to reach **63.2 %** of its final steady-state value. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the pole location of the transfer function $G(s) = K/(Ts + 1)$, i.e., the pole is at $s = -1/T$. It is a measure of the **speed of response** — a smaller $T$ means a faster system, while a larger $T$ means a slower system. After $t = 4T$, the response is within **2 %** of its final value.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is meant by "damping ratio" in a second-order system? List the four cases based on its value.

**Model Answer:**
The **damping ratio $\zeta$** is a dimensionless parameter that compares the actual damping in a second-order system to the critical damping value. It is defined as $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ in a mass-spring-damper, where $c$ is the damping coefficient, $k$ is the spring stiffness, and $m$ is the mass.

The four cases are:
1. **$\zeta = 0$** — Undamped (sustained oscillation)
2. **$0 < \zeta < 1$** — Underdamped (decaying oscillation)
3. **$\zeta = 1$** — Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory)
4. **$\zeta > 1$** — Overdamped (slow exponential rise)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the unit step response of a first-order system whose transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts + 1}$. Sketch the response and mark the time-constant point. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A first-order system has $K = 2$ and $T = 0.5$ s. Find the steady-state value, the value of output at $t = 0.5$ s, and the steady-state error when subjected to a unit ramp input. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Input and Output in s-domain:**

$$
R(s) = \frac{1}{s}, \quad C(s) = G(s) R(s) = \frac{K}{s(Ts + 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial fractions:**

$$
\frac{K}{s(Ts+1)} = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K}{s + 1/T}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Laplace:**

$$
\boxed{\,c(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/T}\right), \quad t \geq 0\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch:** A smooth exponential rise from 0 to $K$, crossing $0.632\,K$ at $t = T$, and $0.982\,K$ at $t = 4T$.

```
c(t)
  |
K | - - - - - - - - - - - - -  (Final value = K)
  |              ___---
0.982K |------/--          ← 4T point (2% band)
  |     /
0.632K|----/                ← T point (63.2%)
  |   /
  |  /
  | /
  |/__________________________ t
  0    T    2T   3T   4T
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

Given $K = 2$, $T = 0.5$ s.

**Steady-state value for unit step:**

$$
c(\infty) = K = 2
$$

**Output at $t = 0.5$ s for unit step:**

$$
c(0.5) = 2\left(1 - e^{-0.5/0.5}\right) = 2(1 - e^{-1}) = 2(1 - 0.3679) = 2 \times 0.6321 = 1.2642
$$

**Steady-state error for unit ramp:** The error formula derived earlier gives $e_{ss} = T/K_{\text{velocity path}}$; for a first-order system $G(s) = K/(Ts+1)$ with unit ramp:

$$
c(t) = K(t - T) + KT\,e^{-t/T} \quad \text{(transient dies out, leaving } K(t-T)\text{)}
$$

$$
e(t) = t - K(t - T) = t(1 - K) + KT
$$

For $K = 2$:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty}\big[t(1 - 2) + 2 \times 0.5\big] = \lim_{t \to \infty}\big[-t + 1\big] \to -\infty
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A first-order system with $K > 1$ will have the output *grow faster* than the ramp — leading to a *negative infinite* error. This is a classic KTU trap!** When $K = 1$, $e_{ss} = T$. Always check the value of $K$ before quoting the standard result.

In this case, with $K = 2$, the system actually *overshoots* the ramp. For the conventional KTU question pattern where $K = 1$:

$$
e_{ss} = T = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Derive the unit step response of an underdamped second-order system with transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$, and clearly state the expressions for rise time, peak time, settling time, and percentage overshoot. (8 Marks)

**(b)** A unity-feedback control system has an open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{25}{s(s + 3)}$. Determine the closed-loop damping ratio, natural frequency, percentage overshoot, peak time, and 2 % settling time for a unit step input. (6 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
c(t) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin\left(\omega_{d} t + \phi\right), \quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)
$$

Specifications:

$$
t_{r} = \frac{\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta}{\omega_{d}}, \quad t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}, \quad t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} \text{ (2 % band)}, \quad \%M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{25}{s^{2} + 3s + 25}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the standard form $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:**

$$
\omega_{n}^{2} = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{n} = 5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
2\zeta\omega_{n} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{3}{2 \times 5} = 0.3
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage overshoot:**

$$
\%M_{p} = e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{1 - 0.09}} \times 100 = e^{-0.9425/0.9539} \times 100 = e^{-0.9880} \times 100 = 37.2\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 5\sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 5 \times 0.9539 = 4.770 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Peak time:**

$$
t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416}{4.770} = 0.659 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Settling time (2 %):**

$$
t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \frac{4}{0.3 \times 5} = \frac{4}{1.5} = 2.667 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\omega_{n} = 5$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.3$, $\%M_{p} = 37.2\,\%$, $t_{p} = 0.659$ s, $t_{s} = 2.667$ s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Misidentifying $\omega_{n}$:** Always square the constant term of the denominator — a frequent error is reading $\omega_{n}$ directly as the coefficient of $s$ or the leading constant.
> 2. **Wrong band for $t_{s}$:** The default in KTU papers is the **2 % criterion** ($t_s = 4/\zeta\omega_n$); only use $3/\zeta\omega_n$ if the question *explicitly* says 5 %.
> 3. **Forgetting $\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$** in the overshoot exponent — this costs a full mark in step-by-step valuation.
> 4. **Overshoot in % not fraction:** Always multiply by 100 in the final answer for $M_{p}$.
> 5. **Ramp steady-state error for first-order:** Students frequently write $e_{ss} = 0$ for ramp — this is **wrong**; the correct value is $T$ (when $K = 1$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Time Response**

- **Standard Test Signals:** Step ($1/s$), Ramp ($1/s^{2}$), Impulse ($1$), Parabolic ($1/s^{3}$). Unit-input versions are most common in KTU papers.
- **First-Order TF:** $G(s) = K/(Ts+1)$ — one energy-storage element only.
- **First-Order Step Response:** $c(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/T})$ — exponential, no overshoot.
- **First-Order Ramp Response:** $c(t) = K(t - T) + KTe^{-t/T}$ — **steady-state error = T (for K = 1)**.
- **Time Constant Significance:** $c(T) = 0.632 K$, $c(4T) = 0.982 K$ (2 % settling), $c(3T) = 0.950 K$ (5 % settling).
- **Second-Order Standard TF:** $G(s) = \omega_{n}^{2}/(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2})$.
- **Damping Cases:** $\zeta = 0$ undamped, $0 < \zeta < 1$ underdamped, $\zeta = 1$ critically damped, $\zeta > 1$ overdamped.
- **Underdamped Step Response:** $c(t) = 1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d} t + \phi)$, $\phi = \cos^{-1}\zeta$.
- **Critical Specifications:** $t_{r}$, $t_{p}$, $t_{s}$, $M_{p}$ — memorize all four formulas.
- **Overshoot depends only on $\zeta$** — not on $\omega_{n}$. Higher $\zeta$ → lower overshoot.
- **Settling time** depends on $\zeta\omega_{n}$ product (called the *damping factor* or *real part of pole*).
- **Ramp Error of Second-Order:** $e_{ss} = 2\zeta/\omega_{n}$ (velocity error constant $K_{v} = \omega_{n}/(2\zeta)$).
- **Closed-loop extraction:** For unity feedback, $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$; always normalise denominator to $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}$ form.
- **DC Motor Position Control** is the most commonly cited *real-world* example of a second-order system in KTU viva questions.
- **Final exam tip:** Always quote the *band* (2 % or 5 %) for $t_{s}$ and *units* (rad/s, s, %) for every answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Linear Control Systems: Concept of BIBO Stability, Routh-Hurwitz Criterion, Root Locus Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability of Linear Control Systems: Concept of BIBO Stability, Routh-Hurwitz Criterion, Root Locus Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability — The Fundamental Concern in Control Systems

In every closed-loop control system designed for industrial drives, robotics, aerospace vehicles, or process automation, the **primary design objective** is **stability**. A control system is said to be stable only if its output remains bounded and reaches a desired steady state for any bounded input — without exhibiting sustained or growing oscillations. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this property is formally termed **BIBO Stability** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The student is expected to understand (a) the formal definition of BIBO stability, (b) the Routh-Hurwitz algebraic criterion for absolute stability, and (c) the Root Locus graphical technique to study stability as system parameters vary.

---

## 1.2 BIBO Stability — Formal Definition

A **linear time-invariant (LTI)** system is said to be **BIBO stable** if and only if every bounded input applied to the system produces a bounded output. Mathematically, if $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M_u < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$, then the corresponding output $y(t)$ must also satisfy $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$.

For an LTI system characterized by its impulse response $h(t)$, BIBO stability is equivalent to the condition that $h(t)$ is **absolutely integrable** over the interval $[0, \infty)$.

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

In the **s-domain**, this condition translates into a strict placement of all poles of the closed-loop transfer function: **every pole must lie strictly in the open left-half of the s-plane (LHP)**. That is, the **real part of every pole must be strictly negative** ($\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pole Location Rule for BIBO Stability:**
> - All poles in Left-Half Plane (LHP) $\Rightarrow$ **Stable**
> - Simple (non-repeated) poles on the $j\omega$-axis $\Rightarrow$ **Marginally Stable (Critically Stable)**
> - Any pole in the Right-Half Plane (RHP) or repeated poles on $j\omega$-axis $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable**

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balanced Glass" Picture

Imagine balancing a tall, thin glass of water on a tray.

| Scenario | Analogy | Control System Equivalent |
|----------|---------|---------------------------|
| Glass upright, small tilt, returns upright | Stable pendulum | Output settles to a steady value |
| Glass oscillates forever, never falls | Marginally stable | Sustained sinusoidal oscillations (undamped) |
| Glass tilts more and eventually topples | Unstable | Output grows without bound (exponential divergence) |

A **stable control system** is like the first glass: when disturbed, it returns to equilibrium. An **unstable system** is like the third glass: even a small disturbance causes the response to grow uncontrollably until something "breaks" (in the drive, this could mean motor runaway, insulation breakdown, or catastrophic mechanical failure).

---

## 1.4 Why Stability is Critical in Electrical Drives

In a DC or AC drive, instability may manifest as:

- **Current oscillations** leading to IGBT/MOSFET thermal runaway.
- **Speed hunt** — the motor speed keeps oscillating around the set-point without settling.
- **Torque pulsations** causing mechanical resonance in the load coupling.
- **Voltage collapse** in DC link during regenerative braking.

Hence, before implementing any compensator (PI, PID, lead, lag), the engineer **must** verify that the closed-loop system is stable. This is the entry-point topic of Module 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants / Metrics Highlighted for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Marginal stability frequency** $\omega_n$ (rad/s) — the imaginary part of poles on the $j\omega$-axis.
> - **Characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — the master equation whose roots dictate stability.
> - **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** — quantified in dB and degrees (covered later in frequency-response methods, Module 5).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Characteristic Equation — Heart of Stability Analysis

For a unity-feedback closed-loop system with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback $H(s)$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

The **characteristic equation (CE)** is obtained by setting the denominator to zero:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

Let the expanded form be:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

The **n roots** of this equation are the **closed-loop poles**. The system is stable **if and only if all n roots have negative real parts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Necessary (but not sufficient) conditions for stability — Routh's First Test:**
> 1. All coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ must be **real** and **positive** (no missing terms, no negative coefficients).
> 2. The polynomial must be of proper degree with no zero coefficient (no missing power of $s$).
> If even one coefficient is zero or negative, the system is **unstable** by inspection.

---

## 2.2 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion — Algebraic Stability Test

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a **systematic algebraic procedure** to determine the number of closed-loop poles lying in the Right-Half Plane (RHP) — without actually computing the roots. It is one of the most frequently tested topics in KTU university exams.

### 2.2.1 Construction of the Routh Array

For a CE of order $n$:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + a_3 s^{n-3} + \cdots = 0$$

The Routh array is built as follows:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | ... |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| $s^n$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ | ... |
| $s^{n-1}$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $a_5$ | ... |
| $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ | ... |
| $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | ... |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| $s^0$ | $a_n$ | | | |

where the elements are computed as:

$$b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \qquad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}, \qquad b_3 = \frac{a_1 a_6 - a_0 a_7}{a_1}$$

$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}, \qquad c_2 = \frac{b_1 a_5 - a_1 b_3}{b_1}$$

In general, each element of a row is obtained by:

$$\text{Element} = \frac{(\text{First element of previous row}) \cdot (\text{Element above to the right}) - (\text{First element of row above that}) \cdot (\text{Element two rows above to the right})}{\text{First element of the previous row}}$$

### 2.2.2 Stability Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Theorem:**
> The number of roots of the characteristic equation with positive real parts equals the **number of sign changes** in the **first column** of the Routh array.
> - **Zero sign changes** $\Rightarrow$ No RHP roots $\Rightarrow$ **Stable**
> - **One or more sign changes** $\Rightarrow$ Corresponding number of RHP roots $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable**

### 2.2.3 Special Cases in Routh Array

**Case 1: First column element becomes zero (but the row is not all zeros).**
- Replace the zero with a small positive number $\epsilon \to 0^+$ and continue.
- Sign changes are determined by the signs in the limit.

**Case 2: An entire row becomes zero.**
- This indicates **symmetrically placed roots** about the origin (e.g., $\pm j\omega$, $\pm \sigma$, etc.).
- Form the **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ from the row above the zero row.
- Take $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$ and replace the zero row with its coefficients.
- Proceed with the array.

### 2.2.4 Finding the Range of Gain $K$ for Stability

Often, the CE contains a gain parameter $K$ (e.g., $s^3 + 3s^2 + 5s + K = 0$). Using the Routh array, one can find the **range of $K$** for which the system remains stable. The boundary condition is when the first column has a **zero** (marginal stability). Solving for $K$ at this boundary gives the **critical gain**.

---

## 2.3 Root Locus — Graphical Stability Analysis

The **Root Locus** is the locus (path) traced by the closed-loop poles in the s-plane as a **single system parameter** (typically the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $\infty$.

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Foundation

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$, which can be rewritten as:

$$KG(s)H(s) = -1$$

This yields two simultaneous conditions — the **magnitude criterion** and the **angle criterion**:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm (2q + 1) \cdot 180^\circ, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

$$\vert KG(s)H(s) \vert = 1$$

The **angle criterion** is the locus-defining condition (independent of $K$), while the **magnitude criterion** is used to find the value of $K$ at any point on the locus.

### 2.3.2 Rules for Sketching the Root Locus

Let $n$ = number of open-loop poles, $m$ = number of open-loop zeros, and $n - m$ = number of asymptotes.

| # | Rule | Formula / Statement |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Locus on real axis | To the **left** of an **odd** number of real-axis poles + zeros (combined). |
| 2 | Number of branches | Equal to number of poles $n$. |
| 3 | Symmetry | About the real axis (complex roots occur in conjugate pairs). |
| 4 | Starting & Ending points | Start at poles ($K=0$); end at zeros ($K=\infty$); the remaining $n-m$ branches go to infinity. |
| 5 | Asymptote angles | $\theta_k = \dfrac{(2q+1)180^\circ}{n-m}, \quad q = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$ |
| 6 | Centroid (intersection of asymptotes) | $\sigma = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{n - m}$ |
| 7 | Breakaway / Break-in points | Roots of $\dfrac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)] = 0$ that lie on the locus (and satisfy angle criterion). |
| 8 | $j\omega$-axis crossing | Use Routh array (auxiliary polynomial) to find $\omega$ and the critical $K$. |
| 9 | Departure angle from complex pole | $\theta_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle \text{zeros} - \sum \angle \text{other poles}$ |
| 10 | Arrival angle at complex zero | $\theta_a = 180^\circ - \sum \angle \text{poles} + \sum \angle \text{other zeros}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Asymptote Angles:** Odd multiples of $180°/(n-m)$ — i.e., 45°, 135°, 225°, 315° for $n-m = 4$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-----|
| 1 | BIBO stability | $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ | Test absolute integrability of impulse response |
| 2 | Pole-placement rule | All $\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$ | Quick test for LTI stability |
| 3 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Source of closed-loop poles |
| 4 | Routh array — first element of new row | $b_j = \dfrac{a_1 a_{2j} - a_0 a_{2j+1}}{a_1}$ | Build the Routh table |
| 5 | Sign changes | = Number of RHP poles | Direct stability verdict |
| 6 | Critical gain | First column entry = 0 in Routh array | Boundary of stability |
| 7 | Auxiliary polynomial | Formed from row above zero row | Handle row-of-zeros case |
| 8 | Root locus angle condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm (2q+1)180°$ | Define locus points |
| 9 | Root locus magnitude condition | $\vert KG(s)H(s) \vert = 1$ | Find $K$ at any point on locus |
| 10 | Asymptote angles | $(2q+1)180°/(n-m)$ | Sketch asymptote directions |
| 11 | Centroid | $(\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros})/(n-m)$ | Intersection point of asymptotes |
| 12 | Breakaway points | Roots of $\frac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)] = 0$ on the locus | Points where branches split/merge |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Techniques

- **Drive speed controllers** — designers use the root locus to select proportional gain $K_p$ so that dominant closed-loop poles yield a desired damping ratio $\zeta$ (typically 0.6–0.7 for minimal overshoot).
- **Process control (chemical, petrochemical)** — Routh-Hurwitz quickly tells the engineer whether the loop can be closed at all, and the maximum permissible loop gain.
- **Aerospace autopilots** — both techniques are used in preliminary design to satisfy stringent stability margins before non-linear simulations.
- **Power electronic converters** — stability of voltage-mode or current-mode control loops is verified using these tools before PCB prototyping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of BIBO Stability and Pole Placement in LHP

For an LTI system with transfer function $H(s)$ and impulse response $h(t)$, the response to an input $u(t)$ is given by the convolution integral:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(\tau) \, u(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

Taking absolute values and applying the **triangle inequality**:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, \vert u(t - \tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

If $u(t)$ is bounded, i.e., $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M_u$ for all $t \geq 0$, then:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_u \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau \leq M_u \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

Hence, $y(t)$ remains bounded for all $t$ if and only if:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau < \infty$$

This is the **absolute integrability** condition. Now, by partial fraction expansion, if all poles $p_i$ of $H(s)$ are distinct with negative real parts ($\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$), then $h(t) = \sum A_i e^{p_i t}$ (for $t \geq 0$), and each term decays exponentially, so the integral converges. Conversely, if any pole has $\text{Re}(p_i) \geq 0$, the corresponding exponential term either grows or persists, making the integral diverge.

**Conclusion:** BIBO stability $\iff$ All poles in open LHP.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Routh Array Element Formula

Consider two consecutive rows of the Routh array with first-column elements $a, b$ and second-column elements $c, d$ (with $a$ being in the row immediately above the row being computed).

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| Above-row-2 | $a$ | $c$ | $e$ |
| Above-row-1 | $b$ | $d$ | $f$ |
| Current row | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ |

The element $b_1$ is computed by determinant-like operation:

$$b_1 = \frac{b \cdot c - a \cdot d}{b}$$

**Derivation logic:** The Routh array is constructed so that each new row is orthogonal (in a determinant sense) to the two rows above it when dotted with the polynomial coefficients. This orthogonality is the algebraic mechanism that encodes the Hurwitz determinants — ensuring sign-change counting works correctly.

> For a third-order CE $a_0 s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3 = 0$, the explicit Routh array is:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $b_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $c_1$ | 0 | 0 |

where:

$$b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \qquad c_1 = a_3$$

For stability: $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3 > 0$ **and** $b_1 > 0$, which gives:

$$a_1 a_2 > a_0 a_3 \quad \text{(Hurwitz condition for order 3)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Routh-Hurwitz for Gain Range

**Problem:** Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system with characteristic equation

$$s^4 + 3s^3 + 5s^2 + 4s + K = 0$$

is stable.

**Step 1: Build the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 5 | $K$ |
| $s^3$ | 3 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $c_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2: Compute $b_1$ and $b_2$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{3 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 4}{3} = \frac{15 - 4}{3} = \frac{11}{3}$$

$$b_2 = \frac{3 \cdot K - 1 \cdot 0}{3} = K$$

**Step 3: Compute $c_1$.**

$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot 4 - 3 \cdot b_2}{b_1} = \frac{\frac{11}{3} \cdot 4 - 3K}{\frac{11}{3}} = \frac{\frac{44}{3} - 3K}{\frac{11}{3}} = \frac{44 - 9K}{11}$$

**Step 4: Apply the stability condition (all first-column entries > 0).**

$$\begin{aligned}
&1 > 0 \quad \checkmark \\
&3 > 0 \quad \checkmark \\
&\frac{11}{3} > 0 \quad \checkmark \\
&c_1 = \frac{44 - 9K}{11} > 0 \Rightarrow 44 - 9K > 0 \Rightarrow K < \frac{44}{9} \approx 4.89 \\
&K > 0 \quad (\text{from the } s^0 \text{ row}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{0 < K < \frac{44}{9} \approx 4.89}$$

At $K = 44/9$, the system is marginally stable. The auxiliary polynomial from the $s^2$ row is:

$$A(s) = b_1 s^2 + b_2 = \frac{11}{3} s^2 + \frac{44}{9} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 = -\frac{4}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$$

So the system oscillates at $\omega = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$ rad/s at critical gain.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Root Locus Sketch for a Second-Order System

**System:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)}$, $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback).

**Step 1: Identify poles and zeros.**

- Open-loop poles: $s = 0$, $s = -2$ ($n = 2$).
- Open-loop zeros: none ($m = 0$).
- Number of branches: $n = 2$.

**Step 2: Asymptotes.**

$$n - m = 2 \Rightarrow \text{Asymptote angles} = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{2} = 90°, \; 270°$$

**Step 3: Centroid.**

$$\sigma = \frac{(0) + (-2) - 0}{2} = -1$$

Asymptotes are vertical lines passing through $s = -1$.

**Step 4: Real-axis segments.**

Locus exists on the real axis to the left of an odd number of poles + zeros. The segment between $s = 0$ and $s = -2$ has **1 pole to the left** (odd) $\Rightarrow$ locus exists on $[-2, 0]$.

**Step 5: Breakaway point.**

Set $\dfrac{d}{ds}\left[\dfrac{1}{G(s)}\right] = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left[s(s+2)\right] = \frac{d}{ds}[s^2 + 2s] = 2s + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1$$

So branches break away at $s = -1$ and head vertically toward the asymptotes.

**Step 6: Sketch.**

The two branches start at the poles $0$ and $-2$, move toward each other on the real axis, meet at the breakaway point $s = -1$, and then depart vertically (perpendicular to the real axis) toward infinity along the asymptotes $\text{Re}(s) = -1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Inference from Root Locus:** As $K$ increases from 0, the closed-loop poles move from open-loop positions into the LHP interior. They cross the imaginary axis at $K = 1$ (the value at which characteristic equation $s^2 + 2s + K = 0$ has imaginary roots $s = \pm j\sqrt{2}$). For $K > 1$, both poles are in the LHP $\Rightarrow$ **stable**. For $K < 1$, the poles are real and in the LHP (one positive, one more negative) $\Rightarrow$ also stable. For $K = 1$, marginally stable. This is a **conditionally stable** system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: BIBO Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: LTI System H s]) --> poles[Find all closed loop poles]
    poles --> chk1{All Re s less than 0?}
    chk1 -- Yes --> stable[STABLE BIBO]
    chk1 -- No --> chk2{Any Re s greater than 0?}
    chk2 -- Yes --> unstable[UNSTABLE]
    chk2 -- No --> chk3{Repeated poles on jw axis?}
    chk3 -- Yes --> unstable2[UNSTABLE]
    chk3 -- No --> marginal[MARGINALLY STABLE sustained oscillation]
    start --> integral[Compute integral of absolute h t]
    integral --> intchk{Less than infinity?}
    intchk -- Yes --> stable
    intchk -- No --> unstable
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Routh-Hurwitz Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1([Step 1 Form CE of order n]) --> s2[Step 2 Verify all coefficients positive]
    s2 --> chk{Missing or negative coefficient?}
    chk -- Yes --> unst[UNSTABLE by inspection]
    chk -- No --> s3[Step 3 Construct Routh array]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Compute entries using determinant formula]
    s4 --> s5{First column element equals zero?}
    s5 -- Yes --> s6[Step 6 Use epsilon or auxiliary polynomial]
    s5 -- No --> s7[Step 7 Count sign changes in first column]
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8{Any sign change?}
    s8 -- No --> stab[STABLE]
    s8 -- Yes --> nRHP[Number of sign changes equals number of RHP poles UNSTABLE]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Root Locus Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a([Start Open loop Gs Hs]) --> b[Identify poles n and zeros m]
    b --> c[Plot real axis locus left of odd count]
    c --> d[Compute asymptote angles 2q+1 times 180 by n minus m]
    d --> e[Compute centroid sigma sum of poles minus sum of zeros divided by n minus m]
    e --> f[Find breakaway break in points derivative equals zero]
    f --> g[Compute jw axis crossing using Routh]
    g --> h[Compute departure angles from complex poles]
    h --> i[Sketch complete root locus]
    i --> j[Mark stable region left half plane and find K at any point]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Analysis Workflow in Drive Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Plant Model Gs]
        A2[Sensor Model Hs]
        A3[Gain K to be tuned]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS
        B1[Routh Hurwitz Array]
        B2[Root Locus Sketch]
        B3[Step Response Simulation]
    end
    subgraph DECISION
        C1{Stable?}
        C2[Retune K]
        C3[Add Compensator]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        D1[Final Controller Parameters]
        D2[Stability Margins GM PM]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 -- No --> C2
    C2 --> B1
    C1 -- No --> C3
    C3 --> B1
    C1 -- Yes --> B3
    B3 --> D1
    B3 --> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** In a real-world KTU-style drive design problem, the engineer iteratively uses Routh-Hurwitz (for a quick yes/no stability check) and Root Locus (to choose $K$ for a desired transient response) in tandem, before resorting to time-domain simulation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define BIBO stability. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a linear time-invariant system to be BIBO stable in terms of its impulse response and closed-loop poles.**

**Model Answer:**
BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability means that for every bounded input signal applied to the system, the output remains bounded.

**Condition (Impulse response):** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response $h(t)$ is absolutely integrable:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

**Condition (Pole placement):** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if **all** the closed-loop poles lie strictly in the **left-half of the s-plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$ for every pole $s_i$). Poles on the $j\omega$-axis give marginal stability, and any pole in the RHP makes the system unstable.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. How is the range of gain $K$ for stability determined using this criterion?**

**Model Answer:**
The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is an algebraic method that determines the number of roots of the characteristic equation lying in the right-half of the s-plane — without explicitly solving for the roots.

**Procedure:**
1. Form the characteristic equation $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ in terms of the gain $K$.
2. Construct the Routh array.
3. The number of sign changes in the **first column** of the Routh array equals the number of poles in the RHP.
4. For stability, there must be **zero sign changes**.
5. To find the range of $K$, set up the inequalities that require every first-column entry to be positive. The boundary case (first-column element = 0) gives the critical value of $K$.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** The characteristic equation of a closed-loop control system is given by:
$$s^5 + s^4 + 2s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 5 = 0$$
Determine the stability of the system using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Comment on the number of roots in the right-half s-plane.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Build the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^5$ | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $s^4$ | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $d_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $e_1$ | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2: Compute $b_1$ and $b_2$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(1)(2) - (1)(2)}{1} = 0$$

A zero has appeared in the first column. Apply the auxiliary polynomial method.

**Step 3: Form auxiliary polynomial from $s^4$ row.**

$$A(s) = 1 \cdot s^4 + 2 s^2 + 5$$

**Step 4: Differentiate.**

$$\frac{dA(s)}{ds} = 4s^3 + 4s$$

Replace the $s^3$ row with the coefficients of $\frac{dA}{ds}$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^5$ | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $s^4$ | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $d_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $e_1$ | 0 | 0 |

**Step 5: Compute $c_1$ and $c_2$.**

$$c_1 = \frac{(4)(2) - (1)(4)}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1$$

$$c_2 = \frac{(4)(5) - (1)(0)}{4} = 5$$

**Step 6: Compute $d_1$.**

$$d_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (4)(5)}{1} = 4 - 20 = -16$$

**Step 7: Compute $e_1$.**

$$e_1 = \frac{(-16)(5) - (1)(0)}{-16} = 5$$

**Step 8: First column summary.**

$1, \; 1, \; 4, \; 1, \; -16, \; 5$

**Step 9: Count sign changes.**

$+\to+$ : no change
$+\to+$ : no change
$+\to+$ : no change
$+\to+$ : no change
$+\to-$ : **1st sign change**
$-\to+$ : **2nd sign change**

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable** with **2 poles in the right-half s-plane**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For the system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$
sketch the root locus and find the value of $K$ and the frequency $\omega$ at which the locus crosses the imaginary axis.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify poles and zeros.**
- Poles: $s = 0, \; -1, \; -2$ ($n = 3$)
- Zeros: none ($m = 0$)
- Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3$

**Step 2: Asymptote angles.**

$$\theta_k = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3}, \; q = 0, 1, 2 \Rightarrow \theta = 60°, \; 180°, \; 300°$$

**Step 3: Centroid.**

$$\sigma = \frac{(0) + (-1) + (-2)}{3} = -1$$

**Step 4: Real axis segments.**

Locus exists on the real axis between $s = 0$ and $s = -1$ (1 pole to the left, odd) and between $s = -2$ and $-\infty$ (3 poles to the left, odd).

**Step 5: Breakaway point between $s=0$ and $s=-1$.**

Set $\dfrac{d}{ds}[s(s+1)(s+2)] = 0$:

$$s(s+1)(s+2) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s$$

$$\frac{d}{ds}[s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s] = 3s^2 + 6s + 2 = 0$$

$$s = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{6} = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{12}}{6} = -1 \pm 0.577$$

Acceptable breakaway on the locus: $s = -1 + 0.577 = -0.423$ (lies between 0 and -1).

The other root $s = -1.577$ lies on the segment $-2$ to $-\infty$ — it is a **break-in point**.

**Step 6: $j\omega$-axis crossing using Routh array.**

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 2 |
| $s^2$ | 3 | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{6-K}{3}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | 0 |

For marginal stability, the $s^1$ row must be zero: $6 - K = 0 \Rightarrow K = 6$.

Auxiliary polynomial: $3s^2 + K = 0 \Rightarrow 3s^2 + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -2 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 7: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{K_{\text{critical}} = 6, \quad \omega = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/s}}$$

The system is stable for $0 < K < 6$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the BIBO stability criterion for a linear time-invariant system. A system has transfer function
$$T(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s^2+2s+5)}$$
Determine if the system is BIBO stable. Justify your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: BIBO Stability Definition.**
A linear time-invariant system is BIBO stable if its impulse response is absolutely integrable, or equivalently, if all closed-loop poles have strictly negative real parts.

**Step 2: Find the poles of $T(s)$.**

Set the denominator to zero: $(s+1)(s^2+2s+5) = 0$

From $s+1 = 0$: $s = -1$.

From $s^2+2s+5 = 0$:
$$s = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 20}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-16}}{2} = -1 \pm j2$$

**Step 3: Examine the pole locations.**

| Pole | Real Part | Status |
|------|-----------|--------|
| $s_1 = -1$ | $-1 < 0$ | In LHP |
| $s_{2,3} = -1 \pm j2$ | $-1 < 0$ | In LHP |

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

All three poles lie in the open LHP. Hence the system is **BIBO stable**.

The impulse response is:
$$h(t) = A_1 e^{-t} + A_2 e^{-t}\sin(2t + \phi)$$
which decays to zero as $t \to \infty$, confirming absolute integrability.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$
sketch the root locus. Find (i) the breakaway point, (ii) the value of $K$ for marginal stability, and (iii) the frequency of oscillation at marginal stability.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Poles and zeros.**
- Poles: $s = 0, \; -1, \; -3$ ($n = 3$)
- Zeros: $s = -2$ ($m = 1$)
- Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 2$

**Step 2: Asymptote angles.**

$$\theta_k = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{2} = 90°, \; 270°$$

**Step 3: Centroid.**

$$\sigma = \frac{(0) + (-1) + (-3) - (-2)}{2} = \frac{-2}{2} = -1$$

Asymptotes are vertical lines through $s = -1$.

**Step 4: Real axis segments.**

- To the left of $s = 0$: 1 pole + 1 zero to the left of any point between $-\infty$ and $0$? Count on the segment $[-1, 0]$: 1 pole (at $-1$) is to the left — odd $\Rightarrow$ locus exists.
- On segment $[-2, -1]$: poles $-1, -3$ to the left and zero $-2$ — total 3 (odd) $\Rightarrow$ locus exists.
- On segment $[-3, -2]$: poles $-3$ to the left and zero $-2$ — total 2 (even) $\Rightarrow$ no locus.
- To the left of $-3$: 3 poles + 1 zero = 4 (even) $\Rightarrow$ no locus.

So real-axis locus spans $[-1, 0]$ and $[-2, -1]$ — branches move **toward each other** in this range.

**Step 5: Breakaway point.**

Set $\dfrac{d}{ds}\left[\dfrac{s(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)}\right] = 0$.

Let $N(s) = s(s+1)(s+3) = s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s$.

Let $D(s) = s+2$.

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{N(s)}{D(s)}\right] = \frac{N'(s) D(s) - N(s) D'(s)}{[D(s)]^2} = 0$$

This gives $N'(s) D(s) - N(s) D'(s) = 0$:

$$(3s^2 + 8s + 3)(s+2) - (s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s)(1) = 0$$

Expanding:
$$3s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s^2 + 16s + 3s + 6 - s^3 - 4s^2 - 3s = 0$$

$$2s^3 + 10s^2 + 16s + 6 = 0$$

$$s^3 + 5s^2 + 8s + 3 = 0$$

By inspection, $s = -1$ is a root: $(-1)^3 + 5(-1)^2 + 8(-1) + 3 = -1 + 5 - 8 + 3 = -1 \neq 0$. Try $s = -3$: $-27 + 45 - 24 + 3 = -3 \neq 0$. Use rational root theorem: $s = -1/2$? 

$-1/8 + 5/4 - 4 + 3 = -0.125 + 1.25 - 4 + 3 = 0.125$ — close.

Using numerical methods: one real root near $s \approx -0.65$ (lies in the locus segment $[-1, 0]$ — this is the **breakaway** point). The other two roots are complex.

**Step 6: $j\omega$-axis crossing (Routh array).**

CE: $s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + K(s+2) = 0$
$\Rightarrow s^3 + 4s^2 + (3+K)s + 2K = 0$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | 1 | $3+K$ |
| $s^2$ | 4 | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{4(3+K) - 2K}{4} = \frac{12 + 2K}{4}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | 0 |

For marginal stability: $12 + 2K = 0 \Rightarrow K = -6$ (negative — not physical for gain).

This implies the system is stable for **all** $K > 0$. The branches of the root locus do not cross into the RHP.

**Step 7: Final answers.**

(i) **Breakaway point:** $s \approx -0.65$ (numerical)
(ii) **Critical $K$:** No positive critical $K$ exists — system is stable for all $K > 0$.
(iii) **Frequency at marginal stability:** Not applicable (system never becomes marginally unstable for positive $K$).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Sign error in Routh array computation:** The formula is $\dfrac{(\text{First of upper row}) \times (\text{Second of lower row}) - (\text{First of lower row}) \times (\text{Second of upper row})}{\text{First of lower row}}$. Students often invert the subtraction order.
> 2. **Forgetting the auxiliary polynomial step:** When the first column becomes zero, the immediate substitution of $\epsilon$ is **only valid if the entire row is not all zero**. If the entire row is zero, **must** use the auxiliary polynomial from the row above.
> 3. **Centroid sign error:** The centroid is $\sigma = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{n - m}$. The minus sign is often missed.
> 4. **Asymptote angles:** Must be odd multiples of $180°/(n-m)$ — students often use even multiples.
> 5. **Breakaway point verification:** A root of $\dfrac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)] = 0$ is a valid breakaway point **only if it lies on a real-axis segment of the locus**. Always verify the location.
> 6. **Stability range:** When $K$ appears in the $s^0$ row, ensure $K > 0$ is included; otherwise, the analysis is incomplete.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **BIBO stability** = bounded input $\Rightarrow$ bounded output. Pole-placement test: all closed-loop poles must have **strictly negative real parts**.
- The **characteristic equation** is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Its roots are the closed-loop poles.
- **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** counts sign changes in the first column of the Routh array = number of RHP poles.
- **Routh's necessary conditions:** all CE coefficients must be real, positive, and none missing.
- **Auxiliary polynomial** method is used when a complete row of the Routh array becomes zero.
- **Root locus** traces closed-loop pole positions as $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.
- Root locus obeys the **angle criterion** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm(2q+1)180°$ and the **magnitude criterion** $\vert KG(s)H(s)\vert = 1$.
- Asymptote angles: $\theta_k = (2q+1)180°/(n-m)$.
- Centroid: $\sigma = (\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros})/(n-m)$.
- **Breakaway points:** roots of $\dfrac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)] = 0$ that lie on the locus.
- **$j\omega$-axis crossing** frequency and critical $K$ are found using the Routh array with the auxiliary polynomial at the marginal row.
- Real-axis locus: a point on the real axis belongs to the locus if the total number of real poles + zeros to its **right** is **odd**.
- For KTU problems, always present the Routh array as a **neat tabular structure** and explicitly state "$\therefore$ system is stable/unstable" at the end.
- For root locus, **always sketch** the locus with annotations of poles, zeros, asymptotes, centroid, and breakaway points — even a rough sketch earns partial marks.
- The two techniques are **complementary**: Routh-Hurwitz for a quick algebraic yes/no, root locus for the complete design of gain $K$ to achieve desired transient response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction, Properties and its Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT502 - ELECTRICAL DRIVES AND CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction, Properties and its Construction # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a train of input electrical pulses (digital command signals) into discrete angular mechanical movements, known as **"steps"**. The angular position of the rotor is directly proportional to the number of input pulses received, while the rotational speed is proportional to the frequency (or pulse rate) of the applied command signal. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular displacement called the **Step Angle (β)**. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRT502 - Electrical Drives and Control Systems), the stepper motor is classified as a **special-purpose electromechanical actuator** widely used in open-loop digital control systems, robotics, CNC machines, and precision positioning equipment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Standard 2-Mark Answer Format):** > "A stepper motor is a digital actuator which converts electrical pulses into discrete mechanical steps. The rotor moves through a fixed angle (step angle) for each pulse input, making it inherently compatible with digital control systems without requiring external feedback." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of a stepper motor like the **ratchet mechanism on a climbing harness** or the **click mechanism of a ballpoint pen's twist action**. Imagine you have a **6-sided die (cube)** mounted on a shaft. Each time you "tell" the motor to take one step (via one electrical pulse), the shaft rotates exactly **60°** — the die moves from face "1" to "2", then to "3", and so on. It never moves by 30.5° or 61.2°. It **only** stops at those exact six positions. This is the essence of a stepper motor: **discrete, repeatable, and exact angular increments.** In a real stepper motor, instead of a die, you have a toothed rotor and stator, and the "telling" is done by energizing electromagnetic coils in a specific sequence. The electromagnetic attraction between energized stator teeth and rotor teeth creates the discrete steps. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Stepper"?** The motor gets its name from the fact that it moves in **discrete steps** (one step per pulse) rather than continuous rotation like a conventional AC/DC motor. This makes it behave like a **"digital-to-analog (mechanical) converter."** ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following parameters are highlighted as high-frequency KTU exam content: - **Step Angle (β):** The angular displacement per pulse, measured in **degrees (°)**. Typical industrial values: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev), **0.9°** (400 steps/rev), **15°** (24 steps/rev), **7.5°** (48 steps/rev). - **Steps per Revolution (N):** Total number of discrete steps in one full 360° rotation. Calculated as: $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ - **Slew Rate:** The maximum pulse rate (steps/second) at which the motor can start, stop, or reverse without losing synchronization, typically **ranging from 200 to 20,000 pulses per second**. - **Holding Torque:** The maximum torque the motor can sustain while energized at standstill (DC current flowing, rotor locked), measured in **N·m**. - **Detent Torque:** The residual magnetic torque present in **Permanent Magnet (PM) and Hybrid** stepper motors when the windings are de-energized. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The construction, working principle, and the relationship between **stator teeth, rotor teeth, and step angle** are the most heavily tested concepts in this module. Master the formula $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ and its derivations thoroughly. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Stepper Motors Stepper motors possess several **distinctive properties** that make them uniquely suited for digital control applications. The following structured analysis covers the operational characteristics demanded by the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### 2.1.1 Discrete Angular Movement (Stepwise Rotation) The rotor moves in **fixed, quantized angular increments** rather than smooth continuous rotation. The size of each step is determined by the motor's construction (number of stator/rotor poles) and is independent of the load (within the motor's torque capacity). ### 2.1.2 Open-Loop Compatibility Stepper motors can operate **without position feedback sensors** (such as encoders or resolvers). The input pulse count inherently determines the rotor position, making the system **simpler, cheaper, and more reliable** than closed-loop servo systems. This is the single most important property for KTU exam answers. ### 2.1.3 Digital-to-Mechanical Conversion Each input electrical pulse directly corresponds to one mechanical step. The motor therefore acts as a **digital-to-mechanical transducer**, accepting **TTL/CMOS-compatible logic signals** directly from microcontrollers, PLCs, or stepper driver ICs. ### 2.1.4 High Holding Torque at Standstill Unlike conventional motors that may rotate freely when de-energized, an energized stepper motor can hold its rotor position against external loads up to its rated **holding torque**, without any mechanical brake or feedback system. ### 2.1.5 Excellent Low-Speed Torque Stepper motors deliver their **maximum torque at low speeds (or zero speed)**, making them ideal for applications requiring high torque at start-up and slow-speed positioning (e.g., 3D printer Z-axis, robotic arm joints). ### 2.1.6 Error Accumulation (Non-Correcting) In open-loop operation, if a pulse is missed due to excessive load or incorrect timing, the motor **does not know** the error occurred, and the position drift accumulates. This is a critical limitation. ### 2.1.7 Resonance and Detent Issues At certain pulse frequencies, stepper motors exhibit **mechanical resonance**, causing loss of torque and stalling. PM and hybrid types also have a small **detent torque** even when unenergized due to permanent magnet flux. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **high-yield cheat sheet** for solving any KTU 2024 numerical problem related to this topic. **Memorize this table.** | # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Applicable To | |---|---------|--------------------|-------|----------------| | 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Step angle, Total steps per revolution | degrees, integer | All stepper types | | 2 | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | Steps per revolution | integer | All stepper types | | 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper | degrees | VR stepper | | 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{2 \cdot N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Multi-stack VR stepper (m-stacks) | degrees | Multi-stack VR | | 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{180°}{N_s \cdot p}$ | Hybrid stepper (with rotor teeth) | degrees | Hybrid stepper | | 6 | $f = \dfrac{v}{\beta}$ | Pulse rate vs. shaft speed | Hz, deg/s, deg | All stepper types | | 7 | $n = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°}$ | Shaft speed in rev/s | rev/s | All stepper types | | 8 | $N = \text{res} = \text{steps/rev}$ | Resolution = steps per revolution | steps/rev | All stepper types | | 9 | $T_h = k_t \cdot I$ | Holding torque proportional to current | N·m | All stepper types | | 10 | $\theta_{total} = n_{pulses} \cdot \beta$ | Total angular displacement | degrees | All stepper types | Where: - $N_s$ = Number of stator teeth (poles) - $N_r$ = Number of rotor teeth - $p$ = Number of pole pairs - $f$ = Pulse frequency (Hz) - $v$ = Angular velocity (deg/s) - $n$ = Shaft rotational speed (rev/s) - $\beta$ = Step angle (degrees) - $T_h$ = Holding torque - $k_t$ = Torque constant - $I$ = Phase current > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the table above, all fraction delimiters, the multiplication dot `\cdot`, and degree symbols `°` are used **inside** `\dfrac{}` environments. This is safe from breaking the markdown table syntax. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Stepper motors are the **workhorse of digital motion control** across numerous industries: - **3D Printers & CNC Machines:** Precise X-Y-Z axis positioning with open-loop control. - **Robotics:** Joint articulation, gripper actuation, and wheel odometry. - **Hard Disk Drives (HDD):** Head positioning actuator (historically). - **Printers & Plotters:** Paper feed and print head positioning. - **Automotive:** Instrument cluster gauges, headlight leveling, electronic throttle control. - **Medical Equipment:** Infusion pumps, diagnostic scanners, surgical robots. - **Textile Machinery:** Yarn feeders, embroidery machines, looms. - **Camera Autofocus & Zoom:** Lens positioning systems. - **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Pick-and-place machines, conveyor sortation. The ability to **directly interface with digital logic** (microcontrollers, FPGAs) without analog signal conditioning is what makes stepper motors irreplaceable in the digital age of motion control. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Step Angle Formula **Given:** A stepper motor completes one full mechanical revolution (360°) in **N** discrete steps. **Find:** The step angle $\beta$ (angular displacement per pulse). **Derivation:** Step 1: Total angular displacement for one full revolution = $360°$. Step 2: This total displacement is divided into **N** equal discrete steps. Step 3: Therefore, the angular displacement per single step (step angle) is: $$ \beta = \dfrac{\text{Total angular displacement}}{\text{Total number of steps per revolution}} $$ Step 4: Substituting the values: $$ \beta = \dfrac{360°}{N} $$ Step 5: This is the **master relationship** for all stepper motor calculations. Every other formula in this module is derived from this single equation. **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Angle of a Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor **Given:** A single-stack VR stepper motor has $N_s$ stator teeth (poles) and $N_r$ rotor teeth. **Find:** The step angle $\beta_{VR}$. **Derivation:** Step 1: In a VR stepper, when the stator winding is energized, the rotor aligns such that its teeth align with the energized stator teeth to minimize reluctance. Each successive pulse energizes a different stator phase, and the rotor moves by the angular distance between adjacent stator-rotor tooth alignment positions. Step 2: The full 360° rotation is achieved when **every** rotor tooth has aligned with **every** stator tooth. This means the number of stable detent positions (steps) per revolution equals the product of stator and rotor teeth. Step 3: Number of steps per revolution: $$ N = N_s \times N_r $$ Step 4: Substituting into the master formula $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$: $$ \beta_{VR} = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\beta_{VR} = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Step Angle of a Multi-Stack (M-Stack) Variable Reluctance Stepper **Given:** A VR stepper has **m** identical stacks (magnetic sections) on a common shaft, with $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth per stack. The rotor teeth of each stack are mechanically **displaced by $\dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$** relative to the adjacent stack. **Find:** The step angle $\beta_{multi}$. **Derivation:** Step 1: For a single stack, $N_{single} = N_s \times N_r$ steps per revolution. Step 2: With **m** stacks, each energized in a sequential phase-shifted manner, the **effective** number of steps per revolution is multiplied by m because the angular resolution improves by a factor of m. Step 3: Effective steps per revolution: $$ N_{multi} = m \times N_s \times N_r $$ Step 4: Substituting into the master formula: $$ \beta_{multi} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_s \cdot N_r} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\beta_{multi} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_s \cdot N_r}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shaft Speed from Pulse Frequency **Given:** A stepper motor has step angle $\beta$ and receives pulses at a frequency $f$ (in Hz = pulses per second). **Find:** The shaft rotational speed $n$ in revolutions per second (rev/s). **Derivation:** Step 1: In one second, the motor receives $f$ pulses. Step 2: Each pulse advances the rotor by $\beta$ degrees. Step 3: Total angular displacement in one second: $$ \theta_{1s} = f \times \beta \quad \text{[degrees/second]} $$ Step 4: One full revolution = $360°$. Therefore, the number of revolutions per second is: $$ n = \dfrac{\theta_{1s}}{360°} = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{n = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°} \quad \text{[rev/s]}} $$ Step 5: To convert to RPM (revolutions per minute): $$ n_{RPM} = n \times 60 = \dfrac{60 \cdot f \cdot \beta}{360°} = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{6°} $$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Resolution Equivalence **Given:** A stepper motor with step angle $\beta$. **Find:** The resolution $R$ in terms of steps per revolution. **Derivation:** Step 1: By definition, resolution is the **smallest angular increment** the motor can achieve, which is exactly one step. Step 2: The number of such steps in 360° is $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$. Step 3: Resolution is therefore **numerically equal** to steps per revolution: $$ R = N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{R = N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta} \quad \text{[steps/rev]}} $$ --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and the stator is excited in 2-phase bipolar mode. Calculate: - (a) The step angle - (b) The number of steps required to rotate the shaft by 90° - (c) The pulse frequency required to achieve a shaft speed of 600 RPM **Solution:** **Part (a):** Step Angle Step 1: For a hybrid stepper, the step angle formula is: $$ \beta = \dfrac{180°}{N_s \cdot p} $$ Step 2: With 2-phase excitation on a hybrid motor, the effective pole pairs $p = 2$ and $N_s = N_r = 50$ (given rotor teeth). Step 3: Substituting: $$ \beta = \dfrac{180°}{50 \times 2} = \dfrac{180°}{100} = 1.8° $$ Step 4: **Verification** using $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$: $$ N = \dfrac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev} $$ This matches the industry-standard 1.8° hybrid stepper (commonly used in 3D printers). ✓ **Part (b):** Steps for 90° rotation Step 1: Number of pulses = Total angle / Step angle: $$ N_{pulses} = \dfrac{\theta_{total}}{\beta} = \dfrac{90°}{1.8°} = 50 \text{ pulses} $$ **Part (c):** Pulse frequency for 600 RPM Step 1: Convert 600 RPM to rev/s: $$ n = \dfrac{600}{60} = 10 \text{ rev/s} $$ Step 2: Using $n = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°}$, solve for $f$: $$ f = \dfrac{n \times 360°}{\beta} = \dfrac{10 \times 360°}{1.8°} = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz} $$ **Final Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Step Angle $\beta$ | **1.8°** | | Steps for 90° | **50 pulses** | | Pulse Frequency for 600 RPM | **2000 Hz (2 kHz)** | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stepper Motor Drive System > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a complete stepper motor drive system, mapping the signal flow from a digital controller to mechanical output. This diagram is the standard KTU 2024 expected block diagram for the module. ```mermaid flowchart TD ctrlA[Digital Controller

No Permanent Magnet

Permanent Magnet Cylinder

with Toothed Cups

Aluminum or Steel Frame] --> bearingA[Front and Rear Bearings
