Soft Iron or PM Core] rotorA --> teethR[Rotor Teeth
Precision Machined] housingA --> statorA[Stator Assembly
Laminated Silicon Steel] statorA --> teethS[Stator Teeth or Poles
Concentrated Windings] teethS --> windingA[Phase Windings
Copper Magnet Wire] windingA --> termA[Terminal Box
Phase Lead Connections] housingA --> encA[Optional Encoder Mounting
For Closed Loop Feedback] ``` ## 4.4 Construction Details of the Three Major Types ### 4.4.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor - **Stator:** Salient poles (typically 4 or 6 poles) with concentrated field windings. Each pole has a small number of large teeth. - **Rotor:** Made of **high-permeability soft iron** (suitably laminated to reduce eddy current losses). Has salient teeth but **no permanent magnet**. - **Working Principle:** When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance). Sequential energization of phases produces stepwise rotation. - **Step Angle:** $\beta_{VR} = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ (typically larger, e.g., 15°, 7.5°) - **Disadvantage:** No detent torque when de-energized (rotor can rotate freely). ### 4.4.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor - **Stator:** Laminated pole pieces with concentrated windings (similar to VR stator). - **Rotor:** A **cylindrical permanent magnet** (axially magnetized) with alternating N-S poles on its surface, or a smooth magnet that interacts with toothed stator poles. - **Working Principle:** The energized stator poles attract or repel the permanent magnet poles, producing rotation. - **Step Angle:** Larger, typically **7.5° to 15°** (48 to 24 steps/rev). - **Advantage:** Has **detent torque** (residual holding torque even when de-energized). ### 4.4.3 Hybrid Stepper Motor (HB - Most Common in Industry) - **Stator:** Fine-tooth laminated stacks (50 to 200 teeth) with two-phase concentrated windings. - **Rotor:** Two **axially-magnetized permanent magnet caps** on the shaft, each with a toothed soft-iron cup. The teeth on the two caps are **mechanically offset by half a tooth pitch**. - **Working Principle:** Combines the **variable reluctance principle** (fine tooth alignment) with the **permanent magnet principle** (axial flux). - **Step Angle:** Very small, typically **0.9° to 5°** (200 to 72 steps/rev). Most common is **1.8° (200 steps/rev)**. - **Advantage:** Highest torque density, smallest step angle, has detent torque, highest precision. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **"Define a stepper motor. List any four salient properties of a stepper motor that make it suitable for open-loop digital control applications."** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** A **stepper motor** is a special-purpose electromagnetic actuator that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular mechanical steps. The rotor advances by a fixed angular increment (the step angle β) for each pulse received, and its speed is proportional to the pulse frequency. **Four salient properties:** 1. **Open-loop compatibility:** Operates accurately without position feedback sensors, simplifying the system. 2. **Digital pulse-to-angle conversion:** Each pulse = one discrete step, providing exact position control. 3. **High holding torque at standstill:** Can hold the rotor against external loads when energized. 4. **Incremental positional accuracy:** Position errors are non-cumulative within rated torque, and position is directly determined by pulse count. 5. **Excellent low-speed torque:** Delivers maximum torque at zero or low speed, ideal for start-stop positioning. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for properties with brief explanation)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Distinguish between a stepper motor and a conventional DC servo motor based on construction, control method, and feedback requirement."** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | **Construction** | Brushless, toothed rotor and stator with concentrated windings | Brushed or brushless DC motor with continuous rotor windings | | **Control Method** | Digital pulse-based (open loop); step angle is fixed by construction | Analog voltage-based; speed proportional to armature voltage | | **Feedback Required** | No feedback (open loop) | Mandatory position/speed feedback (encoder/tachogenerator) for closed-loop control | | **Position Accuracy** | Depends on step angle (e.g., 1.8°); quantized | Continuous, depends on feedback resolution | | **Cost & Complexity** | Lower cost, simpler drive electronics | Higher cost, complex drive and feedback circuitry | *(3 marks: 1 for each distinguishing parameter with clear comparison)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) - Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. Derive the expression for its step angle. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-phase, 8/6 configuration VR stepper motor is supplied with a pulse train of frequency 400 Hz. Calculate the **step angle**, the **shaft speed in RPM**, and the **number of pulses required to rotate the shaft through 75.6°**. **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) Solution: Construction and Working of VR Stepper **Construction (4 marks):** - **Stator:** A multi-toothed soft-iron laminated core with 4 or 6 salient poles. Each pole carries a concentrated field winding. The 4 stator poles are energized by two phases (Phase A: A1-A2, Phase B: B1-B2). - **Rotor:** A salient-pole (toothed) cylinder of soft iron (no permanent magnet) mounted on a shaft running in bearings. The rotor has $N_r$ uniformly distributed teeth. - **Housing:** Aluminum or steel enclosure supporting the bearings, end covers, and a terminal block for winding connections. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - When Phase A is energized, the rotor teeth align with the energized stator poles to minimize magnetic reluctance. This is the first stable position. - When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by **one step angle** to the new minimum reluctance position relative to the Phase B poles. - Sequential energization of phases (A → B → C → D → A) produces continuous stepwise rotation in one direction. Reversing the sequence reverses rotation. **Step Angle Derivation (1 mark):** The total number of stable detent positions (steps) per revolution equals the product of stator teeth and rotor teeth: $$ \beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} $$ --- ### Part (b) Solution: Numerical Computation **Given Data:** - Configuration: 4-phase, 8/6 VR stepper - This means: $N_s = 8$ (stator teeth), $N_r = 6$ (rotor teeth) - Pulse frequency: $f = 400$ Hz - Required angle: $\theta = 75.6°$ **Step 1: Calculate Step Angle (2 marks)** $$ \beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \dfrac{360°}{8 \times 6} = \dfrac{360°}{48} = 7.5° $$ **Step 2: Calculate Shaft Speed in RPM (3 marks)** Using $n = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°}$: $$ n = \dfrac{400 \times 7.5°}{360°} = \dfrac{3000}{360} = 8.333 \text{ rev/s} $$ Converting to RPM: $$ n_{RPM} = 8.333 \times 60 = 500 \text{ RPM} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Number of Pulses for 75.6° (2 marks)** $$ N_{pulses} = \dfrac{\theta_{total}}{\beta} = \dfrac{75.6°}{7.5°} = 10.08 $$ Since pulses must be an integer, the motor requires **11 pulses** to rotate by at least 75.6° (10 pulses would give only 75°). The shaft will be slightly overshoot to 82.5° with 11 pulses. **Final Answers:** - Step Angle: **β = 7.5°** - Shaft Speed: **n = 500 RPM** - Pulses for ≥75.6°: **11 pulses** (actual angle: 82.5°) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a Hybrid Stepper Motor** with a neat diagram. Why is it the most commonly used stepper motor in modern industrial applications? **(b) [7 Marks]** A hybrid stepper motor has **200 rotor teeth** and is excited in **2-phase bipolar mode**. The motor receives pulses at a frequency of **1500 Hz**. Calculate: **(i)** Step angle, **(ii)** Number of steps per revolution, **(iii)** Shaft speed in RPM, **(iv)** Time required to complete one revolution. **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) Solution: Hybrid Stepper Motor Construction & Working **Construction (4 marks):** - **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core with **fine teeth** (typically 50 to 100) wound with two-phase concentrated coils. The teeth are precisely machined for accurate alignment. - **Rotor:** Consists of **two axially-magnetized permanent magnet caps** mounted on the shaft, each carrying a **soft-iron toothed cup**. The teeth on the two cups are **mechanically displaced by half a tooth pitch** (e.g., 90° in electrical terms). This offset is the key to the hybrid motor's operation. - **Bearings, Shaft, and Housing:** Standard construction, with the rotor suspended in the stator bore via precision bearings. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - The axial magnetic flux from the permanent magnets is channeled through the stator teeth via the rotor toothed cups. - When Phase A is energized, the flux path shifts, and the rotor rotates by a small step angle to a new stable detent position. - The half-tooth-pitch offset between the two rotor cups effectively **doubles the number of steps per revolution**, giving the hybrid motor its very fine step angle. - The hybrid motor combines the **high detent torque of PM motors** with the **fine step resolution of VR motors**. **Why Most Commonly Used (1 mark):** - **Smallest step angle (0.9° to 5°)** → highest positioning precision. - **Highest torque-to-volume ratio** among all stepper types. - **Has detent torque** → safe power-off holding. - **Smooth, low-resonance operation** suitable for microstepping. --- ### Part (b) Solution: Numerical Computation **Given Data:** - Rotor teeth: $N_r = 200$ - Excitation: 2-phase bipolar - Pulse frequency: $f = 1500$ Hz **Step 1: Step Angle (2 marks)** For a hybrid stepper with 2-phase excitation: $$ \beta = \dfrac{360°}{2 \times N_r} = \dfrac{360°}{2 \times 200} = \dfrac{360°}{400} = 0.9° $$ **Step 2: Steps per Revolution (1 mark)** $$ N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta} = \dfrac{360°}{0.9°} = 400 \text{ steps/rev} $$ **Step 3: Shaft Speed in RPM (2 marks)** Using $n = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°}$: $$ n = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.9°}{360°} = \dfrac{1350}{360} = 3.75 \text{ rev/s} $$ Converting to RPM: $$ n_{RPM} = 3.75 \times 60 = 225 \text{ RPM} $$ **Step 4: Time for One Revolution (2 marks)** Time per step: $$ t_{step} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{1500} = 0.667 \text{ ms} $$ Time per revolution: $$ t_{rev} = N \times t_{step} = 400 \times 0.667 \text{ ms} = 266.67 \text{ ms} = 0.267 \text{ s} $$ **Verification:** $$ t_{rev} = \dfrac{60}{n_{RPM}} = \dfrac{60}{225} = 0.267 \text{ s} \quad ✓ $$ **Final Answers:** - Step Angle: **β = 0.9°** - Steps per Revolution: **N = 400** - Shaft Speed: **225 RPM** - Time per Revolution: **0.267 s (267 ms)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch in Step Angle:** Always specify the step angle in **degrees (°)** and explicitly state the unit. Writing "0.9" without the degree symbol is considered incomplete by KTU examiners and may cost 0.5 to 1 mark. > > 2. **Confusing Stator and Rotor Teeth:** In VR stepper problems, students often write $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$ or $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$ instead of $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$. This is the **single most common error** and can cost up to 2 marks. > > 3. **Rounding Errors in Pulse Counts:** When calculating the number of pulses for a given angle, always round **up** to the next integer (the motor cannot execute fractional pulses). Stating "10.08 pulses" instead of "11 pulses" is a typical pitfall. > > 4. **Forgetting to Convert Hz to rev/s or RPM:** Pulse frequency in Hz must be converted using the step angle formula. Writing the shaft speed directly in "Hz" is a **unit error** that loses marks. > > 5. **Missing "Two-Phase" or "Multi-Stack" Factors:** In hybrid and multi-stack VR problems, students forget the factor of 2 in the denominator. Always verify which motor type is specified in the question. > > 6. **Diagrams Without Labels:** A block diagram or construction diagram **without proper labels** (stator, rotor, phase windings, shaft) is considered incomplete. KTU examiners expect at least 5 to 6 clear labels in the construction diagram. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** ### Core Concepts - ☐ A **stepper motor** converts digital pulses into discrete mechanical steps. - ☐ The **step angle (β)** is the angular displacement per pulse. - ☐ **Master formula:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$, where $N$ = steps per revolution. - ☐ **Resolution** of a stepper motor is numerically equal to $N$ (steps/rev). ### Three Construction Types - ☐ **Variable Reluctance (VR):** Soft-iron rotor, no permanent magnet, step angle $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$. - ☐ **Permanent Magnet (PM):** Permanent magnet rotor, has detent torque, larger step angle. - ☐ **Hybrid (HB):** PM + fine-toothed rotor, smallest step angle, most widely used, step angle $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{2 \cdot N_r}$ for 2-phase excitation. ### Key Properties to Remember - ☐ **Open-loop operation** (no feedback required). - ☐ **Digital-to-mechanical conversion** (1 pulse = 1 step). - ☐ **High holding torque** at standstill. - ☐ **Position = Pulse count × Step angle.** - ☐ **Speed = Pulse frequency × Step angle / 360°** (in rev/s). ### Numerical Problem Pattern - ☐ **Step 1:** Identify the motor type and extract given data ($N_s$, $N_r$, $f$, $\theta$). - ☐ **Step 2:** Calculate step angle using the appropriate formula. - ☐ **Step 3:** Calculate shaft speed (rev/s or RPM) from frequency and step angle. - ☐ **Step 4:** Calculate pulse count for desired angle. - ☐ **Always include units** in final answers (°, Hz, RPM, ms). ### Most-Asked KTU Question Patterns - ☐ "Derive the step angle formula for a VR stepper motor." (7 marks) - ☐ "Explain the construction of a hybrid stepper motor with a diagram." (7 marks) - ☐ "A stepper motor with X stator teeth and Y rotor teeth is driven at Z Hz. Find step angle, RPM, and pulses for θ degrees." (7 marks) - ☐ "Compare VR, PM, and Hybrid stepper motors." (3 to 7 marks) - ☐ "List the properties of stepper motors." (3 marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Golden Rule:** The stepper motor module is a **high-scoring** module in PCBRT502 because the formulas are fixed and the numerical problems follow predictable patterns. Master the **three construction types**, the **master formula $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$**, and the **derivation chain** — and you can secure full marks in the entire module with focused preparation.
